questions.question: Which best describes "Packet switching"?
options: Packet switching is the router's core job — the step-by-step process of moving an IP packet from an ingress interface to an egress interface. It happens hundreds of thousands of times per second on modern routers.

Administrative Distance (AD) is a numeric value from 0-255 that ranks how trustworthy a routing source is. When multiple routing sources advertise the same prefix with the same prefix length, AD is the tiebreaker — the lower AD wins and that route is installed in the routing table.

Cisco Express Forwarding (CEF) is a hardware-accelerated forwarding mechanism used on all modern Cisco routers and L3 switches. It's the 'how' behind fast packet switching — without CEF, every packet would hit the CPU, limiting throughput to tens of thousands of packets per second instead of…

The routing table (also called RIB — Routing Information Base) is the router's database of all known destination networks and how to reach them. Every forwarding decision starts with a routing table lookup.
questions.explanation: 1.1.a.1 · weight high. Packet switching: Packet switching is the router's core job — the step-by-step process of moving an IP packet from an ingress interface to an egress interface. It happens hundreds of thousands of times per second on modern routers.

questions.question: Which best describes "Routing table"?
options: Administrative Distance (AD) is a numeric value from 0-255 that ranks how trustworthy a routing source is. When multiple routing sources advertise the same prefix with the same prefix length, AD is the tiebreaker — the lower AD wins and that route is installed in the routing table.

The routing table (also called RIB — Routing Information Base) is the router's database of all known destination networks and how to reach them. Every forwarding decision starts with a routing table lookup.

A connected route is automatically installed in the routing table whenever the router has a configured IP address on an interface AND that interface is in up/up state. It's marked with C in show ip route output. You don't need a routing protocol or a static route command — the router infers it…

A static route is manually configured by an administrator to tell the router exactly how to reach a specific network. It's the simplest form of 'telling the router where to send traffic' — no routing protocol overhead, no neighbor relationships, just a direct command.
questions.explanation: 1.1.a.2 · weight high. Routing table: The routing table (also called RIB — Routing Information Base) is the router's database of all known destination networks and how to reach them. Every forwarding decision starts with a routing table lookup.

questions.question: Which best describes "Longest prefix match"?
options: A static route is manually configured by an administrator to tell the router exactly how to reach a specific network. It's the simplest form of 'telling the router where to send traffic' — no routing protocol overhead, no neighbor relationships, just a direct command.

Cisco Express Forwarding (CEF) is a hardware-accelerated forwarding mechanism used on all modern Cisco routers and L3 switches. It's the 'how' behind fast packet switching — without CEF, every packet would hit the CPU, limiting throughput to tens of thousands of packets per second instead of…

Longest prefix match (LPM) is the single most important rule in IP routing. When multiple routes in the routing table match a packet's destination, the router picks the route with the longest subnet mask (the most specific match). This rule runs BEFORE administrative distance and metric are ever…

A dynamic route is one learned automatically by a routing protocol — OSPF, EIGRP, RIP, BGP. Instead of an admin typing each route, routers exchange route information with each other and update their tables as the topology changes.

This rule runs BEFORE administrative distance and metric are ever considered.

questions.question: Which best describes "MAC address table (CAM table)"?
options: Global command required to enable L3 routing on a multilayer switch. Without it, L3 switch acts only as L2.
Switch learns source MAC → ingress port mappings. Ages out after 300s default on Cisco. View with 'show mac address-table'.
Virtual L3 interface tied to a VLAN on a multilayer switch. 'interface vlan 10' + IP = default gateway for VLAN 10.
L2 at access layer (endpoint connection). L3 at distribution layer (inter-VLAN routing, policy).
questions.explanation: 1.1.b.1 · weight high. MAC address table (CAM table): Switch learns source MAC → ingress port mappings. Ages out after 300s default on Cisco. View with 'show mac address-table'.

questions.question: Which best describes "Unknown unicast flooding"?
options: Virtual L3 interface tied to a VLAN on a multilayer switch. 'interface vlan 10' + IP = default gateway for VLAN 10.
L2 at access layer (endpoint connection). L3 at distribution layer (inter-VLAN routing, policy).
If destination MAC is not in the table, switch floods the frame out all ports in the VLAN except the source.
Physical port configured with 'no switchport' — behaves like a router interface with its own IP.
questions.explanation: 1.1.b.2 · weight high. Unknown unicast flooding: If destination MAC is not in the table, switch floods the frame out all ports in the VLAN except the source.

questions.question: Which best describes "Collision domain vs broadcast domain"?
options: Switch learns source MAC → ingress port mappings. Ages out after 300s default on Cisco. View with 'show mac address-table'.
L3 switches route in hardware (ASICs) at wire speed, faster than CPU-based software routing on most routers.
Physical port configured with 'no switchport' — behaves like a router interface with its own IP.
Each switch port = separate collision domain. All ports in same VLAN = one broadcast domain. VLANs split broadcast domains.
questions.explanation: 1.1.b.3 · weight high. Collision domain vs broadcast domain: Each switch port = separate collision domain. All ports in same VLAN = one broadcast domain. VLANs split broadcast domains.

questions.question: Which best describes "Stateless firewall"?
options: Inline device in traffic path. Can DROP malicious packets in real time. Adds latency but actively protects.
Filters packets individually using ACLs — source/dest IP, ports, protocol. No connection awareness.
Passive monitor on a SPAN/mirror port. Sees COPIES of traffic. Alerts on threats but CANNOT block.
Inspects L7 payload, identifies specific applications regardless of port (e.g., distinguishes Zoom vs BitTorrent on port 443).
questions.explanation: 1.1.c.1 · weight high. Stateless firewall: Filters packets individually using ACLs — source/dest IP, ports, protocol. No connection awareness.

questions.question: Which best describes "Stateful firewall"?
options: Inline device in traffic path. Can DROP malicious packets in real time. Adds latency but actively protects.
Passive monitor on a SPAN/mirror port. Sees COPIES of traffic. Alerts on threats but CANNOT block.
Current Cisco NGFW platform (formerly Firepower Threat Defense). Managed by FMC or Cisco Defense Orchestrator.
Tracks active connection state (TCP handshake, UDP pseudo-sessions). Auto-allows return traffic. Classic example: Cisco ASA.
questions.explanation: 1.1.c.2 · weight high. Stateful firewall: Tracks active connection state (TCP handshake, UDP pseudo-sessions). Auto-allows return traffic. Classic example: Cisco ASA.

questions.question: Which best describes "Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)"?
options: Inspects L7 payload, identifies specific applications regardless of port (e.g., distinguishes Zoom vs BitTorrent on port 443).
Filters packets individually using ACLs — source/dest IP, ports, protocol. No connection awareness.
Adds L7 inspection, application awareness, integrated IPS, URL filtering, AMP, and user identity to stateful firewall capabilities.
Tracks active connection state (TCP handshake, UDP pseudo-sessions). Auto-allows return traffic. Classic example: Cisco ASA.
questions.explanation: 1.1.c.3 · weight high. Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW): Adds L7 inspection, application awareness, integrated IPS, URL filtering, AMP, and user identity to stateful firewall capabilities.

questions.question: Which best describes "Deep packet inspection (DPI)"?
options: Matches known attack patterns from an updated database. Reliable for known threats, misses zero-days.
Inspects L7 payload, identifies specific applications regardless of port (e.g., distinguishes Zoom vs BitTorrent on port 443).
Filters packets individually using ACLs — source/dest IP, ports, protocol. No connection awareness.
Flags traffic deviating from a learned baseline. Can catch novel attacks but produces more false positives.
questions.explanation: 1.1.c.4 · weight high. Deep packet inspection (DPI): Inspects L7 payload, identifies specific applications regardless of port (e.g., distinguishes Zoom vs BitTorrent on port 443).

questions.question: Which best describes "Autonomous AP"?
options: Extended Service Set = multiple APs sharing the same SSID to provide seamless roaming across a larger area.
Basic Service Set = one AP + its clients. BSSID = the AP radio's MAC address.
AP switches data LOCALLY at the branch. Survives WLC failure. Used for remote/branch offices.
Standalone AP. Configured individually via CLI/web GUI. All wireless functions local. Fine for 1-3 APs, unmanageable at scale.

questions.question: Which best describes "CAPWAP"?

questions.question: Which best describes "Client-server model"?

questions.question: Which best describes "Two-tier (collapsed core)"?
options: Single building or small multi-building site, <200 users, limited growth. Start here, migrate to three-tier on scale.
Single layer performing inter-VLAN routing, ACLs, QoS, HSRP/VRRP, and high-speed backbone forwarding. Typically L3 (Cat 9300/9500).
Access + combined core/distribution layer. Used in small/medium campuses (~<200 users, single building). Cheaper, simpler, less scalable.
First Hop Redundancy Protocols. Allow two switches to share a virtual IP as default gateway. Deployed at collapsed core or distribution.

First Hop Redundancy Protocols. Allow two switches to share a virtual IP as default gateway. Deployed at collapsed core or distribution.

questions.question: Which best describes "Access layer (three-tier)"?
options: Access → Distribution → Core. Top-down or bottom-up, know each layer's function and what it must NOT do.
L2 edge switches connecting endpoints. Port-level VLAN assignment, PoE, 802.1X, port security, DHCP snooping, DAI, QoS trust boundary.
High-speed backbone. Pure fast forwarding — NEVER do ACLs, QoS remarking, or policy here. 10G/40G/100G uplinks.
Distribution advertises a single summary route to the core instead of many specific routes. Reduces core routing table size.
questions.explanation: 1.2.b.1 · weight high. Access layer (three-tier): L2 edge switches connecting endpoints. Port-level VLAN assignment, PoE, 802.1X, port security, DHCP snooping, DAI, QoS trust boundary.

questions.question: Which best describes "Distribution layer"?
options: L2 edge switches connecting endpoints. Port-level VLAN assignment, PoE, 802.1X, port security, DHCP snooping, DAI, QoS trust boundary.
L3 aggregation + policy enforcement. Inter-VLAN routing (SVIs), ACLs, QoS enforcement, route summarization, HSRP/VRRP, STP root.
Scales by adding distribution blocks without redesigning the core. Right for multi-building campuses with thousands of users.
High-speed backbone. Pure fast forwarding — NEVER do ACLs, QoS remarking, or policy here. 10G/40G/100G uplinks.

questions.question: Which best describes "Core layer"?
options: L3 aggregation + policy enforcement. Inter-VLAN routing (SVIs), ACLs, QoS enforcement, route summarization, HSRP/VRRP, STP root.

L2 edge switches connecting endpoints. Port-level VLAN assignment, PoE, 802.1X, port security, DHCP snooping, DAI, QoS trust boundary.

questions.question: Which best describes "Dual-homed access"?

questions.question: Which best describes "Spine-leaf fabric"?
options: Client-to-server (in/out of the DC). Three-tier is built for this.
Virtual Extensible LAN. Extends L2 segments across L3 spine-leaf fabric. Paired with EVPN (BGP-based control plane).
Top of Rack. Access layer for servers and storage. Do not cross-connect to other leaves.
Two-tier DC design. Every leaf connects to every spine; leaves never connect to leaves, spines never to spines.

questions.question: Which best describes "Leaf switch (ToR)"?
options: Any two servers on different leaves are always exactly 2 hops apart (leaf → spine → leaf).
Virtual Extensible LAN. Extends L2 segments across L3 spine-leaf fabric. Paired with EVPN (BGP-based control plane).
Client-to-server (in/out of the DC). Three-tier is built for this.
Top of Rack. Access layer for servers and storage. Do not cross-connect to other leaves.

questions.question: Which best describes "Spine switch"?
options: Client-to-server (in/out of the DC). Three-tier is built for this.
L3 routing load-balances across all leaf-spine paths simultaneously. Replaces STP's link-blocking.
Any two servers on different leaves are always exactly 2 hops apart (leaf → spine → leaf).
Backbone. Only connects to leaves — never to servers or other spines. Provides uniform forwarding capacity.
questions.explanation: 1.2.c.3 · weight high. Spine switch: Backbone. Only connects to leaves — never to servers or other spines. Provides uniform forwarding capacity.

questions.question: Which best describes "East-west traffic"?
options: Server-to-server within the DC. Dominates virtualized and distributed workloads. Spine-leaf optimizes for this.
Client-to-server (in/out of the DC). Three-tier is built for this.
Two-tier DC design. Every leaf connects to every spine; leaves never connect to leaves, spines never to spines.

Two-tier DC design. Every leaf connects to every spine; leaves never connect to leaves, spines never to spines.
Client-to-server (in/out of the DC). Three-tier is built for this.

questions.question: Which best describes "WAN"?
options: Wide Area Network. Connects geographically separated sites over provider infrastructure. Slower, higher-latency, more expensive than LAN.
Multiprotocol Label Switching. Provider-managed private WAN with guaranteed SLAs. L3 VPN (provider routes) or L2 VPN. Expensive, reliable.
Ethernet-based WAN within a metro area. Types: E-Line (P2P), E-LAN (any-to-any), E-Tree (hub-and-spoke).
Customer Premises Equipment. The router at the customer site, typically the demarc boundary for managed services.
questions.explanation: 1.2.d.1 · weight high. WAN: Wide Area Network. Connects geographically separated sites over provider infrastructure. Slower, higher-latency, more expensive than LAN.

questions.question: Which best describes "MPLS"?
options: MPLS = guaranteed SLA, expensive, provider-routed. SD-WAN = app-aware, cheaper, uses multiple transports, centrally managed. Strategic direction: SD-WAN.
Multiprotocol Label Switching. Provider-managed private WAN with guaranteed SLAs. L3 VPN (provider routes) or L2 VPN. Expensive, reliable.
Boundary between provider-managed and customer-managed infrastructure. Provider owns up to the demarc.
Consumer/business internet access. Cheap, no SLA guarantees. Common as SD-WAN underlay or backup.
questions.explanation: 1.2.d.2 · weight high. MPLS: Multiprotocol Label Switching. Provider-managed private WAN with guaranteed SLAs. L3 VPN (provider routes) or L2 VPN. Expensive, reliable.

questions.question: Which best describes "SD-WAN"?
options: Consumer/business internet access. Cheap, no SLA guarantees. Common as SD-WAN underlay or backup.
Software-Defined WAN. Encrypted overlay across multiple transports (MPLS, broadband, LTE). Central controller, app-aware path selection.
Multiprotocol Label Switching. Provider-managed private WAN with guaranteed SLAs. L3 VPN (provider routes) or L2 VPN. Expensive, reliable.
MPLS = guaranteed SLA, expensive, provider-routed. SD-WAN = app-aware, cheaper, uses multiple transports, centrally managed. Strategic direction: SD-WAN.
questions.explanation: 1.2.d.6 · weight high. SD-WAN: Software-Defined WAN. Encrypted overlay across multiple transports (MPLS, broadband, LTE). Central controller, app-aware path selection.

questions.question: Which best describes "MPLS vs SD-WAN"?
options: Ethernet-based WAN within a metro area. Types: E-Line (P2P), E-LAN (any-to-any), E-Tree (hub-and-spoke).
Consumer/business internet access. Cheap, no SLA guarantees. Common as SD-WAN underlay or backup.
MPLS = guaranteed SLA, expensive, provider-routed. SD-WAN = app-aware, cheaper, uses multiple transports, centrally managed. Strategic direction: SD-WAN.
Dedicated P2P circuit with guaranteed bandwidth. T1=1.544 Mbps, T3=44.736 Mbps. Legacy, being replaced by Metro Ethernet/MPLS.
questions.explanation: 1.2.d.9 · weight high. MPLS vs SD-WAN: MPLS = guaranteed SLA, expensive, provider-routed. SD-WAN = app-aware, cheaper, uses multiple transports, centrally managed. Strategic direction: SD-WAN.

questions.question: Which best describes "SOHO network"?
options: Small Office/Home Office. 1–10 users. Simple flat topology with a single all-in-one device.
SOHO: one device, no redundancy, no advanced features. Enterprise: tiered, redundant, centrally managed.
Combines router, switch (4-8 ports), AP, firewall, DHCP server, and NAT gateway in one box.
questions.explanation: 1.2.e.1 · weight high. SOHO network: Small Office/Home Office. 1–10 users. Simple flat topology with a single all-in-one device.

questions.question: Which best describes "All-in-one device"?
options: SOHO: one device, no redundancy, no advanced features. Enterprise: tiered, redundant, centrally managed.
Single subnet and single broadcast domain. No VLANs, no inter-VLAN routing, no core/distribution layers.
Combines router, switch (4-8 ports), AP, firewall, DHCP server, and NAT gateway in one box.
Small Office/Home Office. 1–10 users. Simple flat topology with a single all-in-one device.
questions.explanation: 1.2.e.2 · weight high. All-in-one device: Combines router, switch (4-8 ports), AP, firewall, DHCP server, and NAT gateway in one box.

questions.question: Which best describes "PAT (Port Address Translation)"?
options: Small Office/Home Office. 1–10 users. Simple flat topology with a single all-in-one device.

Single subnet and single broadcast domain. No VLANs, no inter-VLAN routing, no core/distribution layers.

questions.question: Which best describes "On-premises (on-prem)"?
options: Organization owns, operates, hosts all infrastructure. Full control, full responsibility. CapEx-heavy, slow to scale.
Platform as a Service. Provider manages HW + OS + runtime. You manage app + data. Example: AWS Elastic Beanstalk.
Third-party-hosted infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP). Rented on demand. OpEx model, elastic scaling, shared responsibility.
Software as a Service. Provider manages everything. You just use the app. Example: M365, Salesforce.

questions.question: Which best describes "Cloud"?

Software as a Service. Provider manages everything. You just use the app. Example: M365, Salesforce.

Organization owns, operates, hosts all infrastructure. Full control, full responsibility. CapEx-heavy, slow to scale.
questions.explanation: 1.2.f.2 · weight high. Cloud: Third-party-hosted infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP). Rented on demand. OpEx model, elastic scaling, shared responsibility.

questions.question: Which best describes "CapEx vs OpEx"?

Platform as a Service. Provider manages HW + OS + runtime. You manage app + data. Example: AWS Elastic Beanstalk.

questions.question: Which best describes "IaaS"?

questions.explanation: 6.7.b.1 · weight high. { } = object: Curly braces. Collection of unordered key-value pairs. Access by key.

questions.question: Which best describes "[ ] = array"?
options: Strings + keys MUST use double quotes " ". Single quotes ' ' are INVALID JSON.
{ → object. [ → array. First character of snippet tells you the type.
Curly braces. Collection of unordered key-value pairs. Access by key.
Square brackets. Ordered list. Access by numeric index (0-based).
questions.explanation: 6.7.b.2 · weight high. [ ] = array: Square brackets. Ordered list. Access by numeric index (0-based).

questions.question: Which best describes "Visual identification"?
options: Dot notation + brackets. data.devices[0].name = first device's name field.
{ → object. [ → array. First character of snippet tells you the type.
Square brackets. Ordered list. Access by numeric index (0-based).
Curly braces. Collection of unordered key-value pairs. Access by key.
questions.explanation: 6.7.b.3 · weight high. Visual identification: { → object. [ → array. First character of snippet tells you the type.

options: 'true' / 'false' only. 'True' or 'FALSE' = INVALID JSON. Must be lowercase.
Curly braces. Collection of unordered key-value pairs. Access by key.
JSON has no 'date' type (use string). No 'undefined' (use null). Common exam traps.
string, number, boolean, null, object, array. Memorize all six.
questions.explanation: 6.7.c.1 · weight high. 6 valid JSON types: string, number, boolean, null, object, array. Memorize all six.

options: [ ] brackets. Ordered list of values. Example: ["R1", "R2", "R3"].
JSON has no 'date' type (use string). No 'undefined' (use null). Common exam traps.
string, number, boolean, null, object, array. Memorize all six.
'true' / 'false' only. 'True' or 'FALSE' = INVALID JSON. Must be lowercase.
questions.explanation: 6.7.c.2 · weight high. JSON booleans lowercase: 'true' / 'false' only. 'True' or 'FALSE' = INVALID JSON. Must be lowercase.

questions.question: Which best describes "No date/undefined in JSON"?
options: string, number, boolean, null, object, array. Memorize all six.
'true' / 'false' only. 'True' or 'FALSE' = INVALID JSON. Must be lowercase.
JSON has no 'date' type (use string). No 'undefined' (use null). Common exam traps.

Dot notation + brackets. data.devices[0].name = first device's name field.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Packet switching is the router's core job — the step-by-step process of moving an IP packet from an ingress interface to an egress interface."
options: Packet switching
Static route
Cisco Express Forwarding (CEF)
Broadcast domain segmentation
questions.explanation: 1.1.a.1 · weight high. Packet switching: Packet switching is the router's core job — the step-by-step process of moving an IP packet from an ingress interface to an egress interface. It happens hundreds of thousands of times per second on modern routers.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "The routing table (also called RIB — Routing Information Base) is the router's database of all known destination networks and how to reach them."
options: Connected route
Routing table
Administrative Distance (AD)
Longest prefix match
questions.explanation: 1.1.a.2 · weight high. Routing table: The routing table (also called RIB — Routing Information Base) is the router's database of all known destination networks and how to reach them. Every forwarding decision starts with a routing table lookup.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Administrative Distance (AD) is a numeric value from 0-255 that ranks how trustworthy a routing source is."
options: Static route
Connected route
Administrative Distance (AD)
Dynamic route
questions.explanation: 1.1.a.3 · weight high. Administrative Distance (AD): Administrative Distance (AD) is a numeric value from 0-255 that ranks how trustworthy a routing source is. When multiple routing sources advertise the same prefix with the same prefix length, AD is the tiebreaker — the lower AD wins and that route is installed in the routing table.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Longest prefix match (LPM) is the single most important rule in IP routing."
options: Connected route
Dynamic route
Broadcast domain segmentation
Longest prefix match
questions.explanation: 1.1.a.4 · weight high. Longest prefix match: Longest prefix match (LPM) is the single most important rule in IP routing. When multiple routes in the routing table match a packet's destination, the router picks the route with the longest subnet mask (the most specific match). This rule runs BEFORE administrative distance and metric are ever considered.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "A connected route is automatically installed in the routing table whenever the router has a configured IP address on an interface AND that interface is in up/up state."
options: Administrative Distance (AD)
Packet switching
Connected route
Cisco Express Forwarding (CEF)
questions.explanation: 1.1.a.5 · weight high. Connected route: A connected route is automatically installed in the routing table whenever the router has a configured IP address on an interface AND that interface is in up/up state. It's marked with C in show ip route output. You don't need a routing protocol or a static route command — the router infers it from the interface configuration.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "A static route is manually configured by an administrator to tell the router exactly how to reach a specific network."
options: Longest prefix match
Connected route
Broadcast domain segmentation
Static route
questions.explanation: 1.1.a.6 · weight high. Static route: A static route is manually configured by an administrator to tell the router exactly how to reach a specific network. It's the simplest form of 'telling the router where to send traffic' — no routing protocol overhead, no neighbor relationships, just a direct command.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "A dynamic route is one learned automatically by a routing protocol — OSPF, EIGRP, RIP, BGP."
options: Longest prefix match
Routing table
Dynamic route
Administrative Distance (AD)
questions.explanation: 1.1.a.7 · weight high. Dynamic route: A dynamic route is one learned automatically by a routing protocol — OSPF, EIGRP, RIP, BGP. Instead of an admin typing each route, routers exchange route information with each other and update their tables as the topology changes.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Routers do not forward broadcast frames between interfaces by default."
options: Broadcast domain segmentation
Dynamic route
Cisco Express Forwarding (CEF)
Administrative Distance (AD)
questions.explanation: 1.1.a.8 · weight high. Broadcast domain segmentation: Routers do not forward broadcast frames between interfaces by default. This single property is what makes routers fundamentally different from switches — it's why we use routers to divide networks into smaller, manageable pieces.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Switch learns source MAC → ingress port mappings."
options: Collision domain vs broadcast domain
Routed port
MAC address table (CAM table)
SVI (Switch Virtual Interface)
questions.explanation: 1.1.b.1 · weight high. MAC address table (CAM table): Switch learns source MAC → ingress port mappings. Ages out after 300s default on Cisco. View with 'show mac address-table'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "If destination MAC is not in the table, switch floods the frame out all ports in the VLAN except the source."
options: Unknown unicast flooding
Collision domain vs broadcast domain
Routed port
MAC address table (CAM table)
questions.explanation: 1.1.b.2 · weight high. Unknown unicast flooding: If destination MAC is not in the table, switch floods the frame out all ports in the VLAN except the source.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each switch port = separate collision domain."
options: Collision domain vs broadcast domain
'ip routing' command
SVI (Switch Virtual Interface)
L2 vs L3 switch placement
questions.explanation: 1.1.b.3 · weight high. Collision domain vs broadcast domain: Each switch port = separate collision domain. All ports in same VLAN = one broadcast domain. VLANs split broadcast domains.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Virtual L3 interface tied to a VLAN on a multilayer switch."
options: SVI (Switch Virtual Interface)
MAC address table (CAM table)
Unknown unicast flooding
Hardware ASIC forwarding
questions.explanation: 1.1.b.4 · weight high. SVI (Switch Virtual Interface): Virtual L3 interface tied to a VLAN on a multilayer switch. 'interface vlan 10' + IP = default gateway for VLAN 10.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Physical port configured with 'no switchport' — behaves like a router interface with its own IP."
options: Hardware ASIC forwarding
Unknown unicast flooding
Routed port
Collision domain vs broadcast domain
questions.explanation: 1.1.b.5 · weight high. Routed port: Physical port configured with 'no switchport' — behaves like a router interface with its own IP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Global command required to enable L3 routing on a multilayer switch."
options: SVI (Switch Virtual Interface)
'ip routing' command
L2 vs L3 switch placement
Routed port
questions.explanation: 1.1.b.6 · weight high. 'ip routing' command: Global command required to enable L3 routing on a multilayer switch. Without it, L3 switch acts only as L2.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Filters packets individually using ACLs — source/dest IP, ports, protocol."
options: Stateful firewall
Stateless firewall
Anomaly-based detection
Signature-based detection
questions.explanation: 1.1.c.1 · weight high. Stateless firewall: Filters packets individually using ACLs — source/dest IP, ports, protocol. No connection awareness.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Tracks active connection state (TCP handshake, UDP pseudo-sessions)."
options: IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)
Stateful firewall
Signature-based detection
Anomaly-based detection
questions.explanation: 1.1.c.2 · weight high. Stateful firewall: Tracks active connection state (TCP handshake, UDP pseudo-sessions). Auto-allows return traffic. Classic example: Cisco ASA.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Adds L7 inspection, application awareness, integrated IPS, URL filtering, AMP, and user identity to stateful firewall capabilities."
options: Cisco Secure Firewall (FTD)
Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)
Stateless firewall
Deep packet inspection (DPI)
questions.explanation: 1.1.c.3 · weight high. Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW): Adds L7 inspection, application awareness, integrated IPS, URL filtering, AMP, and user identity to stateful firewall capabilities.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Inspects L7 payload, identifies specific applications regardless of port (e.g., distinguishes Zoom vs BitTorrent on port 443)."
options: Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)
Stateful firewall
Deep packet inspection (DPI)
IDS (Intrusion Detection System)
questions.explanation: 1.1.c.4 · weight high. Deep packet inspection (DPI): Inspects L7 payload, identifies specific applications regardless of port (e.g., distinguishes Zoom vs BitTorrent on port 443).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Passive monitor on a SPAN/mirror port."
options: IDS (Intrusion Detection System)
Stateless firewall
Deep packet inspection (DPI)
Cisco Secure Firewall (FTD)
questions.explanation: 1.1.c.5 · weight high. IDS (Intrusion Detection System): Passive monitor on a SPAN/mirror port. Sees COPIES of traffic. Alerts on threats but CANNOT block.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Inline device in traffic path."
options: Signature-based detection
IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)
Deep packet inspection (DPI)
IDS (Intrusion Detection System)
questions.explanation: 1.1.c.6 · weight high. IPS (Intrusion Prevention System): Inline device in traffic path. Can DROP malicious packets in real time. Adds latency but actively protects.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Standalone AP. Configured individually via CLI/web GUI."
options: Autonomous AP
SSID
CAPWAP
Lightweight AP
questions.explanation: 1.1.d.1 · weight high. Autonomous AP: Standalone AP. Configured individually via CLI/web GUI. All wireless functions local. Fine for 1-3 APs, unmanageable at scale.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points (RFC 5415)."
options: CAPWAP
Split-MAC architecture
Autonomous AP
SSID
questions.explanation: 1.1.d.3 · weight high. CAPWAP: Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points (RFC 5415). Tunnels AP↔WLC traffic. Control = UDP 5246 (DTLS), Data = UDP 5247 (optional DTLS).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Real-time RF functions (beacons, encryption, ACKs) stay on AP."
options: ESS
Split-MAC architecture
FlexConnect mode
BSS / BSSID
questions.explanation: 1.1.d.4 · weight high. Split-MAC architecture: Real-time RF functions (beacons, encryption, ACKs) stay on AP. Management (auth, SSID/VLAN, RF decisions, firmware) handled by WLC.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "AP switches data LOCALLY at the branch."
options: Monitor/Sniffer/SE-Connect modes
Split-MAC architecture
FlexConnect mode
SSID
questions.explanation: 1.1.d.6 · weight high. FlexConnect mode: AP switches data LOCALLY at the branch. Survives WLC failure. Used for remote/branch offices.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Basic Service Set = one AP + its clients."
options: Autonomous AP
SSID
BSS / BSSID
FlexConnect mode
questions.explanation: 1.1.d.8 · weight high. BSS / BSSID: Basic Service Set = one AP + its clients. BSSID = the AP radio's MAC address.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Extended Service Set = multiple APs sharing the same SSID to provide seamless roaming across a larger area."
options: ESS
Split-MAC architecture
Local mode (AP)
SSID
questions.explanation: 1.1.d.9 · weight high. ESS: Extended Service Set = multiple APs sharing the same SSID to provide seamless roaming across a larger area.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Primary in-band mgmt: AP association, RADIUS auth, admin access."
options: RRM (Radio Resource Management)
WLC dynamic interface
WLC management interface
WLC vs DNA Center scope
questions.explanation: 1.1.e.1 · weight high. WLC management interface: Primary in-band mgmt: AP association, RADIUS auth, admin access. The most critical WLC interface.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Network-wide management platform."
options: WLC management interface
Cisco DNA Center / Catalyst Center
SD-Access (SDA)
WLC dynamic interface
questions.explanation: 1.1.e.7 · weight high. Cisco DNA Center / Catalyst Center: Network-wide management platform. Centerpiece of Intent-Based Networking (IBN). Manages wired + wireless + WAN.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Design (hierarchy, IPAM), Policy (SGTs, microseg), Provision (PnP, config push), Assurance (monitoring, AI anomaly detection)."
options: WLC management interface
WLC service port
DNA Center four pillars
SD-Access (SDA)
questions.explanation: 1.1.e.8 · weight high. DNA Center four pillars: Design (hierarchy, IPAM), Policy (SGTs, microseg), Provision (PnP, config push), Assurance (monitoring, AI anomaly detection).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "WLC = wireless-only controller (APs/WLANs)."
options: WLC vs DNA Center scope
RRM (Radio Resource Management)
WLC dynamic interface
SD-Access (SDA)
questions.explanation: 1.1.e.10 · weight high. WLC vs DNA Center scope: WLC = wireless-only controller (APs/WLANs). DNA Center = network-wide controller that can manage WLCs as part of a larger fabric.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Device at the edge of a network that generates or consumes data (PC, phone, printer, IP camera, IoT sensor)."
options: NIC + MAC address
Port security
Endpoint
NAC (Network Access Control)
questions.explanation: 1.1.f.1 · weight high. Endpoint: Device at the edge of a network that generates or consumes data (PC, phone, printer, IP camera, IoT sensor).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "The router or L3 switch SVI an endpoint sends traffic to for destinations outside its local subnet."
options: Endpoint
Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)
NIC + MAC address
Default gateway
questions.explanation: 1.1.f.3 · weight high. Default gateway: The router or L3 switch SVI an endpoint sends traffic to for destinations outside its local subnet.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Access-layer feature that limits which MAC addresses can connect on a given port."
options: Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)
NAC (Network Access Control)
Port security
Endpoint
questions.explanation: 1.1.f.5 · weight high. Port security: Access-layer feature that limits which MAC addresses can connect on a given port.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Port-based auth that requires credentials before granting network access."
options: NAC (Network Access Control)
Cisco ISE
Endpoint
802.1X authentication
questions.explanation: 1.1.f.6 · weight high. 802.1X authentication: Port-based auth that requires credentials before granting network access. Works with RADIUS/ISE.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Blocks rogue DHCP servers by allowing DHCP offers only from trusted ports."
options: DHCP snooping
NIC + MAC address
NAC (Network Access Control)
Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)
questions.explanation: 1.1.f.7 · weight high. DHCP snooping: Blocks rogue DHCP servers by allowing DHCP offers only from trusted ports.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Prevents ARP spoofing by validating ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table."
options: Access layer
802.1X authentication
Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)
NIC + MAC address
questions.explanation: 1.1.f.8 · weight high. Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI): Prevents ARP spoofing by validating ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Client initiates a request; server responds."
options: DNS
RADIUS
SSH vs Telnet
Client-server model
questions.explanation: 1.1.g.1 · weight high. Client-server model: Client initiates a request; server responds. Server listens on a well-known port for incoming connections.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "FTP = TCP 21 control + TCP 20 data (active)."
options: NTP
FTP / TFTP
Client-server model
DNS
questions.explanation: 1.1.g.5 · weight high. FTP / TFTP: FTP = TCP 21 control + TCP 20 data (active). TFTP = UDP 69, no auth — used for IOS/config transfer.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "SMTP (send) = TCP 25 (or 587)."
options: FTP / TFTP
SSH vs Telnet
SMTP / POP3 / IMAP
HTTP / HTTPS
questions.explanation: 1.1.g.7 · weight high. SMTP / POP3 / IMAP: SMTP (send) = TCP 25 (or 587). POP3 (retrieve) = TCP 110. IMAP (access) = TCP 143.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "UDP 1812 auth, UDP 1813 accounting."
options: Syslog
SSH vs Telnet
RADIUS
SMTP / POP3 / IMAP
questions.explanation: 1.1.g.8 · weight high. RADIUS: UDP 1812 auth, UDP 1813 accounting. Open standard. Encrypts password only. Used with 802.1X.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "TCP 49. Cisco-proprietary AAA."
options: HTTP / HTTPS
Client-server model
DNS
TACACS+
questions.explanation: 1.1.g.9 · weight high. TACACS+: TCP 49. Cisco-proprietary AAA. Separates auth/author/acct. Encrypts ENTIRE packet body.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "UDP 123. Time sync across devices."
options: FTP / TFTP
NTP
HTTP / HTTPS
Syslog
questions.explanation: 1.1.g.11 · weight high. NTP: UDP 123. Time sync across devices. Essential for log correlation and cert validation.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "UDP 161 queries / UDP 162 traps."
options: Client-server model
RADIUS
SNMP
FTP / TFTP
questions.explanation: 1.1.g.12 · weight high. SNMP: UDP 161 queries / UDP 162 traps. v1/v2c = plaintext community strings. v3 = auth + encryption (use v3).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "The device providing power — typically a PoE switch or a midspan injector."
options: PoE detection + classification
PSE (Power Sourcing Equipment)
802.3bt Type 3 (PoE++)
802.3bt Type 4 (PoE++)
questions.explanation: 1.1.h.1 · weight high. PSE (Power Sourcing Equipment): The device providing power — typically a PoE switch or a midspan injector.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "The device drawing power — AP, IP phone, IP camera, etc."
options: PD (Powered Device)
802.3bt Type 4 (PoE++)
PoE power budget
PoE detection + classification
questions.explanation: 1.1.h.2 · weight high. PD (Powered Device): The device drawing power — AP, IP phone, IP camera, etc.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Up to 15.4W at PSE (~12.95W at PD)."
options: Cisco UPoE / UPoE+
PSE (Power Sourcing Equipment)
802.3af (PoE)
802.3bt Type 4 (PoE++)
questions.explanation: 1.1.h.3 · weight high. 802.3af (PoE): Up to 15.4W at PSE (~12.95W at PD). Uses 2 of 4 pairs. Basic IP phones, entry APs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Up to 30W at PSE (~25.5W at PD)."
options: 802.3af (PoE)
PoE detection + classification
802.3bt Type 3 (PoE++)
questions.explanation: 1.1.h.4 · weight high. 802.3at (PoE+): Up to 30W at PSE (~25.5W at PD). 600mA. Modern APs, pan-tilt cameras.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Up to 90W using all 4 pairs at higher current."
options: 802.3bt Type 4 (PoE++)
'show power inline'
Cisco UPoE / UPoE+
questions.explanation: 1.1.h.6 · weight high. 802.3bt Type 4 (PoE++): Up to 90W using all 4 pairs at higher current. Cat6A recommended. PTZ cams with heaters, laptops.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Total wattage a switch can deliver across all PoE ports."
options: 'show power inline'
PoE power budget
Cisco UPoE / UPoE+
802.3af (PoE)
questions.explanation: 1.1.h.9 · weight high. PoE power budget: Total wattage a switch can deliver across all PoE ports. Exceeding it denies power to lower-priority ports.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "View PoE status per port — allocated vs consumed watts, device class, port state."
options: PD (Powered Device)
802.3bt Type 4 (PoE++)
'show power inline'
questions.explanation: 1.1.h.11 · weight high. 'show power inline': View PoE status per port — allocated vs consumed watts, device class, port state.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Access + combined core/distribution layer."
options: Two-tier (collapsed core)
Two-tier use case
HSRP / VRRP default gateway redundancy
Access layer (two-tier)
questions.explanation: 1.2.a.1 · weight high. Two-tier (collapsed core): Access + combined core/distribution layer. Used in small/medium campuses (~<200 users, single building). Cheaper, simpler, less scalable.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Single layer performing inter-VLAN routing, ACLs, QoS, HSRP/VRRP, and high-speed backbone forwarding."
options: HSRP / VRRP default gateway redundancy
Two-tier (collapsed core)
Collapsed core/distribution switch
Access layer (two-tier)
questions.explanation: 1.2.a.2 · weight high. Collapsed core/distribution switch: Single layer performing inter-VLAN routing, ACLs, QoS, HSRP/VRRP, and high-speed backbone forwarding. Typically L3 (Cat 9300/9500).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "First Hop Redundancy Protocols."
options: Access layer (two-tier)
HSRP / VRRP default gateway redundancy
Two-tier use case
Two-tier (collapsed core)
questions.explanation: 1.2.a.4 · weight high. HSRP / VRRP default gateway redundancy: First Hop Redundancy Protocols. Allow two switches to share a virtual IP as default gateway. Deployed at collapsed core or distribution.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L2 edge switches connecting endpoints."
options: Dual-homed access
Distribution layer
ADC mnemonic
Access layer (three-tier)
questions.explanation: 1.2.b.1 · weight high. Access layer (three-tier): L2 edge switches connecting endpoints. Port-level VLAN assignment, PoE, 802.1X, port security, DHCP snooping, DAI, QoS trust boundary.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L3 aggregation + policy enforcement."
options: Dual-homed access
ADC mnemonic
Distribution layer
Access layer (three-tier)
questions.explanation: 1.2.b.2 · weight high. Distribution layer: L3 aggregation + policy enforcement. Inter-VLAN routing (SVIs), ACLs, QoS enforcement, route summarization, HSRP/VRRP, STP root.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "High-speed backbone. Pure fast forwarding — NEVER do ACLs, QoS remarking, or policy here."
options: Route summarization
Three-tier scalability
Distribution layer
Core layer
questions.explanation: 1.2.b.3 · weight high. Core layer: High-speed backbone. Pure fast forwarding — NEVER do ACLs, QoS remarking, or policy here. 10G/40G/100G uplinks.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Distribution advertises a single summary route to the core instead of many specific routes."
options: ADC mnemonic
Distribution layer
Three-tier scalability
Route summarization
questions.explanation: 1.2.b.5 · weight high. Route summarization: Distribution advertises a single summary route to the core instead of many specific routes. Reduces core routing table size.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each access switch connects to BOTH distribution switches for redundancy."
options: Distribution layer
ADC mnemonic
Core layer
Dual-homed access
questions.explanation: 1.2.b.6 · weight high. Dual-homed access: Each access switch connects to BOTH distribution switches for redundancy. Standard three-tier practice.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Two-tier DC design. Every leaf connects to every spine; leaves never connect to leaves, spines never to spines."
options: Leaf switch (ToR)
Spine-leaf fabric
East-west traffic
ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path)
questions.explanation: 1.2.c.1 · weight high. Spine-leaf fabric: Two-tier DC design. Every leaf connects to every spine; leaves never connect to leaves, spines never to spines.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Top of Rack. Access layer for servers and storage."
options: VXLAN overlay
Leaf switch (ToR)
North-south traffic
East-west traffic
questions.explanation: 1.2.c.2 · weight high. Leaf switch (ToR): Top of Rack. Access layer for servers and storage. Do not cross-connect to other leaves.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Backbone. Only connects to leaves — never to servers or other spines."
options: North-south traffic
Spine switch
Leaf switch (ToR)
Spine-leaf fabric
questions.explanation: 1.2.c.3 · weight high. Spine switch: Backbone. Only connects to leaves — never to servers or other spines. Provides uniform forwarding capacity.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Server-to-server within the DC."
options: North-south traffic
Spine-leaf fabric
East-west traffic
Spine switch
questions.explanation: 1.2.c.4 · weight high. East-west traffic: Server-to-server within the DC. Dominates virtualized and distributed workloads. Spine-leaf optimizes for this.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L3 routing load-balances across all leaf-spine paths simultaneously."
options: Spine switch
Predictable 2-hop latency
ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path)
VXLAN overlay
questions.explanation: 1.2.c.6 · weight high. ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path): L3 routing load-balances across all leaf-spine paths simultaneously. Replaces STP's link-blocking.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Any two servers on different leaves are always exactly 2 hops apart (leaf → spine → leaf)."
options: Spine switch
Spine-leaf fabric
East-west traffic
Predictable 2-hop latency
questions.explanation: 1.2.c.7 · weight high. Predictable 2-hop latency: Any two servers on different leaves are always exactly 2 hops apart (leaf → spine → leaf).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Wide Area Network. Connects geographically separated sites over provider infrastructure."
options: MPLS
Metro Ethernet
CPE
WAN
questions.explanation: 1.2.d.1 · weight high. WAN: Wide Area Network. Connects geographically separated sites over provider infrastructure. Slower, higher-latency, more expensive than LAN.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Multiprotocol Label Switching."
options: CPE
MPLS
WAN
Metro Ethernet
questions.explanation: 1.2.d.2 · weight high. MPLS: Multiprotocol Label Switching. Provider-managed private WAN with guaranteed SLAs. L3 VPN (provider routes) or L2 VPN. Expensive, reliable.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "MPLS = guaranteed SLA, expensive, provider-routed."
options: MPLS vs SD-WAN
Demarcation point (demarc)
Broadband (DSL/Cable/Fiber)
SD-WAN
questions.explanation: 1.2.d.9 · weight high. MPLS vs SD-WAN: MPLS = guaranteed SLA, expensive, provider-routed. SD-WAN = app-aware, cheaper, uses multiple transports, centrally managed. Strategic direction: SD-WAN.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Combines router, switch (4-8 ports), AP, firewall, DHCP server, and NAT gateway in one box."
options: SOHO private ranges
SOHO network
SOHO vs Enterprise
All-in-one device
questions.explanation: 1.2.e.2 · weight high. All-in-one device: Combines router, switch (4-8 ports), AP, firewall, DHCP server, and NAT gateway in one box.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Many private IPs → single public IP using port numbers to distinguish sessions."
options: SOHO private ranges
PAT (Port Address Translation)
SOHO network
Flat network
questions.explanation: 1.2.e.4 · weight high. PAT (Port Address Translation): Many private IPs → single public IP using port numbers to distinguish sessions. The common form of NAT at a SOHO.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Organization owns, operates, hosts all infrastructure."
options: On-premises (on-prem)
PaaS
IaaS
Hybrid cloud
questions.explanation: 1.2.f.1 · weight high. On-premises (on-prem): Organization owns, operates, hosts all infrastructure. Full control, full responsibility. CapEx-heavy, slow to scale.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Third-party-hosted infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP)."
options: Cloud
PaaS
Direct cloud connections
Shared responsibility model
questions.explanation: 1.2.f.2 · weight high. Cloud: Third-party-hosted infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP). Rented on demand. OpEx model, elastic scaling, shared responsibility.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "CapEx = Capital Expenditure (upfront hardware purchase, on-prem)."
options: Direct cloud connections
PaaS
CapEx vs OpEx
Cloud
questions.explanation: 1.2.f.3 · weight high. CapEx vs OpEx: CapEx = Capital Expenditure (upfront hardware purchase, on-prem). OpEx = Operational Expenditure (pay-as-you-go, cloud).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Provider secures infrastructure; customer secures data, access, app config."
options: SaaS
Hybrid cloud
Cloud
Shared responsibility model
questions.explanation: 1.2.f.7 · weight high. Shared responsibility model: Provider secures infrastructure; customer secures data, access, app config. Split varies by service model.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "8-10μm core. Laser transmitters."
options: Multimode Fiber (MMF)
SFP / SFP+ / QSFP+ / QSFP28
Modal dispersion
Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)
questions.explanation: 1.3.a.1 · weight high. Single-Mode Fiber (SMF): 8-10μm core. Laser transmitters. 100+ km. Yellow jacket. Used for long-haul WAN and campus building-to-building backbone.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "50 or 62.5μm core. LED/VCSEL transmitters."
options: Cat6
Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)
Multimode Fiber (MMF)
UTP 100m limit
questions.explanation: 1.3.a.2 · weight high. Multimode Fiber (MMF): 50 or 62.5μm core. LED/VCSEL transmitters. Up to ~550m at 10G (OM3/OM4). Orange (OM1/OM2) or aqua (OM3/OM4) jacket.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "UTP copper. 1 Gbps at 100m max."
options: Multimode Fiber (MMF)
Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)
SFP / SFP+ / QSFP+ / QSFP28
Cat5e
questions.explanation: 1.3.a.5 · weight high. Cat5e: UTP copper. 1 Gbps at 100m max. Minimum standard for modern networks.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "UTP copper. 1 Gbps at 100m OR 10 Gbps at 55m max."
options: Multimode Fiber (MMF)
Cat5e
Cat6
Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)
questions.explanation: 1.3.a.6 · weight high. Cat6: UTP copper. 1 Gbps at 100m OR 10 Gbps at 55m max. Better shielding than Cat5e.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "UTP copper. 10 Gbps at full 100m."
options: SFP / SFP+ / QSFP+ / QSFP28
Cat6a
Multimode Fiber (MMF)
UTP 100m limit
questions.explanation: 1.3.a.7 · weight high. Cat6a: UTP copper. 10 Gbps at full 100m. Required for 10GBASE-T at max distance.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Unshielded twisted pair copper tops out at 100 meters (328 ft) regardless of category."
options: SFP / SFP+ / QSFP+ / QSFP28
UTP 100m limit
Cat5e
Fiber connectors
questions.explanation: 1.3.a.8 · weight high. UTP 100m limit: Unshielded twisted pair copper tops out at 100 meters (328 ft) regardless of category.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Legacy hub-based or coax 10BASE2/5."
options: Shared media Ethernet
Micro-segmentation
Point-to-point Ethernet
CSMA/CD
questions.explanation: 1.3.b.1 · weight high. Shared media Ethernet: Legacy hub-based or coax 10BASE2/5. All devices share one collision domain. Half-duplex only.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L1 device that repeats signals to all ports."
options: Point-to-point Ethernet
Micro-segmentation
Half-duplex vs full-duplex
Hub
questions.explanation: 1.3.b.2 · weight high. Hub: L1 device that repeats signals to all ports. No MAC awareness. One collision domain total. Obsolete.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Segment where simultaneous transmissions collide."
options: CSMA/CD
Hub
Collision domain
Point-to-point Ethernet
questions.explanation: 1.3.b.4 · weight high. Collision domain: Segment where simultaneous transmissions collide. Hub = 1 total. Switch = 1 PER PORT (micro-segmentation).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection."
options: CSMA/CD
Hub
Micro-segmentation
Half-duplex vs full-duplex
questions.explanation: 1.3.b.5 · weight high. CSMA/CD: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. Listen before transmit, detect collisions, random backoff. Needed only in half-duplex shared media.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Half-duplex = one direction at a time (hub/shared)."
options: Hub
Half-duplex vs full-duplex
Micro-segmentation
Point-to-point Ethernet
questions.explanation: 1.3.b.6 · weight high. Half-duplex vs full-duplex: Half-duplex = one direction at a time (hub/shared). Full-duplex = both directions simultaneously (switched point-to-point).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each switch port = its own collision domain."
options: Hub
Micro-segmentation
CSMA/CD
Shared media Ethernet
questions.explanation: 1.3.b.7 · weight high. Micro-segmentation: Each switch port = its own collision domain. Key benefit of switched networks over hubs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Two devices transmit on a shared collision domain at the same time; frames corrupt."
options: Collision
Late collision
Late collision causes
Collisions on full-duplex
questions.explanation: 1.4.a.1 · weight high. Collision: Two devices transmit on a shared collision domain at the same time; frames corrupt. Normal on half-duplex, never on full-duplex.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection."
options: Collisions on full-duplex
CSMA/CD
Slot time (64 bytes)
Collision
questions.explanation: 1.4.a.2 · weight high. CSMA/CD: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. Listen before transmit, detect collision, jam, random backoff, retry.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Collision window — collisions detected within the first 64 bytes are normal on half-duplex."
options: Late collision
Late collision causes
Collision
Slot time (64 bytes)
questions.explanation: 1.4.a.3 · weight high. Slot time (64 bytes): Collision window — collisions detected within the first 64 bytes are normal on half-duplex. Anything after 64 bytes is a late collision.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Collision detected after 64 bytes."
options: Late collision
Slot time (64 bytes)
Collision
Collisions on full-duplex
questions.explanation: 1.4.a.4 · weight high. Late collision: Collision detected after 64 bytes. Always abnormal. #1 symptom of duplex mismatch.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Duplex mismatch (most common), cable > 100m, faulty NIC."
options: CSMA/CD
Slot time (64 bytes)
Collisions on full-duplex
Late collision causes
questions.explanation: 1.4.a.5 · weight high. Late collision causes: Duplex mismatch (most common), cable > 100m, faulty NIC.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Should be zero. Nonzero = something broken (duplex mismatch or hardware)."
options: Late collision causes
Slot time (64 bytes)
Collisions on full-duplex
Late collision
questions.explanation: 1.4.a.6 · weight high. Collisions on full-duplex: Should be zero. Nonzero = something broken (duplex mismatch or hardware).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "FCS mismatch at receiver → frame discarded."
options: CRC error causes
CRC error
Input errors hierarchy
Troubleshooting order (L1)
questions.explanation: 1.4.b.2 · weight high. CRC error: FCS mismatch at receiver → frame discarded. Counter increments. Always a Layer 1 problem.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Bad/damaged cable (#1), EMI, faulty NIC or port, duplex mismatch, dirty fiber connectors."
options: CRC error causes
CRC error
CRC / FCS
Input errors hierarchy
questions.explanation: 1.4.b.3 · weight high. CRC error causes: Bad/damaged cable (#1), EMI, faulty NIC or port, duplex mismatch, dirty fiber connectors.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "input errors = CRC + runts + giants + frame + overrun + ignored."
options: CRC error
Input errors hierarchy
CRC / FCS
Troubleshooting order (L1)
questions.explanation: 1.4.b.4 · weight high. Input errors hierarchy: input errors = CRC + runts + giants + frame + overrun + ignored. Identify which sub-counter is rising.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "64 bytes. Anything smaller is a runt."
options: MTU mismatch
Ethernet maximum frame size
Ethernet minimum frame size
Runt
questions.explanation: 1.4.c.1 · weight high. Ethernet minimum frame size: 64 bytes. Anything smaller is a runt. Includes 14-byte header + 46-byte min payload + 4-byte FCS.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "1518 bytes standard. 1522 bytes with a single 802.1Q tag."
options: Baby giant
Ethernet maximum frame size
Ethernet minimum frame size
MTU mismatch
questions.explanation: 1.4.c.2 · weight high. Ethernet maximum frame size: 1518 bytes standard. 1522 bytes with a single 802.1Q tag. Anything larger is a giant.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Frame < 64 bytes. Collision fragment on half-duplex (normal)."
options: Giant
Runt
Ethernet maximum frame size
Ethernet minimum frame size
questions.explanation: 1.4.c.3 · weight high. Runt: Frame < 64 bytes. Collision fragment on half-duplex (normal). On full-duplex = duplex mismatch or bad NIC.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Frame > 1518 bytes (or > 1522 on trunk)."
options: Ethernet maximum frame size
MTU mismatch
Baby giant
Giant
questions.explanation: 1.4.c.4 · weight high. Giant: Frame > 1518 bytes (or > 1522 on trunk). Caused by MTU mismatch or faulty NIC.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Total count of all inbound frame problems (CRC + runts + giants + frame + overrun + ignored)."
options: Input errors
Interface resets
clear counters
Output errors
questions.explanation: 1.4.d.1 · weight high. Input errors: Total count of all inbound frame problems (CRC + runts + giants + frame + overrun + ignored).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "One side full-duplex, the other half-duplex."
options: Root cause (manual+auto)
Half-duplex side symptoms
Duplex mismatch
Full-duplex side symptoms
questions.explanation: 1.4.e.1 · weight high. Duplex mismatch: One side full-duplex, the other half-duplex. Link shows up/up but performance is terrible.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Both sides auto (preferred) OR both sides manually set to identical speed+duplex."
options: Duplex mismatch
Detection commands
Root cause (manual+auto)
Fix
questions.explanation: 1.4.e.5 · weight high. Fix: Both sides auto (preferred) OR both sides manually set to identical speed+duplex.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Different speeds on each side."
options: Parallel detection
Cisco best practice
Auto-negotiation (IEEE 802.3u)
Speed mismatch
questions.explanation: 1.4.f.1 · weight high. Speed mismatch: Different speeds on each side. Link usually fails to come up at all (down/down). Easier to spot than duplex mismatch.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Two sides exchange capabilities and pick best common speed/duplex."
options: Cisco best practice
Auto-negotiation (IEEE 802.3u)
Speed mismatch
IEEE fallback defaults
questions.explanation: 1.4.f.2 · weight high. Auto-negotiation (IEEE 802.3u): Two sides exchange capabilities and pick best common speed/duplex. Requires BOTH sides set to auto.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "When negotiation fails: half-duplex for 10/100 Mbps, full-duplex for 1000 Mbps."
options: IEEE fallback defaults
Parallel detection
Cisco best practice
Auto-negotiation (IEEE 802.3u)
questions.explanation: 1.4.f.4 · weight high. IEEE fallback defaults: When negotiation fails: half-duplex for 10/100 Mbps, full-duplex for 1000 Mbps. Source of duplex mismatch.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L1 failure. Check cable, connectors, remote power, remote interface, SFP/transceiver."
options: Router interface default
Switch interface default
up/up
down/down
questions.explanation: 1.4.g.3 · weight high. down/down: L1 failure. Check cable, connectors, remote power, remote interface, SFP/transceiver.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Interface manually disabled with 'shutdown'."
options: show ip interface brief
up/down
administratively down/down
Router interface default
questions.explanation: 1.4.g.4 · weight high. administratively down/down: Interface manually disabled with 'shutdown'. Bring up with 'no shutdown'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Shutdown by default. You must 'no shutdown' after configuring."
options: Router interface default
show ip interface brief
administratively down/down
down/down
questions.explanation: 1.4.g.5 · weight high. Router interface default: Shutdown by default. You must 'no shutdown' after configuring.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Primary command for status code check."
options: up/down
administratively down/down
Switch interface default
show ip interface brief
questions.explanation: 1.4.g.7 · weight high. show ip interface brief: Primary command for status code check. Shows interface, IP, Status, Protocol columns.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Summary table of all ports: Port, Name, Status, Vlan, Duplex, Speed, Type."
options: err-disabled
show interfaces status
Status column values
show interfaces trunk
questions.explanation: 1.4.h.2 · weight high. show interfaces status: Summary table of all ports: Port, Name, Status, Vlan, Duplex, Speed, Type. Fastest way to scan for problems.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "connected (up/up), notconnect (down/down), disabled (shutdown), err-disabled (auto-shut)."
options: a- prefix on duplex
err-disabled
Status column values
show interfaces status
questions.explanation: 1.4.h.3 · weight high. Status column values: connected (up/up), notconnect (down/down), disabled (shutdown), err-disabled (auto-shut).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Switch auto-shutdown due to security violation (port security), BPDU guard, or error condition."
options: show interfaces [int]
err-disabled
show ip interface brief
show interfaces trunk
questions.explanation: 1.4.h.4 · weight high. err-disabled: Switch auto-shutdown due to security violation (port security), BPDU guard, or error condition. Requires shut/no-shut or errdisable recovery to restore.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L3-focused: interface name, IP, OK?, Method, Status, Protocol."
options: show ip interface brief
a- prefix on duplex
Status column values
show interfaces status
questions.explanation: 1.4.h.5 · weight high. show ip interface brief: L3-focused: interface name, IP, OK?, Method, Status, Protocol. Use for IP and up/down check.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Trunk-specific: native VLAN, allowed VLANs, encapsulation, trunking mode."
options: Status column values
show interfaces trunk
show ip interface brief
show interfaces status
questions.explanation: 1.4.h.6 · weight high. show interfaces trunk: Trunk-specific: native VLAN, allowed VLANs, encapsulation, trunking mode.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Transmission Control Protocol."
options: TCP acknowledgments (ACK)
TCP sequence numbers
TCP flags
TCP
questions.explanation: 1.5.a.1 · weight high. TCP: Transmission Control Protocol. L4, connection-oriented, reliable, ordered delivery.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Every byte of data has a sequence number."
options: TCP acknowledgments (ACK)
TCP header size
TCP sequence numbers
TCP retransmission
questions.explanation: 1.5.a.2 · weight high. TCP sequence numbers: Every byte of data has a sequence number. Enables ordered reassembly and duplicate detection.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Receiver confirms received bytes."
options: TCP flags
TCP header size
TCP flow control (windowing)
TCP acknowledgments (ACK)
questions.explanation: 1.5.a.3 · weight high. TCP acknowledgments (ACK): Receiver confirms received bytes. Missing ACK triggers retransmission at sender.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Sender resends unacknowledged data after timeout."
options: TCP congestion control
TCP acknowledgments (ACK)
TCP retransmission
TCP sequence numbers
questions.explanation: 1.5.a.4 · weight high. TCP retransmission: Sender resends unacknowledged data after timeout. Core reliability mechanism.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Receiver advertises window size — how much unACK'd data sender may transmit."
options: TCP flags
TCP flow control (windowing)
TCP sequence numbers
TCP acknowledgments (ACK)
questions.explanation: 1.5.a.5 · weight high. TCP flow control (windowing): Receiver advertises window size — how much unACK'd data sender may transmit.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "20 bytes minimum, 60 bytes maximum (with options)."
options: TCP header size
TCP
TCP sequence numbers
TCP acknowledgments (ACK)
questions.explanation: 1.5.a.7 · weight high. TCP header size: 20 bytes minimum, 60 bytes maximum (with options). Larger than UDP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "SYN (synchronize), ACK (acknowledge), FIN (finish), RST (reset), PSH (push), URG (urgent)."
options: TCP sequence numbers
TCP congestion control
TCP flags
TCP acknowledgments (ACK)
questions.explanation: 1.5.a.8 · weight high. TCP flags: SYN (synchronize), ACK (acknowledge), FIN (finish), RST (reset), PSH (push), URG (urgent).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. Establishes TCP connection and synchronizes sequence numbers."
options: Three-way handshake sequence
Initial Sequence Number (ISN)
SYN-ACK (step 2)
ACK (step 3)
questions.explanation: 1.5.b.1 · weight high. Three-way handshake sequence: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. Establishes TCP connection and synchronizes sequence numbers.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Client sends SYN flag with its Initial Sequence Number (ISN)."
options: SYN (step 1)
ACK (step 3)
SYN-ACK (step 2)
Three-way handshake sequence
questions.explanation: 1.5.b.2 · weight high. SYN (step 1): Client sends SYN flag with its Initial Sequence Number (ISN). Enters SYN-SENT state.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Server responds with SYN + ACK."
options: MSS (Maximum Segment Size)
SYN-ACK (step 2)
Initial Sequence Number (ISN)
Three-way handshake sequence
questions.explanation: 1.5.b.3 · weight high. SYN-ACK (step 2): Server responds with SYN + ACK. Carries server's ISN and ACKs client's SYN (ack = client ISN + 1).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Client ACKs server's SYN (ack = server ISN + 1)."
options: ESTABLISHED state
Three-way handshake sequence
SYN (step 1)
ACK (step 3)
questions.explanation: 1.5.b.4 · weight high. ACK (step 3): Client ACKs server's SYN (ack = server ISN + 1). Both sides enter ESTABLISHED.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Both sides completed handshake; ready for application data transfer."
options: SYN-ACK (step 2)
Three-way handshake sequence
MSS (Maximum Segment Size)
ESTABLISHED state
questions.explanation: 1.5.b.7 · weight high. ESTABLISHED state: Both sides completed handshake; ready for application data transfer.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'I have no more data to send.' Initiates graceful close of one direction."
options: Graceful vs abrupt close
TIME-WAIT state
FIN flag
Four-way teardown sequence
questions.explanation: 1.5.c.2 · weight high. FIN flag: 'I have no more data to send.' Initiates graceful close of one direction.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Abrupt connection termination."
options: Four-way teardown sequence
FIN flag
RST flag
TIME-WAIT state
questions.explanation: 1.5.c.5 · weight high. RST flag: Abrupt connection termination. No FIN/ACK exchange. Used for refused connections, crashes, or firewall denies.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "FIN = graceful (hang-up after goodbye)."
options: TIME-WAIT state
FIN flag
Half-close
Graceful vs abrupt close
questions.explanation: 1.5.c.6 · weight high. Graceful vs abrupt close: FIN = graceful (hang-up after goodbye). RST = abrupt (slam phone down).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Bytes the sender can transmit before waiting for an ACK."
options: Slow start
TCP window size
Window size 0
Congestion avoidance
questions.explanation: 1.5.d.1 · weight high. TCP window size: Bytes the sender can transmit before waiting for an ACK. Dynamically advertised by receiver.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Ordering (reassembly), duplicate detection, and ACK reporting ('send me byte N next')."
options: Window size 0
Sequence number uses
Congestion avoidance
Fast retransmit
questions.explanation: 1.5.d.6 · weight high. Sequence number uses: Ordering (reassembly), duplicate detection, and ACK reporting ('send me byte N next').

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "8 bytes only. Four fields: source port, destination port, length, checksum."
options: Best-effort delivery
UDP header size
UDP
Connectionless
questions.explanation: 1.5.e.2 · weight high. UDP header size: 8 bytes only. Four fields: source port, destination port, length, checksum.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "No handshake, no connection state."
options: UDP
UDP header size
No ordering
Connectionless
questions.explanation: 1.5.e.3 · weight high. Connectionless: No handshake, no connection state. Sender just sends datagrams.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Datagrams can arrive out of order; no sequence numbers to reassemble."
options: No ordering
Connectionless
UDP
Best-effort delivery
questions.explanation: 1.5.e.5 · weight high. No ordering: Datagrams can arrive out of order; no sequence numbers to reassemble.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "VoIP, video streaming/conferencing, online gaming, DNS queries, DHCP, SNMP, Syslog, NTP, TFTP."
options: Connectionless
UDP use cases
Best-effort delivery
UDP

questions.explanation: 1.5.f.2 · weight high. FTP Control — TCP 21: FTP command channel. Maintains the session (login, ls, get, put).

options: Telnet — TCP 23
IMAP — TCP 143
SMTP — TCP 25

options: FTP Data — TCP 20
SMTP — TCP 25
HTTP — TCP 80
POP3 — TCP 110
questions.explanation: 1.5.f.5 · weight high. SMTP — TCP 25: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol. Server-to-server mail relay. Port 587 used for authenticated submission.

options: HTTP — TCP 80
HTTPS — TCP 443

SMTP — TCP 25

options: IMAP — TCP 143
SMTP — TCP 25
HTTP — TCP 80
Port ranges
questions.explanation: 1.5.f.8 · weight high. IMAP — TCP 143: Internet Message Access Protocol. Access email kept on server. Multi-device sync.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Standard name resolution queries."
options: TFTP — UDP 69
DNS — UDP 53
Syslog severity levels
DHCP Client — UDP 68
questions.explanation: 1.5.g.1 · weight high. DNS — UDP 53: Standard name resolution queries. Small, stateless, fast. >99% of DNS uses UDP.

options: DHCP Client — UDP 68
DHCP Server — UDP 67
TFTP — UDP 69

questions.explanation: 1.5.g.4 · weight high. TFTP — UDP 69: Trivial FTP. No authentication, no directory listing. Used for router/switch config and IOS transfer.

options: DHCP Server — UDP 67
SNMP query — UDP 161

questions.explanation: 1.5.g.6 · weight high. SNMP query — UDP 161: Management station sends GET/SET to device on 161.

options: DNS — UDP 53
DHCP Server — UDP 67
SNMP trap — UDP 162
SNMP query — UDP 161
questions.explanation: 1.5.g.7 · weight high. SNMP trap — UDP 162: Device sends unsolicited alert to management station on 162.

options: TFTP — UDP 69
DHCP Client — UDP 68
Syslog — UDP 514
SNMP query — UDP 161
questions.explanation: 1.5.g.8 · weight high. Syslog — UDP 514: Centralized logging destination port. Severity levels 0-7.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "0 Emergency, 1 Alert, 2 Critical, 3 Error, 4 Warning, 5 Notification, 6 Informational, 7 Debug."
options: Syslog severity levels

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Default for standard name queries."
options: Dual-protocol port (DNS)
DNS over UDP 53
DNS over TCP 53
TC (Truncation) flag
questions.explanation: 1.5.h.1 · weight high. DNS over UDP 53: Default for standard name queries. Small, fast. Used for ~99% of DNS traffic.
