options: AXFR
TC (Truncation) flag
DNS over TCP 53
DNS over UDP 53
questions.explanation: 1.5.h.2 · weight high. DNS over TCP 53: Used for (1) zone transfers and (2) truncated UDP responses. Same port as UDP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Set in UDP DNS response when it's too big."
options: DNS over TCP 53
AXFR
TC (Truncation) flag
IXFR
questions.explanation: 1.5.h.3 · weight high. TC (Truncation) flag: Set in UDP DNS response when it's too big. Tells client to retry over TCP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DNS is the canonical example — one port (53) serving both TCP and UDP with different roles."
options: AXFR
Dual-protocol port (DNS)
DNS over UDP 53
DNS over TCP 53
questions.explanation: 1.5.h.6 · weight high. Dual-protocol port (DNS): DNS is the canonical example — one port (53) serving both TCP and UDP with different roles.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "32-bit number written as 4 octets in dotted-decimal (e.g., 192.168.1.10)."
options: Network vs host portion
Positional values
Network address
IPv4 address
questions.explanation: 1.6.a.1 · weight high. IPv4 address: 32-bit number written as 4 octets in dotted-decimal (e.g., 192.168.1.10).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "IP AND subnet mask = network address."
options: AND operation
IPv4 address space
Broadcast address
Network address
questions.explanation: 1.6.a.7 · weight high. AND operation: IP AND subnet mask = network address. How a router determines which subnet an IP belongs to.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "First octet 1-126. Default /8."
options: Class C
Class B
Class A
Class E
questions.explanation: 1.6.b.1 · weight high. Class A: First octet 1-126. Default /8. Leading bit 0. ~16.7M hosts/network. Example: 10.0.0.0/8.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "First octet 128-191. Default /16."
options: Class A
Class B
CIDR replaced classful
Class E
questions.explanation: 1.6.b.2 · weight high. Class B: First octet 128-191. Default /16. Leading bits 10. 65,534 hosts/network. Example: 172.16.0.0/16.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "First octet 192-223. Default /24."
options: Class D
Class B
Class C
questions.explanation: 1.6.b.3 · weight high. Class C: First octet 192-223. Default /24. Leading bits 110. 254 hosts/network. Example: 192.168.1.0/24.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "First octet 224-239. Leading bits 1110."
options: Class D
Class E
Class A
questions.explanation: 1.6.b.4 · weight high. Class D: First octet 224-239. Leading bits 1110. Reserved for multicast. Not assignable to hosts.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "127 is NOT Class A — it's loopback."
options: CIDR replaced classful
Class D
Class A
questions.explanation: 1.6.b.6 · weight high. 127.0.0.0/8 gap: 127 is NOT Class A — it's loopback. Exam trick: first-octet ranges skip 127.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "32-bit value. Contiguous 1s (network) followed by contiguous 0s (host)."
options: /26 mask
/24 mask
CIDR /n notation
Subnet mask format
questions.explanation: 1.6.c.1 · weight high. Subnet mask format: 32-bit value. Contiguous 1s (network) followed by contiguous 0s (host). 0 after 1 is INVALID.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Counts the 1-bits. /24 = 24 ones = 255.255.255.0."
options: CIDR /n notation
Subnet mask format
/26 mask
/30 mask
questions.explanation: 1.6.c.2 · weight high. CIDR /n notation: Counts the 1-bits. /24 = 24 ones = 255.255.255.0. Must convert instantly both directions.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "255.255.255.0. 256 addresses, 254 usable hosts."
options: /26 mask
/31 mask
/24 mask
CIDR /n notation
questions.explanation: 1.6.c.3 · weight high. /24 mask: 255.255.255.0. 256 addresses, 254 usable hosts. Class C default.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "255.255.255.128. 128 addresses, 126 usable hosts."
options: /28 mask
Subnet mask format
/25 mask
/24 mask
questions.explanation: 1.6.c.4 · weight high. /25 mask: 255.255.255.128. 128 addresses, 126 usable hosts. Block size 128.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "255.255.255.192. 64 addresses, 62 usable hosts."
options: /26 mask
Subnet mask format
CIDR /n notation
/24 mask
questions.explanation: 1.6.c.5 · weight high. /26 mask: 255.255.255.192. 64 addresses, 62 usable hosts. Block size 64.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "255.255.255.224. 32 addresses, 30 usable hosts."
options: /27 mask
CIDR /n notation
Subnet mask format
/28 mask
questions.explanation: 1.6.c.6 · weight high. /27 mask: 255.255.255.224. 32 addresses, 30 usable hosts. Block size 32.

options: /28 mask
/25 mask
/24 mask
Subnet mask format
questions.explanation: 1.6.c.7 · weight high. /28 mask: 255.255.255.240. 16 addresses, 14 usable hosts. Block size 16.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "255.255.255.248. 8 addresses, 6 usable hosts."
options: CIDR /n notation
/27 mask
/29 mask
/30 mask
questions.explanation: 1.6.c.8 · weight high. /29 mask: 255.255.255.248. 8 addresses, 6 usable hosts. Block size 8.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "255.255.255.252. 4 addresses, 2 usable."
options: Subnet mask format
/27 mask
/30 mask
CIDR /n notation
questions.explanation: 1.6.c.9 · weight high. /30 mask: 255.255.255.252. 4 addresses, 2 usable. Standard for point-to-point links.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Block size = 256 − interesting-octet mask value."
options: Find network address
Interesting octet
Find broadcast address
Block size method
questions.explanation: 1.6.d.1 · weight high. Block size method: Block size = 256 − interesting-octet mask value. Subnets start at multiples of block size.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "The octet where the mask transitions from 1s to 0s."
options: Find network address
Interesting octet
First usable host
Block size method
questions.explanation: 1.6.d.2 · weight high. Interesting octet: The octet where the mask transitions from 1s to 0s. Do your math here.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Round IP's interesting octet DOWN to the nearest multiple of the block size."
options: Last usable host
Find network address
Interesting octet
Find broadcast address
questions.explanation: 1.6.d.3 · weight high. Find network address: Round IP's interesting octet DOWN to the nearest multiple of the block size. All host bits = 0.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Next network − 1. Or: all host bits = 1."
options: Interesting octet
Find broadcast address
First usable host
Block size method
questions.explanation: 1.6.d.4 · weight high. Find broadcast address: Next network − 1. Or: all host bits = 1.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Number of subnets = 2^(borrowed bits)."
options: Host-count column (Class C)
Subnets formula
Borrowed bits
Hosts formula
questions.explanation: 1.6.e.1 · weight high. Subnets formula: Number of subnets = 2^(borrowed bits). Each borrowed bit doubles subnets.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Hosts per subnet = 2^(host bits) − 2."
options: Hosts formula
Subnets formula
Borrowed bits
Round up to power of 2
questions.explanation: 1.6.e.2 · weight high. Hosts formula: Hosts per subnet = 2^(host bits) − 2. Minus 2 for network and broadcast.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Bits taken from host portion and given to network portion."
options: Host-count column (Class C)
Hosts formula
Borrowed bits
Round up to power of 2
questions.explanation: 1.6.e.3 · weight high. Borrowed bits: Bits taken from host portion and given to network portion. Mask /26 from /24 default = 2 borrowed.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "/25=126, /26=62, /27=30, /28=14, /29=6, /30=2."
options: Round up to power of 2
Host-count column (Class C)
Subnets formula
Hosts formula
questions.explanation: 1.6.e.4 · weight high. Host-count column (Class C): /25=126, /26=62, /27=30, /28=14, /29=6, /30=2. Memorize cold.

options: Hosts formula
Round up to power of 2
Host-count column (Class C)
Subnets formula
questions.explanation: 1.6.e.5 · weight high. Round up to power of 2: Need 50 hosts → 2^6 − 2 = 62 (fits). Always round UP to the next fitting power.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Always allocate the largest required subnet first, then next-largest, and so on."

RIPv1 / IGRP
Largest-first rule
questions.explanation: 1.6.f.2 · weight high. Largest-first rule: Always allocate the largest required subnet first, then next-largest, and so on.

options: Largest-first rule
Classless routing required
RIPv1 / IGRP
No overlaps / no gaps
questions.explanation: 1.6.f.3 · weight high. No overlaps / no gaps: Each new subnet starts right after the previous subnet's broadcast address.

options: Classless routing required
/30 for P2P links
VLSM
No overlaps / no gaps
questions.explanation: 1.6.f.4 · weight high. Classless routing required: OSPF, EIGRP, RIPv2 support VLSM. They include subnet mask in routing updates.

options: Hex A-F values
Binary → decimal
Hex → decimal
Hex digit = 4 bits
questions.explanation: 1.6.g.2 · weight high. Binary → decimal: Sum the positional values of all 1-bits. 11001010 = 128+64+8+2 = 202.

options: Hex digit = 4 bits
Binary → decimal
Hex → decimal
Decimal → binary
questions.explanation: 1.6.g.3 · weight high. Decimal → binary: Subtract largest fitting power of 2, mark a 1, repeat. Always write all 8 bits.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "0, 128, 192, 224, 240, 248, 252, 254, 255."
options: Hex → decimal
Positional values (128-1)
Hex digit = 4 bits
9 valid mask octet values
questions.explanation: 1.6.g.4 · weight high. 9 valid mask octet values: 0, 128, 192, 224, 240, 248, 252, 254, 255. Each adds one more 1-bit from the left.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each hexadecimal character represents exactly 4 bits."
options: Decimal → binary
Binary → decimal
Hex digit = 4 bits
Hex → decimal
questions.explanation: 1.6.g.5 · weight high. Hex digit = 4 bits: Each hexadecimal character represents exactly 4 bits. 0-9 = 0-9, A-F = 10-15.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15."
options: Binary → decimal
Hex A-F values
Positional values (128-1)
9 valid mask octet values
questions.explanation: 1.6.g.6 · weight high. Hex A-F values: A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.

ping

traceroute / tracert

options: show ip route
Route code L (Local)
Route codes O / D / R
ping
questions.explanation: 1.6.h.4 · weight high. Route code L (Local): /32 host route for the router's own interface IP.

options: Route code L (Local)
S* (candidate default)
Route code S (Static)
show ip route

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "O = OSPF, D = EIGRP (Dual-algorithm), R = RIP."
options: Route codes O / D / R
Route code S (Static)
S* (candidate default)
show ip interface brief
questions.explanation: 1.6.h.6 · weight high. Route codes O / D / R: O = OSPF, D = EIGRP (Dual-algorithm), R = RIP. Dynamically learned routes.

options: Route code L (Local)
traceroute / tracert
S* (candidate default)

questions.explanation: 1.6.h.7 · weight high. S* (candidate default): Static default route (0.0.0.0/0) marked as candidate for gateway of last resort.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Shows every hop along the path to destination."
options: S* (candidate default)
show ip interface brief
traceroute / tracert
Route codes O / D / R
questions.explanation: 1.6.h.9 · weight high. traceroute / tracert: Shows every hop along the path to destination. Identifies where packets drop.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Private Class A. All 10.x.x.x addresses."
options: Not internet-routable
APIPA
Enterprise use of 10/8
questions.explanation: 1.7.a.1 · weight high. 10.0.0.0/8 range: Private Class A. All 10.x.x.x addresses. 16,777,214 hosts. Largest RFC 1918 range.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Internet routers drop packets with 10.x.x.x source or destination."
options: Docker default
Not internet-routable
Enterprise use of 10/8
questions.explanation: 1.7.a.3 · weight high. Not internet-routable: Internet routers drop packets with 10.x.x.x source or destination. Requires NAT to reach internet.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Valid range is 172.16.0.0 through 172.31.255.255."

Edge case: 172.15.x.x

options: /12 binary explanation
Edge case: 172.15.x.x
Docker default

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "PUBLIC. Above the 172.31 end of the private range."
options: Edge case: 172.32.x.x
/12 binary explanation
Edge case: 172.15.x.x
questions.explanation: 1.7.b.4 · weight high. Edge case: 172.32.x.x: PUBLIC. Above the 172.31 end of the private range.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Private Class C. All 192.168.x.x addresses."
options: /12 binary explanation
Home router default
192.168 vs 192.169 boundary
questions.explanation: 1.7.c.1 · weight high. 192.168.0.0/16 range: Private Class C. All 192.168.x.x addresses. 65,534 hosts. Familiar from home networks.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Almost every consumer router uses 192.168.0.0/24 or 192.168.1.0/24 with gateway .1."
options: 192.168 vs 192.169 boundary
Home router default
Docker default
questions.explanation: 1.7.c.2 · weight high. Home router default: Almost every consumer router uses 192.168.0.0/24 or 192.168.1.0/24 with gateway .1.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Defines the three private IPv4 ranges: 10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16."

Inside Local
PAT / NAT Overload
RFC 1918
questions.explanation: 1.7.d.1 · weight high. RFC 1918: Defines the three private IPv4 ranges: 10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16. Not routable on public internet.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Many-to-one using ports to differentiate."
options: Inside Global
Outside Local
PAT / NAT Overload
RFC 1918
questions.explanation: 1.7.d.5 · weight high. PAT / NAT Overload: Many-to-one using ports to differentiate. Command keyword 'overload'. What home routers do.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Private IP of internal host (e.g., 192.168.1.10)."
options: Inside Global
Inside Local
show ip nat translations
RFC 1918
questions.explanation: 1.7.d.6 · weight high. Inside Local: Private IP of internal host (e.g., 192.168.1.10). The 'real' internal address.

options: Outside Local
RFC 1918
NAT

questions.explanation: 1.7.d.7 · weight high. Inside Global: Public IP that internal host is translated to (e.g., 203.0.113.5). What the internet sees.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "View current NAT translation table."

Inside Local
Inside Global
Outside Local
questions.explanation: 1.7.d.10 · weight high. show ip nat translations: View current NAT translation table. show ip nat statistics shows hit counts.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Automatic Private IP Addressing."

APIPA on Windows/macOS
APIPA not routable

APIPA on Windows/macOS
APIPA not routable

APIPA

questions.explanation: 1.7.e.3 · weight high. APIPA not routable: 169.254.x.x is link-local only. Cannot reach other subnets or the internet.

APIPA

APIPA meaning in practice

127.0.0.0/8 loopback range

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Exam trick: 127.5.5.5 is ALSO loopback."
options: All 127.x.x.x is loopback
127.0.0.1 / localhost
Loopback address vs interface
Ping order (bottom-up)
questions.explanation: 1.7.f.3 · weight high. All 127.x.x.x is loopback: Exam trick: 127.5.5.5 is ALSO loopback. The whole /8 counts, not just .0.0.1.

options: 127.0.0.0/8 loopback range

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "128 bits. 2^128 ≈ 340 undecillion addresses."
options: IPv6 address length
SLAAC support
Hextet
Why IPv6
questions.explanation: 1.8.a.1 · weight high. IPv6 address length: 128 bits. 2^128 ≈ 340 undecillion addresses. 4× larger than IPv4's 32 bits.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "One of the 8 groups in an IPv6 address."
options: No broadcast in IPv6
Hextet
64/64 split
IPv6 address length
questions.explanation: 1.8.a.2 · weight high. Hextet: One of the 8 groups in an IPv6 address. 16 bits = 4 hex digits. Separated by colons.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Fixed 40 bytes. Simpler than IPv4 — no checksum, no fragmentation fields, options in extension headers."
options: IPv6 header size
Hextet
Why IPv6
No broadcast in IPv6
questions.explanation: 1.8.a.3 · weight high. IPv6 header size: Fixed 40 bytes. Simpler than IPv4 — no checksum, no fragmentation fields, options in extension headers.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "IPv6 eliminates broadcast entirely."
options: IPv6 header size
No broadcast in IPv6
SLAAC support
IPv6 address length
questions.explanation: 1.8.a.4 · weight high. No broadcast in IPv6: IPv6 eliminates broadcast entirely. Replaced by multicast and anycast.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Hosts can generate their own addresses via Stateless Address Autoconfiguration."
options: SLAAC support
IPv6 address length
64/64 split
IPv6 header size
questions.explanation: 1.8.a.5 · weight high. SLAAC support: Hosts can generate their own addresses via Stateless Address Autoconfiguration. No DHCP required.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Standard IPv6 LAN: first 64 bits = network prefix, last 64 bits = interface ID."
options: IPv6 address length
Why IPv6
64/64 split
IPv6 header size
questions.explanation: 1.8.a.6 · weight high. 64/64 split: Standard IPv6 LAN: first 64 bits = network prefix, last 64 bits = interface ID.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Remove leading zeros in each hextet."
options: ::1 (loopback)
:: only ONCE
Rule 1: Drop leading zeros
Rule 2: Double colon ::
questions.explanation: 1.8.b.1 · weight high. Rule 1: Drop leading zeros: Remove leading zeros in each hextet. 0DB8 → DB8, 0001 → 1, 0000 → 0. Must keep at least one digit.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Replace ONE consecutive run of all-zero hextets with ::."
options: Rule 2: Double colon ::
:: alone
Rule 1: Drop leading zeros
::1 (loopback)
questions.explanation: 1.8.b.2 · weight high. Rule 2: Double colon ::: Replace ONE consecutive run of all-zero hextets with ::. Use for the longest run.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Double colon can only appear once per address."
options: :: only ONCE
:: alone
::1 (loopback)
Rule 2: Double colon ::
questions.explanation: 1.8.b.3 · weight high. :: only ONCE: Double colon can only appear once per address. Two would be ambiguous.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Count visible hextets; subtract from 8; fill :: with that many 0000 groups."
options: Rule 1: Drop leading zeros
:: alone
Expanding ::
:: only ONCE
questions.explanation: 1.8.b.4 · weight high. Expanding ::: Count visible hextets; subtract from 8; fill :: with that many 0000 groups.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Every individual IPv6 LAN segment."
options: /128 host route
/64 standard LAN prefix
/48 site allocation
/127 point-to-point
questions.explanation: 1.8.c.1 · weight high. /64 standard LAN prefix: Every individual IPv6 LAN segment. Required for SLAAC (interface ID must be 64 bits).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Typical block from ISP to organization."
options: /127 point-to-point
No subnet math (usually)
/48 site allocation
/128 host route
questions.explanation: 1.8.c.2 · weight high. /48 site allocation: Typical block from ISP to organization. 16 bits available for internal subnetting → 65,536 /64 subnets.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Recommended for router-to-router P2P links (RFC 6164)."
options: /48 site allocation
/127 point-to-point
No subnet math (usually)
/64 standard LAN prefix
questions.explanation: 1.8.c.3 · weight high. /127 point-to-point: Recommended for router-to-router P2P links (RFC 6164). 2 addresses, no waste. IPv6 analogue of IPv4 /30.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Single address. Used for loopback interfaces and specific host routes."
options: No subnet math (usually)
/127 point-to-point
/48 site allocation
/128 host route
questions.explanation: 1.8.c.4 · weight high. /128 host route: Single address. Used for loopback interfaces and specific host routes.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Stateless Address Autoconfiguration."
options: SLAAC
Router Solicitation (RS)
A-flag
DAD (Duplicate Address Detection)
questions.explanation: 1.8.d.1 · weight high. SLAAC: Stateless Address Autoconfiguration. Host self-generates IPv6 address from RA prefix + interface ID. No DHCP required.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "ICMPv6 Type 133. Host sends to FF02::2 asking for network config."
options: Router Advertisement (RA)
SLAAC
Router Solicitation (RS)
DAD (Duplicate Address Detection)
questions.explanation: 1.8.d.2 · weight high. Router Solicitation (RS): ICMPv6 Type 133. Host sends to FF02::2 asking for network config.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "ICMPv6 Type 134. Router responds with prefix, default gateway (its link-local), and A/O/M flags."
options: Router Solicitation (RS)
EUI-64 interface ID
Router Advertisement (RA)
DAD (Duplicate Address Detection)
questions.explanation: 1.8.d.3 · weight high. Router Advertisement (RA): ICMPv6 Type 134. Router responds with prefix, default gateway (its link-local), and A/O/M flags.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Generated from MAC by inserting FFFE in middle and flipping 7th bit."
options: DAD (Duplicate Address Detection)
EUI-64 interface ID
Router Solicitation (RS)
SLAAC
questions.explanation: 1.8.d.4 · weight high. EUI-64 interface ID: Generated from MAC by inserting FFFE in middle and flipping 7th bit. 64 bits.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Host sends NS for its own address before claiming it."
options: Router Solicitation (RS)
Privacy extensions
EUI-64 interface ID
DAD (Duplicate Address Detection)
questions.explanation: 1.8.d.6 · weight high. DAD (Duplicate Address Detection): Host sends NS for its own address before claiming it. No reply = address is unique.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Address Autoconfiguration flag in RA."
options: A-flag
Router Advertisement (RA)
Router Solicitation (RS)
SLAAC
questions.explanation: 1.8.d.7 · weight high. A-flag: Address Autoconfiguration flag in RA. A=1 tells host to use SLAAC for addressing.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Hybrid: SLAAC provides the address, DHCPv6 provides DNS/domain/NTP."
options: Cisco O-flag command
Stateless DHCPv6
O-flag
questions.explanation: 1.8.e.1 · weight high. Stateless DHCPv6: Hybrid: SLAAC provides the address, DHCPv6 provides DNS/domain/NTP. Server does NOT track address assignments.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Other Configuration. O=1 in RA tells host to query DHCPv6 for DNS/other info (but not address)."
options: O-flag
Stateless DHCPv6
Cisco O-flag command
questions.explanation: 1.8.e.2 · weight high. O-flag: Other Configuration. O=1 in RA tells host to query DHCPv6 for DNS/other info (but not address).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DHCPv6 server assigns full address and tracks it in a binding table."
options: M-flag
Cisco M-flag command
Stateful DHCPv6
Link-local always auto
questions.explanation: 1.8.f.1 · weight high. Stateful DHCPv6: DHCPv6 server assigns full address and tracks it in a binding table. Like DHCPv4 for IPv6.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Managed Address Configuration."
options: SARR process
Link-local always auto
Stateful DHCPv6
M-flag
questions.explanation: 1.8.f.2 · weight high. M-flag: Managed Address Configuration. M=1 in RA tells host to get its address from DHCPv6 server.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Solicit → Advertise → Request → Reply."
options: Gateway STILL from RA
Stateful DHCPv6
Link-local always auto
SARR process
questions.explanation: 1.8.f.3 · weight high. SARR process: Solicit → Advertise → Request → Reply. Four-step DHCPv6 exchange. Analogous to DORA in DHCPv4.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Even with stateful DHCPv6, the default gateway comes from the Router Advertisement, NOT DHCPv6."
options: M-flag
Stateful DHCPv6
Gateway STILL from RA
SARR process
questions.explanation: 1.8.f.4 · weight high. Gateway STILL from RA: Even with stateful DHCPv6, the default gateway comes from the Router Advertisement, NOT DHCPv6. Classic exam gotcha.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "FE80:: link-local is always self-generated via SLAAC, regardless of which method is used for the global address."
options: Gateway STILL from RA
Stateful DHCPv6
Cisco M-flag command
Link-local always auto

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Router Solicitation. Host → FF02::2 asking for router info."
options: Solicited-node multicast
NDP
RS (Type 133)
RA (Type 134)
questions.explanation: 1.8.g.2 · weight high. RS (Type 133): Router Solicitation. Host → FF02::2 asking for router info.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx. Last 24 bits of unicast."
options: Solicited-node multicast
NA (Type 136)
NDP
RS (Type 133)
questions.explanation: 1.8.g.6 · weight high. Solicited-node multicast: FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx. Last 24 bits of unicast. Efficient NS delivery; avoids full broadcast.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Neighbor cache view. IPv6 equivalent of 'show arp'."
options: NS (Type 135)
Solicited-node multicast
show ipv6 neighbors
NA (Type 136)
questions.explanation: 1.8.g.7 · weight high. show ipv6 neighbors: Neighbor cache view. IPv6 equivalent of 'show arp'.

'ipv6 enable'
show ipv6 route
show ipv6 interface brief

'ipv6 address ... /64'

questions.explanation: 1.8.h.2 · weight high. 'ipv6 address ... /64': Interface command. Assigns global unicast. Auto-enables IPv6 and generates a link-local.

'ipv6 address ... /64'

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "IPv6 routing table. Codes: C (connected), L (local /128), S (static), O (OSPFv3), D (EIGRP for IPv6)."

show ipv6 route
'ipv6 unicast-routing'
'ipv6 address FE80::1 link-local'
questions.explanation: 1.8.h.6 · weight high. show ipv6 route: IPv6 routing table. Codes: C (connected), L (local /128), S (static), O (OSPFv3), D (EIGRP for IPv6).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Prefix 2000::/3. Starts with 2 or 3."
options: Unique Local Address (ULA)
GUA structure
Link-Local Address (LLA)
Global Unicast Address (GUA)

Link-Local Address (LLA)
Unique Local Address (ULA)
LLA required for IPv6
questions.explanation: 1.9.a.2 · weight high. Unique Local Address (ULA): FC00::/7, in practice FD00::/8. Starts with FD. IPv6 equivalent of RFC 1918 private. Not internet-routable.

Quick prefix recognition
Global Unicast Address (GUA)
Link-Local Address (LLA)

Quick prefix recognition
Global Unicast Address (GUA)
Link-Local Address (LLA)

Subnet-Router Anycast
Anycast

options: Anycast has no special prefix
Cisco anycast command
Anycast use cases
Anycast

FF02::A (EIGRP routers)

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx. xx:xxxx = last 24 bits of a unicast."

FF02::5 (OSPF routers)

questions.explanation: 1.9.c.7 · weight high. Solicited-node multicast: FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx. xx:xxxx = last 24 bits of a unicast. One solicited-node group per interface address.

EUI-64 example
Privacy extensions (RFC 7217)

options: Step 2: Stuff FFFE
Modified EUI-64
EUI-64 example
Step 1: Split MAC

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Insert FF:FE between the two halves."
options: Privacy extensions (RFC 7217)
Modified EUI-64
Step 2: Stuff FFFE
Step 3: Flip 7th bit
questions.explanation: 1.9.d.3 · weight high. Step 2: Stuff FFFE: Insert FF:FE between the two halves. Expands to 64 bits. Signature of EUI-64.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "MAC 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E → 021A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E."

EUI-64 example
Modified EUI-64

ipconfig is Windows-only

ipconfig

macOS uses ifconfig

Wrong DNS symptom
Wrong subnet mask symptom

IP addressing mismatch
APIPA symptom
Wrong subnet mask symptom

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Some hosts reachable, others not."

Wrong DNS symptom
APIPA symptom

dig

nslookup

dig
ping

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "(1) ping 127.0.0.1 (stack) (2) own IP (NIC) (3) gateway (local) (4) remote IP (routing) (5) hostname (DNS)."

pathping (Windows)
traceroute / tracert
dig

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Only three non-overlapping 2.4 GHz channels in US."

2.4 GHz channels 1, 6, 11
Co-channel interference (CCI)
5 GHz channel bonding
questions.explanation: 1.11.a.1 · weight high. 2.4 GHz channels 1, 6, 11: Only three non-overlapping 2.4 GHz channels in US. Each 22 MHz wide, spaced 5 MHz apart.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "24+ non-overlapping 20 MHz channels."

5 GHz non-overlapping

Adjacent-channel interference (ACI)

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Two APs on OVERLAPPING channels (e.g., 1 and 3)."

2.4 GHz channels 1, 6, 11
2.4 GHz interference sources
Co-channel interference (CCI)
questions.explanation: 1.11.a.5 · weight high. Adjacent-channel interference (ACI): Two APs on OVERLAPPING channels (e.g., 1 and 3). Worse than CCI — signals corrupt each other.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Basic Service Set. One AP + its associated clients."

BSSID
Hidden SSID != security
SSID

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "One AP can broadcast many SSIDs, each mapped to a different VLAN/policy (corp, guest, IoT)."
options: BSS
SSID
Hidden SSID != security
Multiple SSIDs per AP
questions.explanation: 1.11.b.6 · weight high. Multiple SSIDs per AP: One AP can broadcast many SSIDs, each mapped to a different VLAN/policy (corp, guest, IoT).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Suppressing SSID in beacons does NOT secure anything."
options: BSSID
ESS
Hidden SSID != security
SSID

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "2.4 GHz = better range/wall penetration, more interference."
options: dBm scale
2.4 GHz vs 5 GHz tradeoff
SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)
3 dB rule
questions.explanation: 1.11.c.1 · weight high. 2.4 GHz vs 5 GHz tradeoff: 2.4 GHz = better range/wall penetration, more interference. 5 GHz = faster, more channels, shorter range.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "-30=excellent, -50=good, -60=OK, -70=weak minimum, -80=very weak, -90=unusable."
options: 3 dB rule
Wi-Fi 6E
dBm scale
2.4 GHz vs 5 GHz tradeoff
questions.explanation: 1.11.c.2 · weight high. dBm scale: -30=excellent, -50=good, -60=OK, -70=weak minimum, -80=very weak, -90=unusable. Negative, closer to 0 = stronger.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Newest. Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (Dragonfly)."
options: WPA2-Personal (PSK)
WPA2 / AES-CCMP
WPA3 / SAE
WEP
questions.explanation: 1.11.d.4 · weight high. WPA3 / SAE: Newest. Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (Dragonfly). Forward secrecy; resists offline dictionary attack.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Pre-shared key. Everyone shares one password."
options: WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X)
WPA / TKIP
WPA3 / SAE
WPA2-Personal (PSK)
questions.explanation: 1.11.d.5 · weight high. WPA2-Personal (PSK): Pre-shared key. Everyone shares one password. Home/small office.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Individual credentials via RADIUS."
options: WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X)
WPA / TKIP
WPA2 / AES-CCMP
OWE
questions.explanation: 1.11.d.6 · weight high. WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X): Individual credentials via RADIUS. Per-user keys. Required for corporate networks.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Industry-leader Type 1 hypervisor."
options: Type 1 hypervisor
Microsoft Hyper-V
Virtual switch (vSwitch)
VMware ESXi
questions.explanation: 1.12.a.2 · weight high. VMware ESXi: Industry-leader Type 1 hypervisor. Managed by vCenter Server.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Hosted hypervisor. Runs on top of a regular OS (Windows/macOS/Linux) as an application."
options: Type 1 vs Type 2 tradeoff
Type 2 hypervisor
Parallels
VirtualBox
questions.explanation: 1.12.b.1 · weight high. Type 2 hypervisor: Hosted hypervisor. Runs on top of a regular OS (Windows/macOS/Linux) as an application. Dev/test use.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Type 1 = faster, isolation, production."
options: Parallels
VMware Workstation / Fusion
VirtualBox
Type 1 vs Type 2 tradeoff
questions.explanation: 1.12.b.5 · weight high. Type 1 vs Type 2 tradeoff: Type 1 = faster, isolation, production. Type 2 = easier install, runs on any OS, dev/test.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each VM runs in own memory space with own virtual hardware."
options: Server consolidation
Virtual Machine (VM)
Live migration (vMotion)
VM isolation
questions.explanation: 1.12.c.3 · weight high. VM isolation: Each VM runs in own memory space with own virtual hardware. Crash or compromise stays contained.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "VM = heavier, stronger isolation, own OS."
options: VM isolation
VM vs container tradeoff
Live migration (vMotion)
Virtual Machine (VM)
questions.explanation: 1.12.c.5 · weight high. VM vs container tradeoff: VM = heavier, stronger isolation, own OS. Container = lightweight, shares host kernel, faster start.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Packaged application + dependencies running as an isolated process."
options: Container
Container image
Kubernetes (K8s)
Docker
questions.explanation: 1.12.d.1 · weight high. Container: Packaged application + dependencies running as an isolated process. Shares host kernel; no guest OS.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Most common container runtime."
options: Container vs VM
Docker
Container
Kubernetes (K8s)
questions.explanation: 1.12.d.2 · weight high. Docker: Most common container runtime. Defines images, runs containers, provides registry.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Container: lightweight, fast start (~seconds), shares kernel."
options: Container image
Container vs VM
Docker
Kubernetes (K8s)
questions.explanation: 1.12.d.4 · weight high. Container vs VM: Container: lightweight, fast start (~seconds), shares kernel. VM: heavy, stronger isolation, own OS.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Virtual Routing and Forwarding."
options: Overlapping IP space
VRF vs VLAN (L3 vs L2)
VRF use cases
VRF
questions.explanation: 1.12.e.1 · weight high. VRF: Virtual Routing and Forwarding. Multiple separate routing tables on one router. Enables overlapping IPs and isolated customer traffic.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Switch learns from the SOURCE MAC of arriving frames — NEVER the destination."
options: Dynamic entry
MAC learning (source MAC)
show mac address-table
Device moves ports
questions.explanation: 1.13.a.1 · weight high. MAC learning (source MAC): Switch learns from the SOURCE MAC of arriving frames — NEVER the destination. Maps MAC → ingress port.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Switch updates the MAC table entry to the new port on the next frame from that MAC."
options: Device moves ports
MAC flooding attack
Dynamic entry
show mac address-table
questions.explanation: 1.13.a.3 · weight high. Device moves ports: Switch updates the MAC table entry to the new port on the next frame from that MAC.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Forward (known unicast → send out specific port), Flood (unknown → all ports except source), Filter (same source and dest port → drop)."
options: Known unicast forwarding
Fragment-Free
Three switching actions (FFF)
Store-and-Forward
questions.explanation: 1.13.b.1 · weight high. Three switching actions (FFF): Forward (known unicast → send out specific port), Flood (unknown → all ports except source), Filter (same source and dest port → drop).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Receive entire frame, CRC check, then forward."
options: Three switching actions (FFF)
Store-and-Forward
Fragment-Free
Cut-Through
questions.explanation: 1.13.b.2 · weight high. Store-and-Forward: Receive entire frame, CRC check, then forward. Default on modern Catalyst. Drops corrupted frames.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Read first 6 bytes (dest MAC) then forward immediately."
options: Store-and-Forward
Fragment-Free
Known unicast forwarding
Cut-Through
questions.explanation: 1.13.b.3 · weight high. Cut-Through: Read first 6 bytes (dest MAC) then forward immediately. Lowest latency. No CRC check.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Destination MAC IS in the table → send frame ONLY out that one egress port."
options: Three switching actions (FFF)
Store-and-Forward
Known unicast forwarding
Fragment-Free
questions.explanation: 1.13.b.5 · weight high. Known unicast forwarding: Destination MAC IS in the table → send frame ONLY out that one egress port. Most efficient case.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Unknown unicast, Broadcast, Multicast."
options: VLAN = flood boundary
Unknown unicast flooding
Broadcast (FFFF.FFFF.FFFF)
Three flood triggers (UBM)
questions.explanation: 1.13.c.1 · weight high. Three flood triggers (UBM): Unknown unicast, Broadcast, Multicast. These three frame types are flooded by default.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Destination MAC NOT in table → flood to all ports in VLAN except source."
options: VLAN = flood boundary
Three flood triggers (UBM)
Multicast flooding
Unknown unicast flooding
questions.explanation: 1.13.c.2 · weight high. Unknown unicast flooding: Destination MAC NOT in table → flood to all ports in VLAN except source. Resolves once MAC is learned.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Always flooded to all ports in VLAN."
options: Broadcast (FFFF.FFFF.FFFF)
Three flood triggers (UBM)
Unknown unicast flooding
VLAN = flood boundary
questions.explanation: 1.13.c.3 · weight high. Broadcast (FFFF.FFFF.FFFF): Always flooded to all ports in VLAN. ARP requests, DHCP Discover, NetBIOS.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "By default flooded like broadcast."
options: VLAN = flood boundary
Unknown unicast flooding
Multicast flooding
Three flood triggers (UBM)
questions.explanation: 1.13.c.4 · weight high. Multicast flooding: By default flooded like broadcast. IGMP snooping lets switch forward only to interested ports.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Floods never cross VLAN boundaries without a router."
options: Three flood triggers (UBM)
Multicast flooding
Unknown unicast flooding
VLAN = flood boundary
questions.explanation: 1.13.c.5 · weight high. VLAN = flood boundary: Floods never cross VLAN boundaries without a router. Primary reason VLANs exist.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "VLAN ID, MAC Address, Type (DYNAMIC/STATIC), Port."
options: Default aging time
Entry fields
Static MAC entry
MAC address table / CAM table
questions.explanation: 1.13.d.2 · weight high. Entry fields: VLAN ID, MAC Address, Type (DYNAMIC/STATIC), Port. MAC is VLAN-scoped.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "300 seconds (5 min). Dynamic entries expire if no frame from that MAC within the window."
options: Entry fields
Static MAC entry
Default aging time
clear mac address-table dynamic
questions.explanation: 1.13.d.3 · weight high. Default aging time: 300 seconds (5 min). Dynamic entries expire if no frame from that MAC within the window.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Switch port carrying traffic for a SINGLE VLAN."
options: Access port
switchport access vlan [id]
show interfaces switchport
switchport mode access
questions.explanation: 2.1.a.1 · weight high. Access port: Switch port carrying traffic for a SINGLE VLAN. Strips tag on egress, tags on ingress internally.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Explicitly set port to access mode."
options: show vlan brief
switchport mode access
Voice VLAN
VLAN range
questions.explanation: 2.1.a.2 · weight high. switchport mode access: Explicitly set port to access mode. Prevents DTP trunk negotiation.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Assigns the access port to a specific VLAN."
options: show vlan brief
show interfaces switchport
switchport mode access
switchport access vlan [id]
questions.explanation: 2.1.a.3 · weight high. switchport access vlan [id]: Assigns the access port to a specific VLAN. Auto-creates the VLAN if it doesn't exist.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Second VLAN on access port for IP phone (tagged)."
options: Voice VLAN
switchport mode access
Access port
show interfaces switchport
questions.explanation: 2.1.a.4 · weight high. Voice VLAN: Second VLAN on access port for IP phone (tagged). Data VLAN stays untagged for PC. Command: switchport voice vlan [id].

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "1-4094 (12-bit). VLANs 1002-1005 reserved for Token Ring/FDDI legacy."
options: show interfaces switchport
switchport mode access
VLAN range
Access port
questions.explanation: 2.1.a.5 · weight high. VLAN range: 1-4094 (12-bit). VLANs 1002-1005 reserved for Token Ring/FDDI legacy.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Shows VLANs and their access ports."
options: switchport access vlan [id]
Access port
VLAN range
show vlan brief
questions.explanation: 2.1.a.6 · weight high. show vlan brief: Shows VLANs and their access ports. IMPORTANT: trunk ports do NOT appear in this output.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Per-port details: access VLAN, voice VLAN, mode, trunk settings."
options: VLAN range
Voice VLAN
show vlan brief
show interfaces switchport
questions.explanation: 2.1.a.7 · weight high. show interfaces switchport: Per-port details: access VLAN, voice VLAN, mode, trunk settings.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "All ports start in VLAN 1 out of the box."
options: Parking-lot VLAN
Management VLAN
VLAN 1 default
Shut down unused ports
questions.explanation: 2.1.b.1 · weight high. VLAN 1 default: All ports start in VLAN 1 out of the box. VLAN 1 is also the default native VLAN on trunks.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Hardcoded for CDP, VTP, STP, PAgP."
options: Shut down unused ports
VLAN 1 cannot be deleted
Parking-lot VLAN
Management VLAN
questions.explanation: 2.1.b.2 · weight high. VLAN 1 cannot be deleted: Hardcoded for CDP, VTP, STP, PAgP. 'no vlan 1' produces an error.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Dedicated VLAN for switch SVI and remote management."
options: Management VLAN
Shut down unused ports
Parking-lot VLAN
VLAN 1 default
questions.explanation: 2.1.b.3 · weight high. Management VLAN: Dedicated VLAN for switch SVI and remote management. Best practice: separate from user VLANs (e.g., VLAN 99).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Best practice. Prevents rogue devices from getting connectivity."
options: Management VLAN
Parking-lot VLAN
VLAN 1 default
Shut down unused ports
questions.explanation: 2.1.b.5 · weight high. Shut down unused ports: Best practice. Prevents rogue devices from getting connectivity. Pair with parking-lot VLAN.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "One router interface + trunk to switch + subinterfaces per VLAN."
options: L3 switch SVI method
Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS)
Inter-VLAN routing (IVR)
'ip routing' command
questions.explanation: 2.1.c.2 · weight high. Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS): One router interface + trunk to switch + subinterfaces per VLAN. Each subif has 'encapsulation dot1q [vlan]'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Required on router subinterface for ROAS."
options: 'ip routing' command
encapsulation dot1q [vlan]
Inter-VLAN routing (IVR)
Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS)
questions.explanation: 2.1.c.3 · weight high. encapsulation dot1q [vlan]: Required on router subinterface for ROAS. Tags traffic with the VLAN ID.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "SVIs (interface vlan [id]) + 'ip routing' globally."
options: L3 switch SVI method
Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS)
Inter-VLAN routing (IVR)
'ip routing' command
questions.explanation: 2.1.c.4 · weight high. L3 switch SVI method: SVIs (interface vlan [id]) + 'ip routing' globally. Hardware ASIC routing = wire speed. Preferred for campus.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "GLOBAL command. Required on L3 switch to actually route between SVIs."
options: 'ip routing' command
Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS)
Inter-VLAN routing (IVR)
encapsulation dot1q [vlan]
questions.explanation: 2.1.c.5 · weight high. 'ip routing' command: GLOBAL command. Required on L3 switch to actually route between SVIs. Forgotten = no inter-VLAN connectivity.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "ROAS = router CPU, any router + L2 switch works, slower."
options: ROAS vs L3 switch
Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS)
encapsulation dot1q [vlan]
L3 switch SVI method
questions.explanation: 2.1.c.6 · weight high. ROAS vs L3 switch: ROAS = router CPU, any router + L2 switch works, slower. L3 switch = ASIC-accelerated, requires L3-capable switch, faster.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Switch port carrying multiple VLANs."
options: show interfaces trunk
DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol)
Trunk port
switchport trunk allowed vlan
questions.explanation: 2.2.a.1 · weight high. Trunk port: Switch port carrying multiple VLANs. Uses 802.1Q tagging to distinguish. Used between switches, to routers (ROAS), to WLCs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Restrict which VLANs cross the trunk."
options: Trunk port
show interfaces trunk
switchport trunk allowed vlan
DTP auto-auto = access
questions.explanation: 2.2.a.3 · weight high. switchport trunk allowed vlan: Restrict which VLANs cross the trunk. Best practice: only allow needed VLANs. Add/remove keywords support incremental changes.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Cisco proprietary. Auto-negotiates trunks."
options: show interfaces trunk
switchport mode trunk
DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol)
switchport trunk allowed vlan
questions.explanation: 2.2.a.4 · weight high. DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol): Cisco proprietary. Auto-negotiates trunks. Security risk — disable with 'switchport nonegotiate'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "dynamic desirable (actively tries), dynamic auto (waits), trunk (forced but still talks DTP), nonegotiate (silent)."
options: switchport trunk allowed vlan
DTP modes
switchport mode trunk
DTP auto-auto = access
questions.explanation: 2.2.a.5 · weight high. DTP modes: dynamic desirable (actively tries), dynamic auto (waits), trunk (forced but still talks DTP), nonegotiate (silent).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "If BOTH sides are 'dynamic auto', no trunk forms."
options: DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol)
DTP auto-auto = access
show interfaces trunk
DTP modes
questions.explanation: 2.2.a.6 · weight high. DTP auto-auto = access: If BOTH sides are 'dynamic auto', no trunk forms. Both remain access. Classic exam trap.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Shows all trunk ports: mode, encapsulation, native VLAN, allowed/active VLANs."
options: show interfaces trunk
DTP auto-auto = access
switchport mode trunk
switchport trunk allowed vlan
questions.explanation: 2.2.a.7 · weight high. show interfaces trunk: Shows all trunk ports: mode, encapsulation, native VLAN, allowed/active VLANs. Trunks don't appear in 'show vlan brief'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "IEEE standard for VLAN tagging on trunks."
options: VLAN ID field size
802.1Q tag size
ISL
questions.explanation: 2.2.b.1 · weight high. 802.1Q (dot1q): IEEE standard for VLAN tagging on trunks. Open standard. Only trunking encap on modern Cisco switches.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "4 bytes inserted between Source MAC and EtherType fields."
options: PCP / CoS
Max frame with tag
802.1Q tag size
questions.explanation: 2.2.b.2 · weight high. 802.1Q tag size: 4 bytes inserted between Source MAC and EtherType fields.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "12 bits. Range 0-4095, usable 1-4094 (0 and 4095 reserved)."
options: VLAN ID field size
802.1Q tag size
ISL
Max frame with tag
questions.explanation: 2.2.b.3 · weight high. VLAN ID field size: 12 bits. Range 0-4095, usable 1-4094 (0 and 4095 reserved). Hence 4094 possible VLANs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "1522 bytes (1518 standard + 4-byte tag)."
options: PCP / CoS
Max frame with tag
ISL
802.1Q tag size
questions.explanation: 2.2.b.4 · weight high. Max frame with tag: 1522 bytes (1518 standard + 4-byte tag). NOT a giant on a trunk port.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "VLAN whose traffic is sent UNTAGGED across the trunk."
options: CDP native VLAN mismatch
Native VLAN
switchport trunk native vlan
Native VLAN must match
questions.explanation: 2.2.c.1 · weight high. Native VLAN: VLAN whose traffic is sent UNTAGGED across the trunk. Default: VLAN 1.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Both ends of a trunk must agree on native VLAN."
options: CDP native VLAN mismatch
Native VLAN must match
Double-tagging VLAN hop
Native VLAN
questions.explanation: 2.2.c.2 · weight high. Native VLAN must match: Both ends of a trunk must agree on native VLAN. Mismatch → traffic leaks between VLANs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Command to change the native VLAN on a trunk."
options: vlan dot1q tag native
Double-tagging VLAN hop
switchport trunk native vlan
Native VLAN must match
questions.explanation: 2.2.c.3 · weight high. switchport trunk native vlan: Command to change the native VLAN on a trunk. Best practice: unused VLAN (e.g., 999).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "CDP detects mismatch and logs '%CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH'."
options: Double-tagging VLAN hop
switchport trunk native vlan
CDP native VLAN mismatch
Native VLAN must match
questions.explanation: 2.2.c.4 · weight high. CDP native VLAN mismatch: CDP detects mismatch and logs '%CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH'. Common troubleshooting clue.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Attack: attacker sends frame with two 802.1Q tags."
options: Double-tagging VLAN hop
Native VLAN
Native VLAN must match
vlan dot1q tag native
questions.explanation: 2.2.c.5 · weight high. Double-tagging VLAN hop: Attack: attacker sends frame with two 802.1Q tags. First switch strips outer (matches native), second switch forwards to inner-tagged VLAN.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Default ON on all Cisco routers, switches, firewalls, phones, APs."
options: show cdp neighbors [detail]
CDP enabled by default
CDP one-hop only
CDP info carried
questions.explanation: 2.3.a.2 · weight high. CDP enabled by default: Default ON on all Cisco routers, switches, firewalls, phones, APs. Unlike LLDP which is OFF.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Device ID (hostname), IP, platform, capabilities, local interface, port ID, native VLAN, duplex, VTP domain."
options: CDP timers
CDP one-hop only
CDP info carried
show cdp neighbors [detail]
questions.explanation: 2.3.a.4 · weight high. CDP info carried: Device ID (hostname), IP, platform, capabilities, local interface, port ID, native VLAN, duplex, VTP domain.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Only discovers DIRECTLY connected neighbors."
options: Disable CDP per interface
CDP
CDP enabled by default
CDP one-hop only
questions.explanation: 2.3.a.5 · weight high. CDP one-hop only: Only discovers DIRECTLY connected neighbors. Unidirectional — each device sends its own info.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'no cdp enable' under interface."
options: show cdp neighbors [detail]
Disable CDP per interface
CDP info carried
CDP
questions.explanation: 2.3.a.6 · weight high. Disable CDP per interface: 'no cdp enable' under interface. Best practice on untrusted/internet-facing interfaces.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Lists neighbors. 'detail' adds IP address and IOS version."
options: CDP enabled by default
CDP info carried
CDP timers
show cdp neighbors [detail]
questions.explanation: 2.3.a.7 · weight high. show cdp neighbors [detail]: Lists neighbors. 'detail' adds IP address and IOS version.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Device ID, Local Intrfce, Holdtme, Capability, Platform, Port ID."
options: show cdp interface
show cdp neighbors detail
show cdp neighbors columns
Capability codes
questions.explanation: 2.3.b.1 · weight high. show cdp neighbors columns: Device ID, Local Intrfce, Holdtme, Capability, Platform, Port ID.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "R=Router, S=Switch, T=Trans Bridge, B=Source Route Bridge, H=Host, I=IGMP, r=Repeater."
options: Capability codes
show cdp entry [device-id]
show cdp neighbors detail
show cdp interface
questions.explanation: 2.3.b.2 · weight high. Capability codes: R=Router, S=Switch, T=Trans Bridge, B=Source Route Bridge, H=Host, I=IGMP, r=Repeater.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Adds: management IP, IOS version, VTP domain, native VLAN, duplex."
options: Capability codes
show cdp entry [device-id]
show cdp neighbors columns
show cdp neighbors detail
questions.explanation: 2.3.b.3 · weight high. show cdp neighbors detail: Adds: management IP, IOS version, VTP domain, native VLAN, duplex. 'detail' = IP address.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "GLOBAL command. Enable CDP device-wide."
options: CDP security leaks
cdp enable
cdp timer [seconds]
cdp run
questions.explanation: 2.3.c.1 · weight high. cdp run: GLOBAL command. Enable CDP device-wide. Default state. 'no cdp run' disables globally.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "INTERFACE command. Enable CDP on a specific port."
options: cdp run
CDP security leaks
cdp enable
cdp timer [seconds]
questions.explanation: 2.3.c.2 · weight high. cdp enable: INTERFACE command. Enable CDP on a specific port. 'no cdp enable' disables on that port only.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Hostname, IP, platform, IOS version."
options: cdp holdtime [seconds]
cdp run
cdp enable
CDP security leaks
questions.explanation: 2.3.c.5 · weight high. CDP security leaks: Hostname, IP, platform, IOS version. Attacker can identify vulnerabilities from 60s of passive listening.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Link Layer Discovery Protocol."
options: Separate TX/RX per port
LLDP timers
LLDP
LLDP TLVs
questions.explanation: 2.3.d.1 · weight high. LLDP: Link Layer Discovery Protocol. IEEE 802.1AB. Open-standard, vendor-neutral equivalent of CDP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "OPPOSITE of CDP. Must run 'lldp run' globally to enable."
options: LLDP disabled by default
LLDP
LLDP TLVs
LLDP timers
questions.explanation: 2.3.d.2 · weight high. LLDP disabled by default: OPPOSITE of CDP. Must run 'lldp run' globally to enable.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "30s advertisement, 120s holdtime."
options: Separate TX/RX per port
LLDP
LLDP TLVs
LLDP timers
questions.explanation: 2.3.d.3 · weight high. LLDP timers: 30s advertisement, 120s holdtime. Faster than CDP's 60/180.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "LLDP unique: 'lldp transmit' and 'lldp receive' independent."
options: LLDP timers
LLDP TLVs
Separate TX/RX per port
LLDP disabled by default
questions.explanation: 2.3.d.5 · weight high. Separate TX/RX per port: LLDP unique: 'lldp transmit' and 'lldp receive' independent. CDP has single toggle.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Adds Management Address (IP), System Description, Enabled Capabilities, Port Description."
options: show lldp neighbors
show lldp interface
show lldp neighbors detail
LLDP capability codes
questions.explanation: 2.3.e.2 · weight high. show lldp neighbors detail: Adds Management Address (IP), System Description, Enabled Capabilities, Port Description.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "GLOBAL command. Enable LLDP device-wide."
options: LLDP timer / holdtime / reinit
lldp receive
lldp run
lldp transmit
questions.explanation: 2.3.f.1 · weight high. lldp run: GLOBAL command. Enable LLDP device-wide. Required because default is OFF.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "INTERFACE command. Send LLDP advertisements from this port."
options: Receive-only port
LLDP timer / holdtime / reinit
lldp transmit
lldp receive
questions.explanation: 2.3.f.2 · weight high. lldp transmit: INTERFACE command. Send LLDP advertisements from this port. Independent from receive.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "INTERFACE command. Process incoming LLDP frames."
options: LLDP timer / holdtime / reinit
lldp run
Receive-only port
lldp receive
questions.explanation: 2.3.f.3 · weight high. lldp receive: INTERFACE command. Process incoming LLDP frames. Independent from transmit.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "CDP: 60s/180s. LLDP: 30s/120s."
options: Both can run together
Standard: CDP vs LLDP
Default state
Timer comparison
questions.explanation: 2.3.g.3 · weight high. Timer comparison: CDP: 60s/180s. LLDP: 30s/120s. LLDP faster.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "CDP and LLDP can be simultaneously enabled on the same device."
options: Default state
Interface control
Both can run together
Timer comparison
questions.explanation: 2.3.g.5 · weight high. Both can run together: CDP and LLDP can be simultaneously enabled on the same device. Common in mixed-vendor DCs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Bundle of 2-8 physical links between switches into one logical link."
options: STP + EtherChannel
EtherChannel / Port-Channel
Three EtherChannel protocols
Load balancing
questions.explanation: 2.4.a.1 · weight high. EtherChannel / Port-Channel: Bundle of 2-8 physical links between switches into one logical link. Aggregates bandwidth + provides redundancy.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "STP treats port-channel as ONE link."
options: Load balancing
Three EtherChannel protocols
Member port matching
STP + EtherChannel
questions.explanation: 2.4.a.2 · weight high. STP + EtherChannel: STP treats port-channel as ONE link. All member links forward simultaneously — none blocked.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "All member ports MUST have identical speed, duplex, VLAN, trunk config, STP."
options: STP + EtherChannel
EtherChannel / Port-Channel
interface Port-channel N
Member port matching
questions.explanation: 2.4.a.3 · weight high. Member port matching: All member ports MUST have identical speed, duplex, VLAN, trunk config, STP. Mismatch = port suspended.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "LACP (IEEE 802.3ad, recommended), PAgP (Cisco), static 'on' (no negotiation, risky)."
options: Member port matching
STP + EtherChannel
Load balancing
Three EtherChannel protocols
questions.explanation: 2.4.a.5 · weight high. Three EtherChannel protocols: LACP (IEEE 802.3ad, recommended), PAgP (Cisco), static 'on' (no negotiation, risky).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Logical interface created after 'channel-group'."
options: Three EtherChannel protocols
STP + EtherChannel
interface Port-channel N
EtherChannel / Port-Channel
questions.explanation: 2.4.a.6 · weight high. interface Port-channel N: Logical interface created after 'channel-group'. Configure VLANs/trunk settings HERE, pushed to members.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Link Aggregation Control Protocol."
options: LACP (IEEE 802.3ad)
LACP max active ports
LACP active mode
LACP passive mode
questions.explanation: 2.4.b.1 · weight high. LACP (IEEE 802.3ad): Link Aggregation Control Protocol. Open standard. Recommended over PAgP. Works multi-vendor.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Actively sends LACPDUs to initiate channel."
options: Active + Passive = YES
LACP passive mode
LACP active mode
LACP (IEEE 802.3ad)
questions.explanation: 2.4.b.2 · weight high. LACP active mode: Actively sends LACPDUs to initiate channel. Command: 'channel-group N mode active'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Only responds to received LACP frames."
options: LACP max active ports
Active + Passive = YES
LACP passive mode
LACP (IEEE 802.3ad)
questions.explanation: 2.4.b.3 · weight high. LACP passive mode: Only responds to received LACP frames. Command: 'channel-group N mode passive'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Both sides initiate. Channel forms."
options: Active + Active = YES
Active + Passive = YES
LACP active mode
LACP passive mode
questions.explanation: 2.4.b.4 · weight high. Active + Active = YES: Both sides initiate. Channel forms.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Neither initiates — both wait forever."
options: Active + Passive = YES
Passive + Passive = NO
Active + Active = YES
LACP max active ports
questions.explanation: 2.4.b.6 · weight high. Passive + Passive = NO: Neither initiates — both wait forever. Channel does NOT form. Exam trap.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Channel forms. At least one side must be desirable."
options: Mode mapping LACP ↔ PAgP
Desirable + Desirable / Auto = YES
PAgP
Auto + Auto = NO
questions.explanation: 2.4.c.4 · weight high. Desirable + Desirable / Auto = YES: Channel forms. At least one side must be desirable.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Neither initiates. Channel never forms."
options: Auto + Auto = NO
Desirable + Desirable / Auto = YES
PAgP desirable mode
PAgP
questions.explanation: 2.4.c.5 · weight high. Auto + Auto = NO: Neither initiates. Channel never forms. Exam trap paralleling passive+passive.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "active=desirable, passive=auto."
options: Desirable + Desirable / Auto = YES
PAgP desirable mode
Mode mapping LACP ↔ PAgP
PAgP auto mode
questions.explanation: 2.4.c.6 · weight high. Mode mapping LACP ↔ PAgP: active=desirable, passive=auto. Protocols CANNOT be mixed (LACP with PAgP = no channel).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "No negotiation. Channel forms based purely on local config."
options: Static EtherChannel (mode on)
Mode on risk
Mode on incompatibility
When to use on
questions.explanation: 2.4.d.1 · weight high. Static EtherChannel (mode on): No negotiation. Channel forms based purely on local config. No error detection.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Can cause L2 loops if misconfigured."
options: Static EtherChannel (mode on)
Mode on risk
When to use on
Mode on incompatibility
questions.explanation: 2.4.d.2 · weight high. Mode on risk: Can cause L2 loops if misconfigured. Best practice: use LACP instead.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Cannot pair with active/passive/desirable/auto."
options: Static EtherChannel (mode on)
When to use on
Mode on risk
Mode on incompatibility
questions.explanation: 2.4.d.3 · weight high. Mode on incompatibility: Cannot pair with active/passive/desirable/auto. ONLY on+on works.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "active+active, active+passive, desirable+desirable, desirable+auto, on+on."
options: Never mix protocols
At-least-one-initiator rule
Negotiation matrix — YES
Negotiation matrix — NO
questions.explanation: 2.4.e.1 · weight high. Negotiation matrix — YES: active+active, active+passive, desirable+desirable, desirable+auto, on+on.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "passive+passive, auto+auto, on+active/passive/desirable/auto, any LACP+PAgP combo."
options: Negotiation matrix — YES
Negotiation matrix — NO
At-least-one-initiator rule
Never mix protocols
questions.explanation: 2.4.e.2 · weight high. Negotiation matrix — NO: passive+passive, auto+auto, on+active/passive/desirable/auto, any LACP+PAgP combo.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each protocol needs an initiator (active or desirable)."
options: Never mix protocols
Negotiation matrix — NO
At-least-one-initiator rule
Negotiation matrix — YES
questions.explanation: 2.4.e.3 · weight high. At-least-one-initiator rule: Each protocol needs an initiator (active or desirable). Two responders wait forever.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Bundles switchport-configured interfaces (access or trunk)."
options: show etherchannel summary
Layer 2 EtherChannel
Flag 's' (suspended)
Configure on port-channel
questions.explanation: 2.4.f.1 · weight high. Layer 2 EtherChannel: Bundles switchport-configured interfaces (access or trunk). Port-channel inherits VLAN/trunk config.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "After creation, set trunk/VLAN on 'interface Port-channel N'."
options: Flag 's' (suspended)
Flag 'I' (standalone)
Configure on port-channel
show etherchannel summary
questions.explanation: 2.4.f.2 · weight high. Configure on port-channel: After creation, set trunk/VLAN on 'interface Port-channel N'. Config pushes to members automatically.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Member port config does not match others."
options: Flag 's' (suspended)
Configure on port-channel
Flag 'I' (standalone)
Layer 2 EtherChannel
questions.explanation: 2.4.f.4 · weight high. Flag 's' (suspended): Member port config does not match others. Check VLAN/trunk settings for the mismatch.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Port-channel acts as routed interface with its own IP."
options: Layer 3 EtherChannel
'no switchport' prereq
RU flag
IP on port-channel
questions.explanation: 2.4.g.1 · weight high. Layer 3 EtherChannel: Port-channel acts as routed interface with its own IP. Used for router↔L3 switch or L3↔L3 switch links.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Required on each member port to convert to routed port before channel-group assignment."
options: RU flag
IP on port-channel
Layer 3 EtherChannel
'no switchport' prereq
questions.explanation: 2.4.g.2 · weight high. 'no switchport' prereq: Required on each member port to convert to routed port before channel-group assignment.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Assign IP address on 'interface Port-channel N'."
options: Layer 3 EtherChannel
IP on port-channel
'no switchport' prereq
RU flag
questions.explanation: 2.4.g.3 · weight high. IP on port-channel: Assign IP address on 'interface Port-channel N'. NEVER on individual member ports.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'Routed, in Use' in show etherchannel summary."
options: Layer 3 EtherChannel
IP on port-channel
RU flag
'no switchport' prereq
questions.explanation: 2.4.g.4 · weight high. RU flag: 'Routed, in Use' in show etherchannel summary. Indicates healthy L3 port-channel.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "All members identical: Speed, Duplex, VLAN, Trunk config, STP settings."
options: SDVTS (member match rules)
EtherChannel / Port-Channel
Port suspension recovery
Best-practice config order
questions.explanation: 2.4.h.1 · weight high. SDVTS (member match rules): All members identical: Speed, Duplex, VLAN, Trunk config, STP settings. Any mismatch = suspended.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Fix the mismatch (align config with other members)."
options: RU flag
Port suspension recovery
Best-practice config order
SDVTS (member match rules)
questions.explanation: 2.4.h.2 · weight high. Port suspension recovery: Fix the mismatch (align config with other members). Port re-bundles automatically. No shut/no-shut needed.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Member port actively bundled and forwarding."
options: Channel flags SU / RU
Port flag H (hot-standby)
Port flag I (standalone)
Port flag P (bundled)
questions.explanation: 2.4.i.3 · weight high. Port flag P (bundled): Member port actively bundled and forwarding. The healthy per-port state.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Config mismatch with other members."
options: Port flag P (bundled)
Port flag s (suspended)
Channel flags SU / RU
Channel flags SD / RD
questions.explanation: 2.4.i.4 · weight high. Port flag s (suspended): Config mismatch with other members. Fix mismatch to re-bundle.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Individual — port not bundled."
options: Port flag P (bundled)
Port flag H (hot-standby)
Port flag s (suspended)
Port flag I (standalone)
questions.explanation: 2.4.i.5 · weight high. Port flag I (standalone): Individual — port not bundled. Mode incompatibility (e.g., passive+passive, or on+active).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Switch with the lowest Bridge ID."
options: Alternate Port (RSTP)
Backup Port (RSTP)
Root Bridge
Root Port (RP)
questions.explanation: 2.5.a.1 · weight high. Root Bridge: Switch with the lowest Bridge ID. Reference point for the entire STP topology.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Backup root port. Blocked/discarding."
options: Alternate Port (RSTP)
Backup Port (RSTP)
Root Bridge
Root Port (RP)
questions.explanation: 2.5.a.4 · weight high. Alternate Port (RSTP): Backup root port. Blocked/discarding. Rapidly transitions to forwarding if root port fails.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "All root-bridge ports are designated."
options: Root bridge has no RP
Root Bridge
Backup Port (RSTP)
Alternate Port (RSTP)
questions.explanation: 2.5.a.6 · weight high. Root bridge has no RP: All root-bridge ports are designated. The root doesn't need to reach itself.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Rapid Spanning Tree. Converges in seconds using proposal/agreement."
options: RSTP vs classic STP timing
Rapid PVST+
RSTP (802.1w)
Forwarding state
questions.explanation: 2.5.b.1 · weight high. RSTP (802.1w): Rapid Spanning Tree. Converges in seconds using proposal/agreement. Three port states.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Populating MAC table but not yet forwarding data frames."
options: Forwarding state
RSTP vs classic STP timing
Learning state
Proposal/agreement
questions.explanation: 2.5.b.3 · weight high. Learning state: Populating MAC table but not yet forwarding data frames.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Full operation: forwards frames AND learns MACs."
options: Forwarding state
Learning state
Proposal/agreement
RSTP (802.1w)
questions.explanation: 2.5.b.4 · weight high. Forwarding state: Full operation: forwards frames AND learns MACs. Target state for RP and DP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Cisco default. Runs a separate RSTP instance per VLAN."
options: Discarding state
Learning state
Rapid PVST+
RSTP (802.1w)
questions.explanation: 2.5.b.6 · weight high. Rapid PVST+: Cisco default. Runs a separate RSTP instance per VLAN. Combines RSTP speed with per-VLAN flexibility.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Port transitions immediately from discarding to forwarding."
options: BPDU Guard
err-disabled recovery
PortFast
spanning-tree portfast
questions.explanation: 2.5.c.1 · weight high. PortFast: Port transitions immediately from discarding to forwarding. ACCESS PORTS to endpoints only.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Interface command. 'spanning-tree portfast default' = global for all access ports."
options: Never PortFast switch-to-switch
err-disabled recovery
spanning-tree portfast
BPDU Guard
questions.explanation: 2.5.c.2 · weight high. spanning-tree portfast: Interface command. 'spanning-tree portfast default' = global for all access ports.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Creates instant L2 loop before STP can block."
options: Never PortFast switch-to-switch
err-disabled recovery
PortFast
spanning-tree portfast
questions.explanation: 2.5.c.3 · weight high. Never PortFast switch-to-switch: Creates instant L2 loop before STP can block. Only endpoints (PCs, printers, phones).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Pairs with PortFast. If ANY BPDU arrives → port err-disabled."
options: spanning-tree portfast
BPDU Guard
PortFast
err-disabled recovery
questions.explanation: 2.5.c.4 · weight high. BPDU Guard: Pairs with PortFast. If ANY BPDU arrives → port err-disabled. Command: 'spanning-tree bpduguard enable'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Prevents port from becoming a root port."
options: Root Guard recovery
spanning-tree guard root
Root Guard
Root-inconsistent state
questions.explanation: 2.5.d.1 · weight high. Root Guard: Prevents port from becoming a root port. Superior BPDU received → root-inconsistent (blocked).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Root Guard = superior BPDU only → blocked (auto-recover)."
options: Root Guard
Root-inconsistent state
Root Guard vs BPDU Guard
Root Guard recovery
questions.explanation: 2.5.d.4 · weight high. Root Guard vs BPDU Guard: Root Guard = superior BPDU only → blocked (auto-recover). BPDU Guard = ANY BPDU → err-disabled (manual recover).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "8 bytes: 4-bit priority + 12-bit Extended System ID (VLAN) + 48-bit MAC."
options: Priority must be multiple of 4096
root primary / root secondary
Change root command
Bridge ID
questions.explanation: 2.5.e.1 · weight high. Bridge ID: 8 bytes: 4-bit priority + 12-bit Extended System ID (VLAN) + 48-bit MAC. Determines root election.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "32768. Applies per-VLAN (actual value = 32768 + VLAN ID due to Extended System ID)."
options: root primary / root secondary
Election: lowest priority wins
Default priority
Priority must be multiple of 4096
questions.explanation: 2.5.e.2 · weight high. Default priority: 32768. Applies per-VLAN (actual value = 32768 + VLAN ID due to Extended System ID).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Valid: 0, 4096, 8192, 12288, ..., 61440."
options: root primary / root secondary
Priority must be multiple of 4096
Default priority
Change root command
questions.explanation: 2.5.e.3 · weight high. Priority must be multiple of 4096: Valid: 0, 4096, 8192, 12288, ..., 61440. Extended System ID uses the other 12 bits.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Macros. 'primary' = 24576 (or 4096 below current root)."
options: Bridge ID
root primary / root secondary
Default priority
Change root command
questions.explanation: 2.5.e.5 · weight high. root primary / root secondary: Macros. 'primary' = 24576 (or 4096 below current root). 'secondary' = 28672.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'spanning-tree vlan [id] priority [value]' OR 'spanning-tree vlan [id] root primary'."
options: Bridge ID
Election: lowest priority wins
Change root command
Default priority
questions.explanation: 2.5.e.6 · weight high. Change root command: 'spanning-tree vlan [id] priority [value]' OR 'spanning-tree vlan [id] root primary'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Sum of STP costs along the path to the root bridge."
options: STP cost table (short)
spanning-tree cost [value]
Total path cost
Root port selection tiebreakers
questions.explanation: 2.5.f.2 · weight high. Total path cost: Sum of STP costs along the path to the root bridge. Lowest total wins as root port.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "(1) lowest neighbor Bridge ID, (2) lowest neighbor port priority (default 128), (3) lowest neighbor port number."
options: spanning-tree cost [value]
STP cost table (short)
Root port selection tiebreakers
Total path cost
questions.explanation: 2.5.f.3 · weight high. Root port selection tiebreakers: (1) lowest neighbor Bridge ID, (2) lowest neighbor port priority (default 128), (3) lowest neighbor port number.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Primary STP verification command."
options: show spanning-tree summary
show spanning-tree
Type column
'This bridge is the root'
questions.explanation: 2.5.g.1 · weight high. show spanning-tree: Primary STP verification command. Shows root bridge info, this switch's Bridge ID, and per-port roles and states.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Text that appears in output when the switch you're on is the root bridge."
options: Type column
Port role abbreviations
'This bridge is the root'
show spanning-tree
questions.explanation: 2.5.g.2 · weight high. 'This bridge is the root': Text that appears in output when the switch you're on is the root bridge.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Root (RP), Desg (DP), Altn (alternate/blocked), Back (backup)."
options: 'This bridge is the root'
show spanning-tree
Port role abbreviations
Type column
questions.explanation: 2.5.g.3 · weight high. Port role abbreviations: Root (RP), Desg (DP), Altn (alternate/blocked), Back (backup).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "FWD = Forwarding, LRN = Learning, BLK = Blocking/Discarding."
options: Type column
Port state abbreviations
show spanning-tree
Port role abbreviations
questions.explanation: 2.5.g.4 · weight high. Port state abbreviations: FWD = Forwarding, LRN = Learning, BLK = Blocking/Discarding.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Standalone AP running its own IOS."
options: Autonomous AP
No WLC / no CAPWAP
Autonomous scale limit
Autonomous trunk required
questions.explanation: 2.6.a.1 · weight high. Autonomous AP: Standalone AP running its own IOS. All wireless functions handled locally. Configured per-device.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Switch port must be a trunk — AP handles multiple VLANs locally (one per SSID)."
options: Autonomous trunk required
No WLC / no CAPWAP
Autonomous AP
Autonomous scale limit
questions.explanation: 2.6.a.2 · weight high. Autonomous trunk required: Switch port must be a trunk — AP handles multiple VLANs locally (one per SSID).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Autonomous APs operate without a controller."
options: No WLC / no CAPWAP
Autonomous AP
Autonomous scale limit
Autonomous trunk required
questions.explanation: 2.6.a.4 · weight high. No WLC / no CAPWAP: Autonomous APs operate without a controller. No CAPWAP tunnels. All intelligence local.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Thin AP managed by WLC via CAPWAP."
options: CAPWAP
Lightweight AP
CAPWAP control UDP 5246
Lightweight access port
questions.explanation: 2.6.b.1 · weight high. Lightweight AP: Thin AP managed by WLC via CAPWAP. Only handles real-time RF. All policy and management on WLC.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points (RFC 5415)."
options: CAPWAP
Lightweight access port
Lightweight AP
WLC discovery methods
