questions.explanation: 2.6.b.2 · weight high. CAPWAP: Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points (RFC 5415). Tunnels management + data between AP and WLC.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Management tunnel. ALWAYS DTLS-encrypted."
options: Lightweight AP
CAPWAP control UDP 5246
CAPWAP data UDP 5247
CAPWAP
questions.explanation: 2.6.b.3 · weight high. CAPWAP control UDP 5246: Management tunnel. ALWAYS DTLS-encrypted. Carries config, firmware, client auth.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Client data tunnel. Optionally DTLS-encrypted."
options: CAPWAP data UDP 5247
CAPWAP control UDP 5246
Lightweight access port
Lightweight AP
questions.explanation: 2.6.b.4 · weight high. CAPWAP data UDP 5247: Client data tunnel. Optionally DTLS-encrypted. Carries user traffic.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "802.11 MAC functions split between AP (real-time RF) and WLC (management/policy)."
options: Why split-MAC
WLC functions
Split-MAC architecture
AP functions
questions.explanation: 2.6.c.1 · weight high. Split-MAC architecture: 802.11 MAC functions split between AP (real-time RF) and WLC (management/policy).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "RF TX/RX, encryption/decryption, beacons, probes, ACKs, frame queuing."
options: Split-MAC architecture
AP functions
Why split-MAC
WLC functions
questions.explanation: 2.6.c.2 · weight high. AP functions: RF TX/RX, encryption/decryption, beacons, probes, ACKs, frame queuing. Latency-sensitive.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Authentication, security policy, SSID/WLAN config, RF management (RRM), roaming, QoS, firmware."
options: Split-MAC architecture
Why split-MAC
WLC functions
AP functions
questions.explanation: 2.6.c.3 · weight high. WLC functions: Authentication, security policy, SSID/WLAN config, RF management (RRM), roaming, QoS, firmware.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Lightweight AP mode for branches."
options: Standalone mode
Connected mode
FlexConnect use case
FlexConnect
questions.explanation: 2.6.d.1 · weight high. FlexConnect: Lightweight AP mode for branches. Can survive WAN failure to WLC. Supports local switching.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "WAN down. Uses cached config and credentials."
options: Connected mode
FlexConnect use case
Standalone mode
FlexConnect
questions.explanation: 2.6.d.3 · weight high. Standalone mode: WAN down. Uses cached config and credentials. New users who never connected before CAN'T authenticate.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "AP switches traffic locally at branch, bypassing WLC tunnel."
options: Local switching
Standalone mode
FlexConnect use case
Connected mode
questions.explanation: 2.6.d.4 · weight high. Local switching: AP switches traffic locally at branch, bypassing WLC tunnel. Saves WAN bandwidth.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Cloud-managed wireless/networking."
options: Cloud outage resilience
Cisco Meraki
Cloud mgmt via HTTPS
Zero-touch provisioning
questions.explanation: 2.6.e.1 · weight high. Cisco Meraki: Cloud-managed wireless/networking. Dashboard in the Meraki cloud. No on-prem WLC required.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Plug in AP → gets internet → auto-downloads config from cloud."
options: Cisco Meraki
Cloud mgmt via HTTPS
Cloud outage resilience
Zero-touch provisioning
questions.explanation: 2.6.e.3 · weight high. Zero-touch provisioning: Plug in AP → gets internet → auto-downloads config from cloud. No manual setup per device.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "On-prem network-wide management platform."
options: Intent-Based Networking (IBN)
Cisco DNA Center / Catalyst Center
DNA Center vs WLC scope
SD-Access (SDA)
questions.explanation: 2.6.f.1 · weight high. Cisco DNA Center / Catalyst Center: On-prem network-wide management platform. Core of Intent-Based Networking.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Declare desired business outcome."
options: Intent-Based Networking (IBN)
Cisco DNA Center / Catalyst Center
DNA Center vs WLC scope
DPPA four pillars
questions.explanation: 2.6.f.2 · weight high. Intent-Based Networking (IBN): Declare desired business outcome. Platform translates intent into device configs automatically.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance."
options: DNA Center vs WLC scope
DPPA four pillars
Cisco DNA Center / Catalyst Center
SD-Access (SDA)
questions.explanation: 2.6.f.3 · weight high. DPPA four pillars: Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance. Memorize the acronym.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DNA Center = entire network (wired + wireless)."
options: DNA Center vs WLC scope
Cisco DNA Center / Catalyst Center
DPPA four pillars
Intent-Based Networking (IBN)
questions.explanation: 2.6.f.5 · weight high. DNA Center vs WLC scope: DNA Center = entire network (wired + wireless). WLC = wireless only.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Autonomous APs handle multiple VLANs locally, so switch port must be a trunk carrying all the VLANs."
options: PoE for APs
Lightweight AP → access port
Autonomous AP → trunk port
FlexConnect exception
questions.explanation: 2.7.a.1 · weight high. Autonomous AP → trunk port: Autonomous APs handle multiple VLANs locally, so switch port must be a trunk carrying all the VLANs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Client traffic tunneled via CAPWAP to WLC."
options: FlexConnect exception
AP management VLAN
Lightweight AP → access port
Autonomous AP → trunk port
questions.explanation: 2.7.a.2 · weight high. Lightweight AP → access port: Client traffic tunneled via CAPWAP to WLC. Switch only sees management VLAN. Access port works.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "APs typically powered via PoE over the same Ethernet cable."
options: FlexConnect exception
AP management VLAN
Lightweight AP → access port
PoE for APs
questions.explanation: 2.7.a.4 · weight high. PoE for APs: APs typically powered via PoE over the same Ethernet cable. Switch must support appropriate PoE standard (af/at/bt).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "WLC connects to switch via trunk."
options: WLC management interface
WLC dynamic interface VLANs
WLC troubleshoot
WLC → trunk port
questions.explanation: 2.7.b.1 · weight high. WLC → trunk port: WLC connects to switch via trunk. Must carry management VLAN + all dynamic interface VLANs mapped to WLANs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each dynamic interface maps a WLAN to a VLAN."
options: WLC → trunk port
WLC troubleshoot
WLC management interface
WLC dynamic interface VLANs
questions.explanation: 2.7.b.3 · weight high. WLC dynamic interface VLANs: Each dynamic interface maps a WLAN to a VLAN. Trunk to WLC must allow all these VLANs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Verify PoE status per port: allocated watts, consumed, device class."
options: show power inline
AP PoE requirements
FlexConnect exception
PoE budget planning
questions.explanation: 2.7.d.2 · weight high. show power inline: Verify PoE status per port: allocated watts, consumed, device class. Use when deploying APs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Out-of-band. Physical serial connection (RJ-45 or USB)."
options: Console access
Out-of-band vs in-band
VTY lines
Console default settings
questions.explanation: 2.8.a.1 · weight high. Console access: Out-of-band. Physical serial connection (RJ-45 or USB). Works without network/IP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "9600 baud, 8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit (8-N-1)."
options: Out-of-band vs in-band
Console default settings
VTY lines
Console access
questions.explanation: 2.8.a.2 · weight high. Console default settings: 9600 baud, 8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit (8-N-1). No flow control.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Virtual TeletYpe. Remote CLI access lines (usually 0-4 or 0-15)."
options: VTY lines
Console default settings
Out-of-band vs in-band
Console access
questions.explanation: 2.8.a.3 · weight high. VTY lines: Virtual TeletYpe. Remote CLI access lines (usually 0-4 or 0-15). Used by Telnet/SSH.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Out-of-band = separate network/channel (console, management VLAN)."
options: Out-of-band vs in-band
Console access
Console default settings
VTY lines
questions.explanation: 2.8.a.4 · weight high. Out-of-band vs in-band: Out-of-band = separate network/channel (console, management VLAN). In-band = uses production network.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "VTY config to restrict to SSH only."
options: SSH version 2
Telnet
SSH
transport input ssh
questions.explanation: 2.8.b.3 · weight high. transport input ssh: VTY config to restrict to SSH only. 'transport input all' or 'transport input telnet ssh' is insecure.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Hostname, Domain name, RSA Key, User (local), VTY config."
options: VTY for SSH
SSH prereqs (HDKUV)
ip domain-name
ip ssh version 2
questions.explanation: 2.8.c.1 · weight high. SSH prereqs (HDKUV): Hostname, Domain name, RSA Key, User (local), VTY config. All five required or SSH won't work.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Generate RSA key pair for SSH."
options: crypto key generate rsa
ip domain-name
SSH prereqs (HDKUV)
username ... secret
questions.explanation: 2.8.c.2 · weight high. crypto key generate rsa: Generate RSA key pair for SSH. Choose 2048 bits minimum. Requires hostname and domain name first.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Global command. Required before RSA key generation (key name includes host.domain)."
options: ip domain-name
VTY for SSH
ip ssh version 2
username ... secret
questions.explanation: 2.8.c.3 · weight high. ip domain-name: Global command. Required before RSA key generation (key name includes host.domain).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "line vty 0 15 → transport input ssh → login local."
options: crypto key generate rsa
SSH prereqs (HDKUV)
ip domain-name
VTY for SSH
questions.explanation: 2.8.c.5 · weight high. VTY for SSH: line vty 0 15 → transport input ssh → login local. Restricts to SSH and uses local credentials.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Enforce SSH v2 only. Reject insecure v1."
options: ip ssh version 2
crypto key generate rsa
username ... secret
SSH prereqs (HDKUV)
questions.explanation: 2.8.c.6 · weight high. ip ssh version 2: Enforce SSH v2 only. Reject insecure v1.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Global command. Enable HTTPS web GUI."
options: WLC GUI = HTTPS
ip http secure-server
HTTP / HTTPS management
ip http server
questions.explanation: 2.8.d.3 · weight high. ip http secure-server: Global command. Enable HTTPS web GUI. Uses TLS and device certificate.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "WLCs are primarily managed through HTTPS web GUI."
options: WLC GUI = HTTPS
HTTP / HTTPS management
ip http secure-server
ip http server
questions.explanation: 2.8.d.4 · weight high. WLC GUI = HTTPS: WLCs are primarily managed through HTTPS web GUI. Browser-based config.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Terminal Access Controller Access-Control System Plus."
options: Per-command authorization
TACACS+
TACACS+ separates AAA
TACACS+ best use case
questions.explanation: 2.8.e.1 · weight high. TACACS+: Terminal Access Controller Access-Control System Plus. Cisco-developed AAA. TCP port 49.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Encrypts ENTIRE packet body (not just password)."
options: TACACS+ separates AAA
TACACS+ best use case
TACACS+ full encryption
TACACS+
questions.explanation: 2.8.e.2 · weight high. TACACS+ full encryption: Encrypts ENTIRE packet body (not just password). Unique vs RADIUS.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Authentication, Authorization, Accounting are three INDEPENDENT exchanges."
options: TACACS+ best use case
TACACS+ separates AAA
TACACS+
TACACS+ full encryption
questions.explanation: 2.8.e.3 · weight high. TACACS+ separates AAA: Authentication, Authorization, Accounting are three INDEPENDENT exchanges. Enables granular control.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "TACACS+ can authorize/deny INDIVIDUAL IOS commands per user."
options: TACACS+ full encryption
TACACS+ best use case
Per-command authorization
TACACS+
questions.explanation: 2.8.e.4 · weight high. Per-command authorization: TACACS+ can authorize/deny INDIVIDUAL IOS commands per user. Critical for network device mgmt.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Network device administration."
options: TACACS+ best use case
Per-command authorization
TACACS+ full encryption
TACACS+ separates AAA
questions.explanation: 2.8.e.5 · weight high. TACACS+ best use case: Network device administration. Controlling which admins can run which commands.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service."
options: RADIUS password-only encryption
TACACS+ vs RADIUS scenario
RADIUS combines auth+authz
RADIUS
questions.explanation: 2.8.f.1 · weight high. RADIUS: Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service. Open standard AAA. UDP 1812 auth / 1813 accounting.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Only the password field is encrypted."
options: RADIUS
TACACS+ vs RADIUS scenario
RADIUS password-only encryption
RADIUS combines auth+authz
questions.explanation: 2.8.f.2 · weight high. RADIUS password-only encryption: Only the password field is encrypted. Rest of packet is plaintext. Weaker than TACACS+.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Access-Accept returns both authentication success AND authorization attributes in one exchange."
options: RADIUS password-only encryption
RADIUS
RADIUS combines auth+authz
TACACS+ vs RADIUS scenario
questions.explanation: 2.8.f.3 · weight high. RADIUS combines auth+authz: Access-Accept returns both authentication success AND authorization attributes in one exchange.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Network ACCESS control — 802.1X wired/wireless, WPA2-Enterprise Wi-Fi, VPN."
options: TACACS+ vs RADIUS scenario
RADIUS best use case
RADIUS password-only encryption
RADIUS combines auth+authz
questions.explanation: 2.8.f.4 · weight high. RADIUS best use case: Network ACCESS control — 802.1X wired/wireless, WPA2-Enterprise Wi-Fi, VPN. Multi-vendor.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Device admin access → TACACS+."
options: TACACS+ vs RADIUS scenario
RADIUS password-only encryption
RADIUS combines auth+authz
RADIUS
questions.explanation: 2.8.f.5 · weight high. TACACS+ vs RADIUS scenario: Device admin access → TACACS+. User network access → RADIUS. Both can run on Cisco ISE.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Device mgmt via cloud dashboard (HTTPS)."
options: Zero-touch provisioning
Cloud-managed networking
Management models comparison
Cloud offline behavior
questions.explanation: 2.8.g.1 · weight high. Cloud-managed networking: Device mgmt via cloud dashboard (HTTPS). No on-prem controller required. Meraki is the canonical example.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Device plugs in → internet → downloads config from cloud automatically."
options: Zero-touch provisioning
Cloud-managed networking
Cloud offline behavior
Management models comparison
questions.explanation: 2.8.g.2 · weight high. Zero-touch provisioning: Device plugs in → internet → downloads config from cloud automatically. Key cloud benefit.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Internal WLC label for admins (e.g., 'Corporate-WiFi')."
options: WLAN SSID
Multiple WLANs per WLC
WLAN Profile Name
WLAN ID (1-512)
questions.explanation: 2.9.a.1 · weight high. WLAN Profile Name: Internal WLC label for admins (e.g., 'Corporate-WiFi'). Not visible to wireless clients.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Broadcast network name seen by clients."
options: WLAN ID (1-512)
WLAN SSID
WLAN Status: Enabled
WLAN Profile Name
questions.explanation: 2.9.a.2 · weight high. WLAN SSID: Broadcast network name seen by clients. Visible in beacons (e.g., 'CorpNet'). Can differ from Profile Name.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "WLAN is disabled by default after creation."
options: Multiple WLANs per WLC
WLAN Status: Enabled
WLAN ID (1-512)
WLAN SSID
questions.explanation: 2.9.a.4 · weight high. WLAN Status: Enabled: WLAN is disabled by default after creation. Must be explicitly enabled before APs broadcast.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Pre-shared key. Auth Key Mgmt = PSK."
options: WPA2-Personal (PSK)
WPA2 + AES-CCMP
PSK vs 802.1X decision
WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X)
questions.explanation: 2.9.b.2 · weight high. WPA2-Personal (PSK): Pre-shared key. Auth Key Mgmt = PSK. Everyone shares one password. Home/small office.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Per-user credentials via RADIUS."
options: WPA2-Personal (PSK)
WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X)
RADIUS server on WLC
PSK vs 802.1X decision
questions.explanation: 2.9.b.3 · weight high. WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X): Per-user credentials via RADIUS. Auth Key Mgmt = 802.1X. Corporate standard.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Configured at Security → AAA → RADIUS → Authentication."
options: WPA2-Personal (PSK)
PSK vs 802.1X decision
WPA2 + AES-CCMP
RADIUS server on WLC
questions.explanation: 2.9.b.4 · weight high. RADIUS server on WLC: Configured at Security → AAA → RADIUS → Authentication. WLC acts as NAS forwarding to RADIUS.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Small office, no RADIUS = PSK."
options: RADIUS server on WLC
PSK vs 802.1X decision
WPA2 + AES-CCMP
WPA2-Personal (PSK)
questions.explanation: 2.9.b.5 · weight high. PSK vs 802.1X decision: Small office, no RADIUS = PSK. Enterprise, per-user auth, audit trail = 802.1X.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "WLC virtual interface tied to a VLAN."
options: WLAN → Interface → VLAN
Dynamic interface
Switch trunk requirement
Wireless segmentation
questions.explanation: 2.9.c.1 · weight high. Dynamic interface: WLC virtual interface tied to a VLAN. Has IP/mask/gateway on that VLAN. Like an SVI on a L3 switch.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Mapping chain: WLAN's General tab selects interface → interface is tied to VLAN → client traffic flows on that VLAN."
options: WLAN → Interface → VLAN
Wireless segmentation
Dynamic interface
Switch trunk requirement
questions.explanation: 2.9.c.2 · weight high. WLAN → Interface → VLAN: Mapping chain: WLAN's General tab selects interface → interface is tied to VLAN → client traffic flows on that VLAN.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Trunk to the WLC must allow ALL dynamic interface VLANs (plus management)."
options: WLAN → Interface → VLAN
Switch trunk requirement
Wireless segmentation
Dynamic interface
questions.explanation: 2.9.c.3 · weight high. Switch trunk requirement: Trunk to the WLC must allow ALL dynamic interface VLANs (plus management).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Different SSIDs → different VLANs → different policies (ACLs, DHCP scope, QoS)."
options: WLAN → Interface → VLAN
Wireless segmentation
Switch trunk requirement
Dynamic interface
questions.explanation: 2.9.c.4 · weight high. Wireless segmentation: Different SSIDs → different VLANs → different policies (ACLs, DHCP scope, QoS).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Highest priority. Use for VoIP WLANs."
options: Silver — Best Effort
Platinum — Voice
Gold — Video
Bronze — Background
questions.explanation: 2.9.d.1 · weight high. Platinum — Voice: Highest priority. Use for VoIP WLANs. Maps to AC_VO, DSCP EF (46).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "High priority. Video conferencing/streaming."
options: QoS profile assignment scheme
Silver — Best Effort
Gold — Video
Platinum — Voice
questions.explanation: 2.9.d.2 · weight high. Gold — Video: High priority. Video conferencing/streaming. Maps to AC_VI, DSCP AF41.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Lowest priority. Guest WLANs, bulk/backup transfers."
options: Bronze — Background
Platinum — Voice
Silver — Best Effort
QoS profile assignment scheme
questions.explanation: 2.9.d.4 · weight high. Bronze — Background: Lowest priority. Guest WLANs, bulk/backup transfers. Maps to AC_BK, DSCP AF11.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "VoIP → Platinum. Corporate data → Silver."
options: Gold — Video
QoS profile assignment scheme
Silver — Best Effort
Platinum — Voice
questions.explanation: 2.9.d.5 · weight high. QoS profile assignment scheme: VoIP → Platinum. Corporate data → Silver. Guest → Bronze. Protects voice even under congestion.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Prevents wireless clients on same WLAN from communicating directly."
options: Session Timeout
Client Exclusion
P2P Blocking (client isolation)
DTIM Period
questions.explanation: 2.9.e.2 · weight high. P2P Blocking (client isolation): Prevents wireless clients on same WLAN from communicating directly. Best practice for guest networks.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Route for a directly-attached network."
options: Route entry format
Code C (Connected)
Code O (OSPF)
Code D (EIGRP)
questions.explanation: 3.1.a.1 · weight high. Code C (Connected): Route for a directly-attached network. Added automatically when interface comes up. AD 0.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "/32 host route for the router's own interface IP."
options: Code C (Connected)
Route entry format
Code O (OSPF)
Code L (Local)
questions.explanation: 3.1.a.2 · weight high. Code L (Local): /32 host route for the router's own interface IP. Auto-generated. Ensures local processing.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Learned via EIGRP's DUAL algorithm."
options: Code C (Connected)
Code D (EIGRP)
Code L (Local)
S* candidate default
questions.explanation: 3.1.a.5 · weight high. Code D (EIGRP): Learned via EIGRP's DUAL algorithm. AD 90 (internal), 170 (external).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'O 10.1.1.0/24 [110/20] via 192.168.1.2' — code, network/mask, [AD/metric], next-hop."
options: Code O (OSPF)
Route entry format
Code B (BGP)
Code S (Static)
questions.explanation: 3.1.a.9 · weight high. Route entry format: 'O 10.1.1.0/24 [110/20] via 192.168.1.2' — code, network/mask, [AD/metric], next-hop.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Network portion of the route entry."
options: Parent network grouping
Prefix length impact
Prefix (network address)
Prefix matching
questions.explanation: 3.1.b.1 · weight high. Prefix (network address): Network portion of the route entry. All host bits zeroed. Defines which addresses the route covers.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "/8 covers 16M addresses, /24 covers 256, /32 covers 1."
options: Prefix matching
Parent network grouping
Prefix (network address)
Prefix length impact
questions.explanation: 3.1.b.2 · weight high. Prefix length impact: /8 covers 16M addresses, /24 covers 256, /32 covers 1. More specific prefix = fewer addresses.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Router compares destination IP's first N bits against prefix, where N = prefix length."
options: Prefix matching
Parent network grouping
Prefix length impact
Prefix (network address)
questions.explanation: 3.1.b.3 · weight high. Prefix matching: Router compares destination IP's first N bits against prefix, where N = prefix length.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Displayed as /N. Counts the 1-bits in the mask."
options: Mask specificity = match
/30 for P2P links
Prefix length (CIDR)
Magic number method
questions.explanation: 3.1.c.1 · weight high. Prefix length (CIDR): Displayed as /N. Counts the 1-bits in the mask. /24 = 24 network bits.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Longer mask = more specific = wins longest prefix match."
options: Magic number method
/30 for P2P links
Mask specificity = match
Prefix length (CIDR)
questions.explanation: 3.1.c.2 · weight high. Mask specificity = match: Longer mask = more specific = wins longest prefix match. /28 > /24 > /16.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Block size = 256 − interesting octet value."
options: Magic number method
/30 for P2P links
Prefix length (CIDR)
Mask specificity = match
questions.explanation: 3.1.c.3 · weight high. Magic number method: Block size = 256 − interesting octet value. Subnets start at multiples of block size.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "IP of the next router on the path."
options: Next-hop IP
C / L have no next hop
Recursive lookup
Exit interface only
questions.explanation: 3.1.d.1 · weight high. Next-hop IP: IP of the next router on the path. Must be on a directly-connected subnet (reachable via ARP).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Directly-connected and local routes don't need a next hop — destination reachable directly via ARP."
options: Next-hop IP
Recursive lookup
Exit interface only
C / L have no next hop
questions.explanation: 3.1.d.2 · weight high. C / L have no next hop: Directly-connected and local routes don't need a next hop — destination reachable directly via ARP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Static route with only an exit interface."
options: Recursive lookup
C / L have no next hop
Exit interface only
Next-hop IP
questions.explanation: 3.1.d.4 · weight high. Exit interface only: Static route with only an exit interface. Fine on P2P. AVOID on Ethernet — causes excessive ARP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "0-255 trust score per route source."
options: Connected AD = 0
RIP AD = 120
Administrative Distance (AD)
EIGRP AD = 90 (internal)
questions.explanation: 3.1.e.1 · weight high. Administrative Distance (AD): 0-255 trust score per route source. Lower = more trusted. Tiebreaker between different protocols.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Max trust. Router physically sees the network."
options: Administrative Distance (AD)
Connected AD = 0
RIP AD = 120
Static AD = 1
questions.explanation: 3.1.e.2 · weight high. Connected AD = 0: Max trust. Router physically sees the network. Best possible AD.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Explicitly configured by admin."
options: OSPF AD = 110
EIGRP external AD = 170
Static AD = 1
Administrative Distance (AD)
questions.explanation: 3.1.e.3 · weight high. Static AD = 1: Explicitly configured by admin. Highly trusted.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "External BGP routes. Trusted partners across AS boundaries."
options: EIGRP external AD = 170
EIGRP AD = 90 (internal)
eBGP AD = 20
iBGP AD = 200
questions.explanation: 3.1.e.4 · weight high. eBGP AD = 20: External BGP routes. Trusted partners across AS boundaries.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Cisco's IGP. Considered more reliable than OSPF."
options: RIP AD = 120
AD = 255 (unusable)
OSPF AD = 110
EIGRP AD = 90 (internal)
questions.explanation: 3.1.e.5 · weight high. EIGRP AD = 90 (internal): Cisco's IGP. Considered more reliable than OSPF.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "All OSPF types (intra-area, inter-area, external) use 110."
options: OSPF AD = 110
EIGRP external AD = 170
iBGP AD = 200
RIP AD = 120
questions.explanation: 3.1.e.6 · weight high. OSPF AD = 110: All OSPF types (intra-area, inter-area, external) use 110.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth."
options: OSPF cost
RIP hop count
Metric purpose
EIGRP composite metric
questions.explanation: 3.1.f.2 · weight high. OSPF cost: Reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth. Default reference = 100 Mbps. Lower = preferred.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Number of routers to destination."
options: ECMP load balancing
RIP hop count
EIGRP composite metric
Metric purpose
questions.explanation: 3.1.f.3 · weight high. RIP hop count: Number of routers to destination. Max 15. 16 = unreachable (infinity).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "OSPF/RIP command that injects a default route to all neighbors."
options: default-information originate
'Gateway of last resort is not set'
questions.explanation: 3.1.g.3 · weight high. default-information originate: OSPF/RIP command that injects a default route to all neighbors. On the edge router.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Router picks the route with the LONGEST matching mask."
options: 0.0.0.0/0 least specific
LPM beats AD
show ip route [destination]
Longest Prefix Match (LPM)
questions.explanation: 3.2.a.1 · weight high. Longest Prefix Match (LPM): Router picks the route with the LONGEST matching mask. Most specific wins. Step 1 of forwarding decision.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "LPM ALWAYS wins before AD is consulted."
options: Longest Prefix Match (LPM)
0.0.0.0/0 least specific
/32 most specific
LPM beats AD
questions.explanation: 3.2.a.2 · weight high. LPM beats AD: LPM ALWAYS wins before AD is consulted. A /32 from RIP (AD 120) beats a /24 from connected (AD 0).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Host route. Matches exactly one IP."
options: /32 most specific
Longest Prefix Match (LPM)
show ip route [destination]
0.0.0.0/0 least specific
questions.explanation: 3.2.a.3 · weight high. /32 most specific: Host route. Matches exactly one IP. Always wins LPM.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Default route. Loses to everything more specific."
options: LPM beats AD
0.0.0.0/0 least specific
show ip route [destination]
/32 most specific
questions.explanation: 3.2.a.4 · weight high. 0.0.0.0/0 least specific: Default route. Loses to everything more specific. True last resort.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Shows which route the router actually uses for a specific IP."
options: show ip route [destination]
0.0.0.0/0 least specific
LPM beats AD
Longest Prefix Match (LPM)
questions.explanation: 3.2.a.5 · weight high. show ip route [destination]: Shows which route the router actually uses for a specific IP. Best way to verify LPM.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Used when routes have SAME prefix length but DIFFERENT protocols."
options: show ip ospf database
AD as tiebreaker
Losing route kept as backup
AD not consulted across masks
questions.explanation: 3.2.b.1 · weight high. AD as tiebreaker: Used when routes have SAME prefix length but DIFFERENT protocols. Lower AD wins.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Route not installed in table still lives in source protocol's database."
options: show ip ospf database
AD not consulted across masks
Losing route kept as backup
AD as tiebreaker
questions.explanation: 3.2.b.2 · weight high. Losing route kept as backup: Route not installed in table still lives in source protocol's database. Takes over if primary fails.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "AD is NEVER consulted when prefix lengths differ."
options: Losing route kept as backup
show ip ospf database
AD as tiebreaker
AD not consulted across masks
questions.explanation: 3.2.b.3 · weight high. AD not consulted across masks: AD is NEVER consulted when prefix lengths differ. LPM decides first.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Connected=0, Static=1, eBGP=20, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, IS-IS=115, RIP=120, EIGRP-ext=170, iBGP=200, Unknown=255."
options: maximum-paths command
Modify AD
AD values (memorize)
Chant the key values
questions.explanation: 3.2.c.1 · weight high. AD values (memorize): Connected=0, Static=1, eBGP=20, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, IS-IS=115, RIP=120, EIGRP-ext=170, iBGP=200, Unknown=255.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'0-1-20-90-110-115-120-200.' Say until automatic."
options: Modify AD
Step 2: Lowest AD
AD values (memorize)
Chant the key values
questions.explanation: 3.2.c.2 · weight high. Chant the key values: '0-1-20-90-110-115-120-200.' Say until automatic. Write on whiteboard first thing in the exam.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Same prefix AND same AD → metric decides."
options: maximum-paths command
CEF per-destination LB
Metric as 3rd tiebreaker
ECMP default 4 paths
questions.explanation: 3.2.d.1 · weight high. Metric as 3rd tiebreaker: Same prefix AND same AD → metric decides. Within the same protocol. Lower wins.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Used only if Step 1 produces a tie (same mask)."
options: Step 2: Lowest AD
Step 1: Longest Prefix Match
Step 3: Lowest metric
PAM (forwarding decision)
questions.explanation: 3.2.e.3 · weight high. Step 2: Lowest AD: Used only if Step 1 produces a tie (same mask). Lower AD wins.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Used only if Steps 1 AND 2 tie (same mask + same protocol)."
options: Step 3: Lowest metric
PAM (forwarding decision)
Step 2: Lowest AD
Exam trap
questions.explanation: 3.2.e.4 · weight high. Step 3: Lowest metric: Used only if Steps 1 AND 2 tie (same mask + same protocol). Lower metric wins. Ties = ECMP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Don't jump to AD before checking prefix length."
options: Exam trap
Step 3: Lowest metric
PAM (forwarding decision)
Step 1: Longest Prefix Match
questions.explanation: 3.2.e.5 · weight high. Exam trap: Don't jump to AD before checking prefix length. A /28 from RIP still beats a /24 from connected.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop]'."
options: IPv6 default route
Branch office pattern
S* in route table
IPv4 default route
questions.explanation: 3.3.a.1 · weight high. IPv4 default route: 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop]'. Matches any destination. Gateway of last resort.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Static candidate default route marker."
options: IPv6 default route
S* in route table
IPv4 default route
Branch office pattern
questions.explanation: 3.3.a.4 · weight high. S* in route table: Static candidate default route marker. Only appears when a default is configured.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'ip route [network] [mask] [next-hop|interface]'."
options: Network static route syntax
Bidirectional routing rule
IPv6 static syntax
Static AD = 1
questions.explanation: 3.3.b.1 · weight high. Network static route syntax: 'ip route [network] [mask] [next-hop|interface]'. Manual route to a specific network.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Need routes in BOTH directions."
options: Bidirectional routing rule
Static AD = 1
IPv6 static syntax
Network static route syntax
questions.explanation: 3.3.b.3 · weight high. Bidirectional routing rule: Need routes in BOTH directions. R1→R3's LAN AND R3→R1's LAN. Missing return route = classic trap.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'ipv6 route [prefix/length] [next-hop|interface]'."
options: IPv6 static syntax
Static AD = 1
Network static route syntax
Bidirectional routing rule
questions.explanation: 3.3.b.4 · weight high. IPv6 static syntax: 'ipv6 route [prefix/length] [next-hop|interface]'. Same concept.

AD must exceed primary
Common mistake

ipv6 unicast-routing

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Preferred router usually has best path (closest to WAN)."
options: VRRP preempt ON default
Preemption
Preemption rationale
HSRP preempt OFF default
questions.explanation: 3.5.f.4 · weight high. Preemption rationale: Preferred router usually has best path (closest to WAN). Preemption ensures it returns to primary role.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Scenario: HSRP active fails, returns."
options: HSRP preempt OFF default
Preemption rationale
Classic exam trap
VRRP preempt ON default
questions.explanation: 3.5.f.5 · weight high. Classic exam trap: Scenario: HSRP active fails, returns. Without preempt, it stays as standby. Students often get this wrong.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Private IP of host on the inside network."
options: Inside Local
Inside Global
Outside Global
Memory trick
questions.explanation: 4.1.a.1 · weight high. Inside Local: Private IP of host on the inside network. The REAL internal IP. Example: 192.168.1.10.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Public IP representing the inside host to the outside."
options: Memory trick
Inside Local
Inside Global
Outside Local
questions.explanation: 4.1.a.2 · weight high. Inside Global: Public IP representing the inside host to the outside. The translated address seen by external hosts.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'Inside' = where the private host lives."
options: Inside Global
Outside Global
Outside Local
Memory trick
questions.explanation: 4.1.a.5 · weight high. Memory trick: 'Inside' = where the private host lives. 'Local' = the real address. 'Global' = what the internet sees.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'ip nat inside source static [inside-local] [inside-global]'."
options: Bidirectional translation
One public IP per server
Static NAT command
Static NAT
questions.explanation: 4.1.b.2 · weight high. Static NAT command: 'ip nat inside source static [inside-local] [inside-global]'. Creates the permanent mapping.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "External traffic TO the public IP is translated to the private IP."
options: Static NAT command
One public IP per server
Bidirectional translation
Static NAT
questions.explanation: 4.1.b.3 · weight high. Bidirectional translation: External traffic TO the public IP is translated to the private IP. Enables externally-initiated connections.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Pool-based one-to-one mapping."
options: Rarely used today
Dynamic NAT
Pool exhaustion = DROP
Pool config
questions.explanation: 4.1.c.1 · weight high. Dynamic NAT: Pool-based one-to-one mapping. Temporary. Public IP returns to pool after timeout.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'ip nat pool NAME [start-ip] [end-ip] netmask [mask]'."
options: Dynamic NAT vs PAT
Pool exhaustion = DROP
Dynamic NAT
Pool config
questions.explanation: 4.1.c.2 · weight high. Pool config: 'ip nat pool NAME [start-ip] [end-ip] netmask [mask]'. Defines available public IPs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "When all pool IPs are in use, new connections are DROPPED (not queued)."
options: Pool config
Dynamic NAT
Pool exhaustion = DROP
Dynamic NAT vs PAT
questions.explanation: 4.1.c.3 · weight high. Pool exhaustion = DROP: When all pool IPs are in use, new connections are DROPPED (not queued).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Dynamic = one-to-one temporary."
options: Rarely used today
Dynamic NAT vs PAT
Pool config
Dynamic NAT
questions.explanation: 4.1.c.4 · weight high. Dynamic NAT vs PAT: Dynamic = one-to-one temporary. PAT = many-to-one using ports. Difference is the 'overload' keyword.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "CRITICAL. Transforms dynamic NAT into PAT."
options: Home router = PAT
PAT / NAT Overload
PAT using pool
'overload' keyword
questions.explanation: 4.1.d.2 · weight high. 'overload' keyword: CRITICAL. Transforms dynamic NAT into PAT. Without it = dynamic NAT (one-to-one).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'ip nat inside source list [acl] interface [outside-if] overload'."
options: PAT / NAT Overload
Port uniqueness
PAT using interface
'overload' keyword
questions.explanation: 4.1.d.3 · weight high. PAT using interface: 'ip nat inside source list [acl] interface [outside-if] overload'. Uses outside interface's IP as public IP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'ip nat inside source list [acl] pool [name] overload'."
options: Home router = PAT
Port uniqueness
PAT using pool
PAT using interface
questions.explanation: 4.1.d.4 · weight high. PAT using pool: 'ip nat inside source list [acl] pool [name] overload'. Uses pool IPs with overload.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each internal host gets unique source port on shared public IP."
options: Home router = PAT
PAT using interface
'overload' keyword
Port uniqueness
questions.explanation: 4.1.d.5 · weight high. Port uniqueness: Each internal host gets unique source port on shared public IP. ~65,000 sessions per public IP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Every consumer router does PAT."
options: PAT using interface
'overload' keyword
PAT / NAT Overload
Home router = PAT
questions.explanation: 4.1.d.6 · weight high. Home router = PAT: Every consumer router does PAT. All home devices share one ISP-assigned public IP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Interface command. Designates interface facing private/internal network."
options: ip nat outside
show ip nat statistics
Missing designations = no NAT
ip nat inside
questions.explanation: 4.1.e.1 · weight high. ip nat inside: Interface command. Designates interface facing private/internal network.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Interface command. Designates interface facing public/external network."
options: ip nat inside
Missing designations = no NAT
ip nat outside
show ip nat statistics
questions.explanation: 4.1.e.2 · weight high. ip nat outside: Interface command. Designates interface facing public/external network.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Reveals interface designations under 'Inside/Outside interfaces'."
options: show ip nat statistics
ip nat outside
ip nat inside
Missing designations = no NAT
questions.explanation: 4.1.e.4 · weight high. show ip nat statistics: Reveals interface designations under 'Inside/Outside interfaces'. Primary verification tool.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Selects which inside hosts get translated."
options: Deny in NAT ACL
Permit in NAT ACL
Standard ACL sufficient
NAT ACL purpose
questions.explanation: 4.1.f.1 · weight high. NAT ACL purpose: Selects which inside hosts get translated. Not a traffic filter — selection filter.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Permit = translate this traffic."
options: Permit in NAT ACL
Deny in NAT ACL
Standard ACL sufficient
NAT ACL purpose
questions.explanation: 4.1.f.2 · weight high. Permit in NAT ACL: Permit = translate this traffic. Different meaning from interface ACLs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Deny = DO NOT translate (but traffic is NOT blocked)."
options: NAT ACL purpose
Deny in NAT ACL
Permit in NAT ACL
Standard ACL sufficient
questions.explanation: 4.1.f.3 · weight high. Deny in NAT ACL: Deny = DO NOT translate (but traffic is NOT blocked). Passes through untranslated.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "NAT source matching only needs source IP."
options: Permit in NAT ACL
Standard ACL sufficient
Deny in NAT ACL
NAT ACL purpose
questions.explanation: 4.1.f.4 · weight high. Standard ACL sufficient: NAT source matching only needs source IP. Standard ACL works: 'access-list 1 permit [net] [wildcard]'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Overall metrics: totals, hits, misses, pool usage, inside/outside interfaces."
options: show ip nat translations
PAT entries show ports
clear ip nat translation *
show ip nat statistics
questions.explanation: 4.1.g.2 · weight high. show ip nat statistics: Overall metrics: totals, hits, misses, pool usage, inside/outside interfaces.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "PAT translations include source port in output."
options: High misses count
PAT entries show ports
show ip nat translations
show ip nat statistics
questions.explanation: 4.1.g.4 · weight high. PAT entries show ports: PAT translations include source port in output. Same public IP, different ports per host.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Configured PAT but forgot overload = got dynamic NAT."
options: NAT troubleshoot order (IAPC)
Missing 'overload'
debug ip nat
#1 issue: missing interfaces
questions.explanation: 4.1.h.3 · weight high. Missing 'overload': Configured PAT but forgot overload = got dynamic NAT. Only works for N hosts where N = pool size.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Log correlation, certificate validation, Kerberos (within 5 min), compliance audit trails."
options: Why time matters
NTP slewing
Client-server hierarchy
NTP
questions.explanation: 4.2.a.2 · weight high. Why time matters: Log correlation, certificate validation, Kerberos (within 5 min), compliance audit trails.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Time flows from authoritative source (atomic clock) down through stratum levels."
options: Client-server hierarchy
NTP slewing
Why time matters
NTP
questions.explanation: 4.2.a.3 · weight high. Client-server hierarchy: Time flows from authoritative source (atomic clock) down through stratum levels.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Reference clock itself (atomic, GPS, radio)."
options: Stratum 16
Stratum 2-15
Lower stratum wins
Stratum 0
questions.explanation: 4.2.b.1 · weight high. Stratum 0: Reference clock itself (atomic, GPS, radio). Hardware. Not directly reachable over NTP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Directly connected to stratum 0."
options: Stratum 16
Stratum 2-15
Stratum 1
Lower stratum wins
questions.explanation: 4.2.b.2 · weight high. Stratum 1: Directly connected to stratum 0. Most accurate NTP source on the network.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each hop increments stratum by 1."
options: Stratum 1
Stratum 0
Stratum 16
Stratum 2-15
questions.explanation: 4.2.b.3 · weight high. Stratum 2-15: Each hop increments stratum by 1. All are valid, usable sources. Lower = more accurate.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "UNSYNCHRONIZED. Device has no valid time source."
options: Lower stratum wins
Stratum 2-15
Stratum 0
Stratum 16
questions.explanation: 4.2.b.4 · weight high. Stratum 16: UNSYNCHRONIZED. Device has no valid time source. Others refuse to sync from it.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Multiple sources? Client picks lowest stratum (closest to reference clock)."
options: Stratum 16
Stratum 1
Stratum 0
Lower stratum wins
questions.explanation: 4.2.b.5 · weight high. Lower stratum wins: Multiple sources? Client picks lowest stratum (closest to reference clock).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Client command. Sync the local clock to the specified NTP server."
options: Multiple NTP servers
ntp server vs ntp peer
ntp server [ip]
show ntp associations
questions.explanation: 4.2.c.1 · weight high. ntp server [ip]: Client command. Sync the local clock to the specified NTP server. Can specify multiple.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Shows 'Clock is synchronized/unsynchronized', stratum, and reference clock ID."
options: show ntp status
Multiple NTP servers
ntp server vs ntp peer
ntp server [ip]
questions.explanation: 4.2.c.4 · weight high. show ntp status: Shows 'Clock is synchronized/unsynchronized', stratum, and reference clock ID.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Router becomes NTP server using its own clock as reference."
options: ntp master [stratum]
Clients become stratum N+1
ntp master vs ntp server
Don't set stratum too low
questions.explanation: 4.2.d.1 · weight high. ntp master [stratum]: Router becomes NTP server using its own clock as reference. Advertises given stratum value.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "If master advertises stratum 5, clients syncing to it become stratum 6."
options: ntp master vs ntp server
ntp master [stratum]
Clients become stratum N+1
Don't set stratum too low
questions.explanation: 4.2.d.3 · weight high. Clients become stratum N+1: If master advertises stratum 5, clients syncing to it become stratum 6. Each hop adds 1.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "List of configured NTP sources, their stratums, and reach."
options: reach 377 (octal)
Stratum 16 = broken
show ntp status
show ntp associations
questions.explanation: 4.2.f.1 · weight high. show ntp associations: List of configured NTP sources, their stratums, and reach. '*' = selected, '+' = candidate.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Shows sync state: 'Clock is synchronized' or 'unsynchronized'."
options: show ntp associations
show ntp status
reach 377 (octal)
Stratum 16 = broken
questions.explanation: 4.2.f.2 · weight high. show ntp status: Shows sync state: 'Clock is synchronized' or 'unsynchronized'. Stratum, reference ID.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "If show ntp status shows stratum 16, device is NOT synced."
options: Stratum 16 = broken
show ntp status
reach 377 (octal)
show ntp associations
questions.explanation: 4.2.f.4 · weight high. Stratum 16 = broken: If show ntp status shows stratum 16, device is NOT synced. Troubleshoot immediately.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Discover → Offer → Request → Acknowledge."
options: DORA process
A = Acknowledge
DHCP relay
O = Offer
questions.explanation: 4.3.a.1 · weight high. DORA process: Discover → Offer → Request → Acknowledge. Four-step DHCP IP assignment sequence.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Client broadcasts 'anyone have an IP?' (dest 255.255.255.255, src 0.0.0.0)."
options: D = Discover
A = Acknowledge
ip helper-address
R = Request
questions.explanation: 4.3.a.2 · weight high. D = Discover: Client broadcasts 'anyone have an IP?' (dest 255.255.255.255, src 0.0.0.0). Client UDP 68 → server UDP 67.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Server broadcasts offer with proposed IP, mask, gateway, DNS, lease time."
options: DHCP relay
ip helper-address
O = Offer
R = Request
questions.explanation: 4.3.a.3 · weight high. O = Offer: Server broadcasts offer with proposed IP, mask, gateway, DNS, lease time. Server UDP 67 → client UDP 68.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Client broadcasts 'I'll take this offer.' Tells other DHCP servers to withdraw."
options: DORA process
A = Acknowledge
R = Request
O = Offer
questions.explanation: 4.3.a.4 · weight high. R = Request: Client broadcasts 'I'll take this offer.' Tells other DHCP servers to withdraw. Required so other servers free their offers.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Configured on a SVI/interface."
options: A = Acknowledge
D = Discover
DHCP relay
ip helper-address
questions.explanation: 4.3.a.6 · weight high. ip helper-address: Configured on a SVI/interface. Forwards DHCP broadcasts as unicast to DHCP server across subnets.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Router/L3 switch acts as relay because broadcasts don't cross L3 boundaries."
options: DHCP relay
O = Offer
A = Acknowledge
D = Discover
questions.explanation: 4.3.a.7 · weight high. DHCP relay: Router/L3 switch acts as relay because broadcasts don't cross L3 boundaries. 'ip helper-address' does this.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Create a DHCP pool. Enters DHCP pool config mode."
options: default-router [ip]
dns-server [ip]
ip dhcp pool [name]
lease [days hours minutes]
questions.explanation: 4.3.b.1 · weight high. ip dhcp pool [name]: Create a DHCP pool. Enters DHCP pool config mode.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Specify the subnet the pool serves."
options: lease [days hours minutes]
network [subnet] [mask]
dns-server [ip]
ip dhcp excluded-address
questions.explanation: 4.3.b.2 · weight high. network [subnet] [mask]: Specify the subnet the pool serves. Under pool config.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DNS server(s) sent to clients in the offer."
options: ip dhcp pool [name]
network [subnet] [mask]
ip dhcp excluded-address
dns-server [ip]
questions.explanation: 4.3.b.4 · weight high. dns-server [ip]: DNS server(s) sent to clients in the offer.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Global command (OUTSIDE pool config)."
options: dns-server [ip]
network [subnet] [mask]
lease [days hours minutes]
ip dhcp excluded-address
questions.explanation: 4.3.b.6 · weight high. ip dhcp excluded-address: Global command (OUTSIDE pool config). Reserves IPs so DHCP won't assign them (e.g., gateway, servers).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "At 50% of lease, client unicasts DHCPREQUEST to original server for renewal."
options: Renewal failure behavior
Unicast vs broadcast renewal
T2 rebinding (87.5%)
T1 renewal (50%)
questions.explanation: 4.3.c.1 · weight high. T1 renewal (50%): At 50% of lease, client unicasts DHCPREQUEST to original server for renewal.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "At 87.5% of lease, if T1 failed, client broadcasts DHCPREQUEST to ANY DHCP server."
options: T1 renewal (50%)
T2 rebinding (87.5%)
Unicast vs broadcast renewal
Renewal failure behavior
questions.explanation: 4.3.c.2 · weight high. T2 rebinding (87.5%): At 87.5% of lease, if T1 failed, client broadcasts DHCPREQUEST to ANY DHCP server.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "T1 = unicast (direct to original server)."
options: T1 renewal (50%)
T2 rebinding (87.5%)
Renewal failure behavior
Unicast vs broadcast renewal
questions.explanation: 4.3.c.4 · weight high. Unicast vs broadcast renewal: T1 = unicast (direct to original server). T2 = broadcast (any server can help).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Mail Exchange. Specifies email server(s) for a domain."
options: CNAME record
PTR record
NS record
MX record
questions.explanation: 4.3.d.4 · weight high. MX record: Mail Exchange. Specifies email server(s) for a domain. Includes priority value.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Client expects the DNS server to return the FINAL answer."
options: Typical client query
Recursive query
Iterative query
DNS caching
questions.explanation: 4.3.e.1 · weight high. Recursive query: Client expects the DNS server to return the FINAL answer. Server does all the work.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DNS server may return a REFERRAL (another server to ask)."
options: DNS caching
Iterative query
Typical client query
Recursive query
questions.explanation: 4.3.e.2 · weight high. Iterative query: DNS server may return a REFERRAL (another server to ask). Caller keeps asking.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Recursive to local DNS resolver."
options: Recursive query
Typical client query
DNS caching
Iterative query
questions.explanation: 4.3.e.3 · weight high. Typical client query: Recursive to local DNS resolver. Resolver then does iterative queries up the hierarchy.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Root → TLD (.com, .org, .net) → domain (example.com) → host (www.example.com)."
options: FQDN
DNS hierarchy
TLD (Top-Level Domain)
13 root server clusters
questions.explanation: 4.3.f.1 · weight high. DNS hierarchy: Root → TLD (.com, .org, .net) → domain (example.com) → host (www.example.com).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Simple Network Management Protocol."
options: UDP 161 / UDP 162
SNMP Manager
SNMP
SNMP Agent
questions.explanation: 4.4.a.1 · weight high. SNMP: Simple Network Management Protocol. Monitor and manage network devices. UDP only (not TCP).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "The management station (e.g., SolarWinds, PRTG)."
options: SNMP Agent
SNMP
SNMP Manager
UDP 161 / UDP 162
questions.explanation: 4.4.a.2 · weight high. SNMP Manager: The management station (e.g., SolarWinds, PRTG). Sends queries and receives traps.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Runs on managed device (router/switch)."
options: SNMP Manager
UDP 161 / UDP 162
SNMP Agent
SNMP
questions.explanation: 4.4.a.3 · weight high. SNMP Agent: Runs on managed device (router/switch). Responds to manager queries. Sends traps on events.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Hierarchical database of manageable objects (OIDs) on a device."
options: SNMP
SNMP Agent
MIB (Management Info Base)
SNMP Manager
questions.explanation: 4.4.a.4 · weight high. MIB (Management Info Base): Hierarchical database of manageable objects (OIDs) on a device. Agent exposes data via MIB.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "161 = manager queries (GET/SET to agent)."
options: MIB (Management Info Base)
SNMP Manager
UDP 161 / UDP 162
SNMP Agent
questions.explanation: 4.4.a.5 · weight high. UDP 161 / UDP 162: 161 = manager queries (GET/SET to agent). 162 = agent traps/informs (to manager).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Manager → Agent queries to retrieve OID values."
options: Inform
Get / GetNext / GetBulk
Trap
Trap vs Inform
questions.explanation: 4.4.b.1 · weight high. Get / GetNext / GetBulk: Manager → Agent queries to retrieve OID values. GetBulk = multiple OIDs in one query (v2c+).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Manager → Agent command to MODIFY a value."
options: Trap vs Inform
Set
Get / GetNext / GetBulk
Inform
questions.explanation: 4.4.b.2 · weight high. Set: Manager → Agent command to MODIFY a value. Requires read-write community/user.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Agent → Manager unsolicited alert."
options: Trap vs Inform
Trap
Get / GetNext / GetBulk
Set
questions.explanation: 4.4.b.3 · weight high. Trap: Agent → Manager unsolicited alert. UDP 162. NOT acknowledged — fire-and-forget (unreliable).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Agent → Manager alert with ACK required."
options: Trap vs Inform
Inform
Trap
Set
questions.explanation: 4.4.b.4 · weight high. Inform: Agent → Manager alert with ACK required. Reliable version of trap. Retransmitted if no ACK.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Both agent-initiated on UDP 162."
options: Inform
Set
Trap vs Inform
Get / GetNext / GetBulk
questions.explanation: 4.4.b.5 · weight high. Trap vs Inform: Both agent-initiated on UDP 162. Trap = fire-and-forget. Inform = reliable (ACK-based).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Plaintext passwords for SNMP access."
options: SNMPv2c community strings
snmp-server community [str] [RO|RW]
v1 vs v2c
Default RO 'public'
questions.explanation: 4.4.c.1 · weight high. SNMPv2c community strings: Plaintext passwords for SNMP access. Two types: read-only (RO) and read-write (RW). Case-sensitive.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Historic default read-only community string."
options: SNMPv2c community strings
snmp-server community [str] [RO|RW]
v1 vs v2c
Default RO 'public'
questions.explanation: 4.4.c.2 · weight high. Default RO 'public': Historic default read-only community string. 'public' / 'private' are textbook defaults — never use in production.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Adds authentication AND encryption to SNMP."
options: SNMPv3
noAuthNoPriv
authPriv
authNoPriv
questions.explanation: 4.4.d.1 · weight high. SNMPv3: Adds authentication AND encryption to SNMP. RECOMMENDED for production. Only secure SNMP version.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "No authentication, no encryption."
options: SNMPv3 algorithms
SNMPv3
noAuthNoPriv
authNoPriv
questions.explanation: 4.4.d.2 · weight high. noAuthNoPriv: No authentication, no encryption. Uses username only. Weakest SNMPv3 level.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Authentication (MD5/SHA) but no encryption."
options: SNMPv3 algorithms
authNoPriv
authPriv
SNMPv3
questions.explanation: 4.4.d.3 · weight high. authNoPriv: Authentication (MD5/SHA) but no encryption. Prevents spoofing but traffic is plaintext.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "BOTH authentication AND encryption."
options: SNMPv3
noAuthNoPriv
authPriv
SNMPv3 algorithms
questions.explanation: 4.4.d.4 · weight high. authPriv: BOTH authentication AND encryption. Strongest level. Auth = MD5/SHA. Encryption = DES/3DES/AES.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Same as v1 (plaintext communities) but adds GetBulk and Inform messages."
options: v2c security
v3 security
v1 security
Which provides encryption?
questions.explanation: 4.4.e.2 · weight high. v2c security: Same as v1 (plaintext communities) but adds GetBulk and Inform messages.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Username-based auth + optional encryption."
options: v2c security
v1 security
v3 security
Which provides encryption?
questions.explanation: 4.4.e.3 · weight high. v3 security: Username-based auth + optional encryption. noAuthNoPriv / authNoPriv / authPriv levels.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "ONLY SNMPv3 (at authPriv level)."
options: v3 security
v2c security
v1 security
Which provides encryption?
questions.explanation: 4.4.e.4 · weight high. Which provides encryption?: ONLY SNMPv3 (at authPriv level). v1 and v2c are always plaintext.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "0 Emergency, 1 Alert, 2 Critical, 3 Error, 4 Warning, 5 Notification, 6 Informational, 7 Debug."
options: Syslog severity 0-7
logging trap [level]
Mnemonic
Syslog UDP 514
questions.explanation: 4.5.a.1 · weight high. Syslog severity 0-7: 0 Emergency, 1 Alert, 2 Critical, 3 Error, 4 Warning, 5 Notification, 6 Informational, 7 Debug.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily.' E-A-C-E-W-N-I-D = 0-1-2-3-4-5-6-7."
options: Mnemonic
Syslog UDP 514
Lower number = more severe
logging trap [level]
questions.explanation: 4.5.a.2 · weight high. Mnemonic: 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily.' E-A-C-E-W-N-I-D = 0-1-2-3-4-5-6-7.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "0 = most critical (emergency)."
options: Syslog UDP 514
Lower number = more severe
Syslog severity 0-7
Mnemonic
questions.explanation: 4.5.a.3 · weight high. Lower number = more severe: 0 = most critical (emergency). 7 = most verbose (debug). Severity INVERSELY related to number.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Captures that level AND MORE SEVERE (lower)."
options: Syslog severity 0-7
logging trap [level]
Syslog UDP 514
Lower number = more severe
questions.explanation: 4.5.a.4 · weight high. logging trap [level]: Captures that level AND MORE SEVERE (lower). trap 4 = levels 0-4 (emergency through warning).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Destination port for syslog messages."
options: logging trap [level]
Mnemonic
Syslog severity 0-7
Syslog UDP 514
questions.explanation: 4.5.a.5 · weight high. Syslog UDP 514: Destination port for syslog messages. Remember: UDP (not TCP), port 514.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "%FACILITY-SEVERITY-MNEMONIC: message."
options: Lower number = more severe
3 Error
2 Critical
Syslog message format
questions.explanation: 4.5.c.2 · weight high. Syslog message format: %FACILITY-SEVERITY-MNEMONIC: message. Example: %LINK-3-UPDOWN. Middle number = severity.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Sends levels 0-4 (Emergency through Warning) to syslog server."
options: 4 Warning
logging trap 4
Syslog severity 0-7
Brain dump on whiteboard
questions.explanation: 4.5.e.2 · weight high. logging trap 4: Sends levels 0-4 (Emergency through Warning) to syslog server. Common production setting.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Mnemonic for 0-7: Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning, Notification, Informational, Debug."
options: Brain dump on whiteboard
1 Alert
'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily'
logging trap [level]
questions.explanation: 4.5.i.1 · weight high. 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily': Mnemonic for 0-7: Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning, Notification, Informational, Debug.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Write syslog severities + AD values + subnet chart FIRST at exam start."
options: 2 Critical
'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily'
Brain dump on whiteboard
logging trap [level]
questions.explanation: 4.5.i.2 · weight high. Brain dump on whiteboard: Write syslog severities + AD values + subnet chart FIRST at exam start. Frees mental space for questions.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Console, VTY terminal, buffer (RAM), syslog server."
options: Four logging destinations
Syslog server logging
Buffer logging
Console logging
questions.explanation: 4.5.j.1 · weight high. Four logging destinations: Console, VTY terminal, buffer (RAM), syslog server. Each has its own severity filter.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'logging [server-ip]' + 'logging trap [level]'."
options: Syslog server logging
Buffer logging
Terminal (VTY) logging
Four logging destinations
questions.explanation: 4.5.j.5 · weight high. Syslog server logging: 'logging [server-ip]' + 'logging trap [level]'. Survives reboot. UDP 514. Best for production.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Set syslog server destination."
options: logging trap [level]
service timestamps log datetime
logging host / logging [ip]
service sequence-numbers
questions.explanation: 4.5.k.1 · weight high. logging host / logging [ip]: Set syslog server destination. Required first step for server logging.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Filter messages sent to syslog server."
options: logging host / logging [ip]
logging trap [level]
service timestamps log datetime
service sequence-numbers
questions.explanation: 4.5.k.2 · weight high. logging trap [level]: Filter messages sent to syslog server. Captures that level + all more severe (lower numbers).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Best practice. Adds date/time to every log message."
options: service sequence-numbers
service timestamps log datetime
logging trap [level]
logging host / logging [ip]
questions.explanation: 4.5.k.3 · weight high. service timestamps log datetime: Best practice. Adds date/time to every log message. Critical for correlation.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Interface command. Makes the router interface a DHCP CLIENT."
options: Centralized DHCP design
Router as DHCP client
Broadcasts don't cross routers
ip address dhcp
questions.explanation: 4.6.a.1 · weight high. ip address dhcp: Interface command. Makes the router interface a DHCP CLIENT. Gets IP from a DHCP server.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Used for WAN interface facing ISP."
options: Full DHCP server recipe
Order matters?
ip address dhcp
Router as DHCP client
questions.explanation: 4.6.a.2 · weight high. Router as DHCP client: Used for WAN interface facing ISP. ISP runs DHCP; router gets public IP dynamically.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Interface command. Forwards DHCP broadcasts as unicast to the server IP."
options: ip helper-address [server-ip]
Centralized DHCP design
Configure on client-facing
Points to SERVER's IP
questions.explanation: 4.6.b.1 · weight high. ip helper-address [server-ip]: Interface command. Forwards DHCP broadcasts as unicast to the server IP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Apply to the interface that the client uses (the router's LAN-side SVI, not the server-side)."
options: Exclude range syntax
Configure on client-facing
ip helper-address [server-ip]
Points to SERVER's IP
questions.explanation: 4.6.b.2 · weight high. Configure on client-facing: Apply to the interface that the client uses (the router's LAN-side SVI, not the server-side).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "The IP you enter in 'ip helper-address' is the DHCP server's address, not the client's."
options: Points to SERVER's IP
ip dhcp excluded-address
Configure on client-facing
ip helper-address [server-ip]
questions.explanation: 4.6.b.3 · weight high. Points to SERVER's IP: The IP you enter in 'ip helper-address' is the DHCP server's address, not the client's.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Routers drop broadcasts by default."
options: DHCP relay agent
Broadcasts don't cross routers
ip address dhcp
Centralized DHCP design
questions.explanation: 4.6.c.1 · weight high. Broadcasts don't cross routers: Routers drop broadcasts by default. DHCP Discover is a broadcast — won't reach server on another subnet.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Router converts client broadcast to unicast to the DHCP server."
options: DHCP relay agent
show ip dhcp binding
Broadcasts don't cross routers
Centralized DHCP design
questions.explanation: 4.6.c.2 · weight high. DHCP relay agent: Router converts client broadcast to unicast to the DHCP server. Essential for centralized DHCP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "1) exclude gateway/static IPs, 2) pool NAME, 3) network, 4) default-router, 5) dns-server, 6) lease."
options: Order matters?
Points to SERVER's IP
Full DHCP server recipe
Cisco router as DHCP server
questions.explanation: 4.6.d.1 · weight high. Full DHCP server recipe: 1) exclude gateway/static IPs, 2) pool NAME, 3) network, 4) default-router, 5) dns-server, 6) lease.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Full DHCP pool support built in."
options: Cisco router as DHCP server
Full DHCP server recipe
show ip dhcp pool
Order matters?
questions.explanation: 4.6.d.3 · weight high. Cisco router as DHCP server: Full DHCP pool support built in. Can serve multiple subnets (multiple pools).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "GLOBAL command (NOT inside pool config)."
options: ip address dhcp
Always exclude gateway
ip dhcp excluded-address
Exclude range syntax
questions.explanation: 4.6.e.1 · weight high. ip dhcp excluded-address: GLOBAL command (NOT inside pool config). Reserves IPs so DHCP won't assign them.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "The router's own gateway IP is usually at .1."
options: Always exclude gateway
ip dhcp excluded-address
Points to SERVER's IP
Exclude range syntax
questions.explanation: 4.6.e.2 · weight high. Always exclude gateway: The router's own gateway IP is usually at .1. Must exclude or DHCP could assign it to a client (conflict).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Shows active DHCP leases: IP, client MAC, lease expiration."
options: DHCP relay agent
show ip dhcp pool
show ip dhcp conflict
show ip dhcp binding
questions.explanation: 4.6.f.1 · weight high. show ip dhcp binding: Shows active DHCP leases: IP, client MAC, lease expiration. Exam often reads from this output.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Classification → Marking → Queuing → Policing/Shaping → Congestion avoidance."
options: Marking
Classification
Per-Hop Behavior (PHB)
QoS processing order
questions.explanation: 4.7.a.1 · weight high. QoS processing order: Classification → Marking → Queuing → Policing/Shaping → Congestion avoidance. Memorize the order.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Identify traffic type (voice, video, data)."
options: Per-Hop Behavior (PHB)
QoS processing order
Marking
Classification
questions.explanation: 4.7.a.2 · weight high. Classification: Identify traffic type (voice, video, data). Based on ACL, protocol, port, or deep packet inspection.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Tag frames/packets with QoS value (CoS, DSCP)."
options: Classification
Per-Hop Behavior (PHB)
Marking
CCNA QoS scope = concepts only
questions.explanation: 4.7.a.3 · weight high. Marking: Tag frames/packets with QoS value (CoS, DSCP). Subsequent hops use the mark to prioritize.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "No config tested. Know terminology, order, and what each component does."
options: CCNA QoS scope = concepts only
Per-Hop Behavior (PHB)
Classification
QoS processing order
questions.explanation: 4.7.a.5 · weight high. CCNA QoS scope = concepts only: No config tested. Know terminology, order, and what each component does.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L2 marking. 3 bits in 802.1Q tag (PCP field)."
options: IP Precedence (legacy)
CoS vs DSCP
CoS (Class of Service)
DSCP
questions.explanation: 4.7.b.1 · weight high. CoS (Class of Service): L2 marking. 3 bits in 802.1Q tag (PCP field). 8 values (0-7). Lost at L3 boundaries.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L3 marking. 6 bits in IP header (ToS field)."
options: CoS (Class of Service)
CoS vs DSCP
DSCP
IP Precedence (legacy)
questions.explanation: 4.7.b.2 · weight high. DSCP: L3 marking. 6 bits in IP header (ToS field). 64 values. SURVIVES across L3 hops.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "CoS = L2 only. DSCP = L3, end-to-end."
options: DSCP
CoS (Class of Service)
CoS vs DSCP
IP Precedence (legacy)
questions.explanation: 4.7.b.3 · weight high. CoS vs DSCP: CoS = L2 only. DSCP = L3, end-to-end. For end-to-end QoS across routed networks, use DSCP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DSCP value 46. Used for VOICE traffic (VoIP)."
options: CS (Class Selector)
Default DSCP 0
AF (Assured Forwarding)
EF (Expedited Forwarding)
questions.explanation: 4.7.c.1 · weight high. EF (Expedited Forwarding): DSCP value 46. Used for VOICE traffic (VoIP). Lowest latency, strict priority.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing."
options: LLQ (Low Latency Queuing)
FIFO
Tail drop
CBWFQ
questions.explanation: 4.7.d.2 · weight high. CBWFQ: Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing. Guarantees minimum bandwidth per class. Used for video and data.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Weighted Random Early Detection."
options: TCP global synchronization
WRED vs tail drop
CoS vs DSCP
WRED
questions.explanation: 4.7.e.1 · weight high. WRED: Weighted Random Early Detection. Drops packets proactively BEFORE queue is full. Selective by class.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Problem WRED solves. Many TCP flows hit tail drop simultaneously → all back off in unison → bandwidth wasted."
options: AF (Assured Forwarding)
TCP global synchronization
WRED
WRED vs tail drop
questions.explanation: 4.7.e.2 · weight high. TCP global synchronization: Problem WRED solves. Many TCP flows hit tail drop simultaneously → all back off in unison → bandwidth wasted.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "WRED = proactive, selective, class-aware."
options: WRED vs tail drop
WRED
FIFO
TCP global synchronization
questions.explanation: 4.7.e.3 · weight high. WRED vs tail drop: WRED = proactive, selective, class-aware. Tail drop = reactive, indiscriminate, at-the-brink.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DROPS or re-marks excess traffic."
options: Shaping
Policing vs shaping
Policing
WRED vs tail drop
questions.explanation: 4.7.f.1 · weight high. Policing: DROPS or re-marks excess traffic. Hard limit. Bursty output. Can be applied at ingress OR egress.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "BUFFERS excess traffic and releases at a smooth rate."
options: Shaping
WRED
Policing vs shaping
Policing
questions.explanation: 4.7.f.2 · weight high. Shaping: BUFFERS excess traffic and releases at a smooth rate. Soft limit. Egress only. Adds latency.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Buffering = delay. BAD for voice/video."
options: Policing doesn't add latency
Typical use cases
Shaping adds latency
WRED
questions.explanation: 4.7.g.1 · weight high. Shaping adds latency: Buffering = delay. BAD for voice/video. Use on WAN links that aggregate traffic.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Drops immediately. Good for enforcing strict caps."
options: Shaping adds latency
FIFO
Policing doesn't add latency
Typical use cases
questions.explanation: 4.7.g.2 · weight high. Policing doesn't add latency: Drops immediately. Good for enforcing strict caps. OK for voice (drops bad voice packets fast).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Demarcation where the network starts trusting incoming QoS markings."
options: Default DSCP 0
Why it matters
QoS trust boundary
Placement rule
questions.explanation: 4.7.h.1 · weight high. QoS trust boundary: Demarcation where the network starts trusting incoming QoS markings. Usually the access-layer switch.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "As close to source as possible."
options: Placement rule
Why it matters
Typical use cases
QoS trust boundary
questions.explanation: 4.7.h.2 · weight high. Placement rule: As close to source as possible. Access switches mark traffic; core just honors markings.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Untrusted endpoints could mark their own traffic as EF (voice priority) and abuse QoS."
options: QoS trust boundary
AF (Assured Forwarding)
Placement rule
Why it matters
questions.explanation: 4.7.h.3 · weight high. Why it matters: Untrusted endpoints could mark their own traffic as EF (voice priority) and abuse QoS. Boundary prevents that.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "1) hostname, 2) domain-name, 3) RSA key, 4) user, 5) VTY config, 6) version 2."
options: SSHv2 minimum 768 bits
transport input all vs ssh
All 6 steps required
SSH 6-step recipe
questions.explanation: 4.8.a.1 · weight high. SSH 6-step recipe: 1) hostname, 2) domain-name, 3) RSA key, 4) user, 5) VTY config, 6) version 2.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Both required BEFORE RSA key generation."
options: hostname + domain-name
crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048
Default 'Router' hostname issue
SSHv2 minimum 768 bits
questions.explanation: 4.8.b.1 · weight high. hostname + domain-name: Both required BEFORE RSA key generation. RSA key name = hostname.domain-name.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "If hostname is still 'Router' (default), key generation fails or produces weak key."
options: Why enforce v2
username [name] secret [pass]
Default 'Router' hostname issue
hostname + domain-name
questions.explanation: 4.8.b.2 · weight high. Default 'Router' hostname issue: If hostname is still 'Router' (default), key generation fails or produces weak key. Change hostname first.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Global command. Forces SSHv2 only."
options: ip ssh version 2
Why enforce v2
Telnet plaintext risk
hostname + domain-name
questions.explanation: 4.8.d.1 · weight high. ip ssh version 2: Global command. Forces SSHv2 only. Rejects insecure SSHv1.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Creates local user with HASHED password (MD5/SCRYPT)."
options: All 6 steps required
secret vs password
SSH 6-step recipe
username [name] secret [pass]
questions.explanation: 4.8.e.1 · weight high. username [name] secret [pass]: Creates local user with HASHED password (MD5/SCRYPT). Use THIS, not 'password'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Enters config for ALL 16 VTY lines."
options: login local
line vty 0 15
secret vs password
transport input ssh
