questions.explanation: 4.8.f.1 · weight high. line vty 0 15: Enters config for ALL 16 VTY lines. Modern best practice (was 'line vty 0 4' on older IOS).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Uses local username/secret database."
options: line vty 0 15
login local
transport input ssh
Telnet plaintext risk
questions.explanation: 4.8.f.3 · weight high. login local: Uses local username/secret database. Alternative: 'login authentication default' for AAA.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Telnet transmits credentials IN CLEARTEXT."
options: transport input all vs ssh
Why enforce v2
Telnet plaintext risk
SSHv2 minimum 768 bits
questions.explanation: 4.8.g.2 · weight high. Telnet plaintext risk: Telnet transmits credentials IN CLEARTEXT. Anyone sniffing sees passwords. Never use in production.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Shows SSH version, auth timeout, retry count."
options: show ip ssh
Telnet plaintext risk
show ssh
SSH 6-step recipe
questions.explanation: 4.8.h.1 · weight high. show ip ssh: Shows SSH version, auth timeout, retry count. Verifies SSH is enabled and configured.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Trivial FTP. UDP 69. NO authentication."
options: TFTP
FTP
SCP
copy to startup-config = REPLACE
questions.explanation: 4.9.a.1 · weight high. TFTP: Trivial FTP. UDP 69. NO authentication. Simple. Used for IOS images and config transfer.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Secure Copy. TCP 22 (uses SSH)."
options: SCP
FTP
SCP (Secure Copy)
TFTP
questions.explanation: 4.9.a.3 · weight high. SCP: Secure Copy. TCP 22 (uses SSH). Encrypted transfer WITH authentication. Most secure file transfer.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Control channel — login, commands (ls, get, put)."
options: SCP (Secure Copy)
FTP TCP 20 (data)
FTP TCP 21 (control)
FTP credentials cleartext
questions.explanation: 4.9.b.1 · weight high. FTP TCP 21 (control): Control channel — login, commands (ls, get, put). Session stays open throughout.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "FTP has auth but no encryption."
options: ip scp server enable
FTP TCP 21 (control)
FTP TCP 20 (data)
FTP credentials cleartext
questions.explanation: 4.9.b.3 · weight high. FTP credentials cleartext: FTP has auth but no encryption. Username/password visible on wire. Use SFTP/SCP in production.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'copy [source]: [destination]:'."
options: FTP TCP 21 (control)
copy command syntax
Source/dest keywords
SCP
questions.explanation: 4.9.c.1 · weight high. copy command syntax: 'copy [source]: [destination]:'. Colons required. Examples: 'copy tftp: flash:', 'copy running-config startup-config'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "tftp:, ftp:, scp:, flash:, running-config, startup-config, nvram:."
options: FTP TCP 21 (control)
Source/dest keywords
copy command syntax
SCP
questions.explanation: 4.9.c.2 · weight high. Source/dest keywords: tftp:, ftp:, scp:, flash:, running-config, startup-config, nvram:. Standard IOS file system locations.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Classic use case. 'copy tftp: flash:' transfers new IOS."
options: show flash:
Most secure = SCP
TFTP for IOS images
File transfer comparison
questions.explanation: 4.9.d.1 · weight high. TFTP for IOS images: Classic use case. 'copy tftp: flash:' transfers new IOS. TFTP chosen for simplicity (no auth needed).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Exam answer to 'most secure file transfer'."
options: Most secure = SCP
TFTP for IOS images
File transfer comparison
show flash:
questions.explanation: 4.9.d.2 · weight high. Most secure = SCP: Exam answer to 'most secure file transfer'. Uses SSH for auth + encryption.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "TFTP (UDP 69, no auth) → FTP (TCP 21/20, plain auth) → SCP (TCP 22, encrypted + auth)."
options: TFTP
TFTP for IOS images
Most secure = SCP
File transfer comparison
questions.explanation: 4.9.d.3 · weight high. File transfer comparison: TFTP (UDP 69, no auth) → FTP (TCP 21/20, plain auth) → SCP (TCP 22, encrypted + auth).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'copy tftp: running-config' MERGES incoming config with current."
options: show flash:
ip scp server enable
copy to running-config = MERGE
copy to startup-config = REPLACE
questions.explanation: 4.9.e.1 · weight high. copy to running-config = MERGE: 'copy tftp: running-config' MERGES incoming config with current. Doesn't delete existing commands.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'copy tftp: startup-config' REPLACES startup-config entirely."
options: copy to startup-config = REPLACE
show flash:
TFTP
copy to running-config = MERGE
questions.explanation: 4.9.e.2 · weight high. copy to startup-config = REPLACE: 'copy tftp: startup-config' REPLACES startup-config entirely. Changes apply after reboot.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Lists contents of flash memory."
options: copy to running-config = MERGE
show flash:
FTP TCP 21 (control)
copy to startup-config = REPLACE
questions.explanation: 4.9.e.3 · weight high. show flash:: Lists contents of flash memory. Used to verify IOS image after 'copy tftp: flash:'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Uses SSH for auth + encryption."
options: SCP (Secure Copy)
FTP TCP 21 (control)
ip scp server enable
SFTP vs SCP
questions.explanation: 4.9.f.1 · weight high. SCP (Secure Copy): Uses SSH for auth + encryption. Most secure file transfer. TCP 22 (same as SSH).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Threat exploits Vulnerability using Exploit → Impact."
options: Threat
DDoS
Attack chain vocab
Threat categories
questions.explanation: 5.1.a.3 · weight high. Attack chain vocab: Threat exploits Vulnerability using Exploit → Impact. Know each term's role.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Software (unpatched), config (defaults, open ports), design (weak crypto), human (weak passwords), physical (open rooms)."
options: Risk formula
Vulnerability
Attack chain vocab
Common vulnerability types
questions.explanation: 5.1.b.2 · weight high. Common vulnerability types: Software (unpatched), config (defaults, open ports), design (weak crypto), human (weak passwords), physical (open rooms).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "The technique/tool/code to take advantage of a vulnerability."
options: Zero-day exploit
Buffer overflow
SQL injection / XSS
Exploit
questions.explanation: 5.1.c.1 · weight high. Exploit: The technique/tool/code to take advantage of a vulnerability. ACTION, not weakness.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Attack against a vulnerability with NO patch available."
options: SQL injection / XSS
Buffer overflow
Zero-day exploit
Exploit
questions.explanation: 5.1.c.3 · weight high. Zero-day exploit: Attack against a vulnerability with NO patch available. Extremely dangerous — no defense exists yet.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Action reducing likelihood/impact of threat."
options: Control categories
Phishing → training
ARP spoofing → DAI
Mitigation / Countermeasure
questions.explanation: 5.1.d.1 · weight high. Mitigation / Countermeasure: Action reducing likelihood/impact of threat. Reduces risk to acceptable level — doesn't eliminate.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Preventive (firewall, ACL), Detective (IDS, syslog), Corrective (backup, IR), Deterrent (banner, cameras)."
options: Rogue DHCP → DHCP snooping
Control categories
ARP spoofing → DAI
Phishing → training
questions.explanation: 5.1.d.2 · weight high. Control categories: Preventive (firewall, ACL), Detective (IDS, syslog), Corrective (backup, IR), Deterrent (banner, cameras).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Dynamic ARP Inspection uses DHCP snooping binding table to validate ARP replies."
options: Mitigation / Countermeasure
Control categories
Phishing → training
ARP spoofing → DAI
questions.explanation: 5.1.d.3 · weight high. ARP spoofing → DAI: Dynamic ARP Inspection uses DHCP snooping binding table to validate ARP replies.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Only trusted ports (toward legitimate server) can send DHCP offers."
options: Rogue DHCP → DHCP snooping
Control categories
ARP spoofing → DAI
Phishing → training
questions.explanation: 5.1.d.4 · weight high. Rogue DHCP → DHCP snooping: Only trusted ports (toward legitimate server) can send DHCP offers. Blocks rogue servers.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Technology can't fully defend."
options: Mitigation / Countermeasure
Control categories
ARP spoofing → DAI
Phishing → training
questions.explanation: 5.1.d.5 · weight high. Phishing → training: Technology can't fully defend. User awareness training is the primary countermeasure.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Manipulating humans to reveal credentials or bypass security."
options: Phishing
Social engineering
Spear phishing
User awareness training
questions.explanation: 5.1.e.1 · weight high. Social engineering: Manipulating humans to reveal credentials or bypass security. Bypasses technical controls entirely.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Primary defense against social engineering."
options: User awareness training
Social engineering
Phishing
Spear phishing
questions.explanation: 5.1.e.4 · weight high. User awareness training: Primary defense against social engineering. Technology alone cannot stop it.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Denial of Service. Overwhelms target from a single source."
options: DoS
Reflection/amplification
SYN flood
DDoS
questions.explanation: 5.1.f.1 · weight high. DoS: Denial of Service. Overwhelms target from a single source. Blockable by ACL or firewall rule.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Distributed DoS. Many sources (botnet) attack simultaneously."
options: DoS
SYN flood
Reflection/amplification
DDoS
questions.explanation: 5.1.f.2 · weight high. DDoS: Distributed DoS. Many sources (botnet) attack simultaneously. Needs scrubbing centers/CDN defense.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Flood TCP SYNs without completing handshake."
options: DoS
Reflection/amplification
DDoS
SYN flood
questions.explanation: 5.1.f.3 · weight high. SYN flood: Flood TCP SYNs without completing handshake. Target's half-open connection queue exhausts.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Attacker inserts between two communicating parties, intercepting and/or modifying traffic."
options: DHCP spoofing
Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)
ARP spoofing
MITM mitigations
questions.explanation: 5.1.g.1 · weight high. Man-in-the-Middle (MITM): Attacker inserts between two communicating parties, intercepting and/or modifying traffic.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Attacker sends forged ARP replies."
options: ARP spoofing
MITM mitigations
Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)
DHCP spoofing
questions.explanation: 5.1.g.2 · weight high. ARP spoofing: Attacker sends forged ARP replies. Hosts ARP-cache the attacker's MAC for other IPs. Traffic flows through attacker.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Rogue DHCP server gives victims attacker's IP as gateway/DNS."
options: DHCP spoofing
MITM mitigations
Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)
ARP spoofing
questions.explanation: 5.1.g.3 · weight high. DHCP spoofing: Rogue DHCP server gives victims attacker's IP as gateway/DNS. Attacker becomes MITM for all outbound traffic.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DAI (stops ARP spoof), DHCP snooping (stops rogue DHCP), HTTPS/VPN (encrypts even if intercepted)."
options: MITM mitigations
DHCP spoofing
ARP spoofing
Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)
questions.explanation: 5.1.g.4 · weight high. MITM mitigations: DAI (stops ARP spoof), DHCP snooping (stops rogue DHCP), HTTPS/VPN (encrypts even if intercepted).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Malware that attaches to legit files."
options: Spyware / Adware
Trojan
Worm
Virus
questions.explanation: 5.1.h.1 · weight high. Virus: Malware that attaches to legit files. Requires USER ACTION (open file) to spread.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Malware disguised as legitimate software."
options: Trojan
Worm
Ransomware
Virus
questions.explanation: 5.1.h.3 · weight high. Trojan: Malware disguised as legitimate software. Relies on DECEPTION. User installs willingly.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Encrypts victim's files, demands payment for decryption key."
options: Worm
Virus
Spyware / Adware
Ransomware
questions.explanation: 5.1.h.4 · weight high. Ransomware: Encrypts victim's files, demands payment for decryption key. Major modern threat.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "General security education for all users."
options: Equipment locks / cages
Awareness beats tech for SE
User awareness
Simulated phishing
questions.explanation: 5.2.a.1 · weight high. User awareness: General security education for all users. Phishing recognition, password hygiene, acceptable use.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Social engineering bypasses technology."
options: Tailgating
User awareness
Simulated phishing
Awareness beats tech for SE
questions.explanation: 5.2.a.2 · weight high. Awareness beats tech for SE: Social engineering bypasses technology. Trained users are the primary defense.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Technical/role-based skill building."
options: Awareness vs training
Training (role-specific)
Awareness beats tech for SE
Equipment locks / cages
questions.explanation: 5.2.b.1 · weight high. Training (role-specific): Technical/role-based skill building. IT admins, incident responders. Deeper than awareness.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Awareness = general users (recognize phishing)."
options: Security vestibule (mantrap)
Surveillance cameras
Training (role-specific)
Awareness vs training
questions.explanation: 5.2.b.2 · weight high. Awareness vs training: Awareness = general users (recognize phishing). Training = IT/technical staff (how to respond to incidents).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Two-door airlock. One opens only after the other closes."
options: Security vestibule (mantrap)
Awareness beats tech for SE
Tailgating
Badge access
questions.explanation: 5.2.c.1 · weight high. Security vestibule (mantrap): Two-door airlock. One opens only after the other closes. Prevents tailgating. Cisco's preferred modern term.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Unauthorized person follows authorized person through access-controlled door."
options: Badge access
Tailgating
Security vestibule (mantrap)
Awareness beats tech for SE
questions.explanation: 5.2.c.2 · weight high. Tailgating: Unauthorized person follows authorized person through access-controlled door. Security vestibule stops this.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Sets priv-exec password with HASH (Type 5 MD5 or Type 8/9)."
options: secret ALWAYS wins
enable password
enable secret
Type 0 = plaintext
questions.explanation: 5.3.a.1 · weight high. enable secret: Sets priv-exec password with HASH (Type 5 MD5 or Type 8/9). Preferred. Cannot be reversed.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "LEGACY. Stores password in plaintext (Type 0) unless service password-encryption."
options: Type 0 = plaintext
enable password
enable secret
secret ALWAYS wins
questions.explanation: 5.3.a.2 · weight high. enable password: LEGACY. Stores password in plaintext (Type 0) unless service password-encryption. WEAK.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "If both 'enable secret' and 'enable password' are set, 'secret' is used."
options: Type 0 = plaintext
secret ALWAYS wins
enable password
enable secret
questions.explanation: 5.3.a.3 · weight high. secret ALWAYS wins: If both 'enable secret' and 'enable password' are set, 'secret' is used. 'password' is ignored.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Unencrypted in running config."
options: enable password
secret ALWAYS wins
enable secret
Type 0 = plaintext
questions.explanation: 5.3.a.4 · weight high. Type 0 = plaintext: Unencrypted in running config. Visible with 'show running-config'. Avoid in production.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "MD5 hash. One-way, but MD5 is now considered weak by modern standards."
options: Type 0
Type 9
Type 5
Type 7
questions.explanation: 5.3.b.2 · weight high. Type 5: MD5 hash. One-way, but MD5 is now considered weak by modern standards.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Vigenère cipher. REVERSIBLE — can be decrypted easily with online tools."
options: Type 5
Type 8
Type 7
Type 9
questions.explanation: 5.3.b.3 · weight high. Type 7: Vigenère cipher. REVERSIBLE — can be decrypted easily with online tools. Only applied by 'service password-encryption'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "PBKDF2 with SHA-256. Strong modern hash."
options: Type 7
Type 0
Type 5
Type 8
questions.explanation: 5.3.b.4 · weight high. Type 8: PBKDF2 with SHA-256. Strong modern hash. Use when available.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "scrypt. Strongest current Cisco password hash."
options: Type 5
Type 7
Type 9
Type 0
questions.explanation: 5.3.b.5 · weight high. Type 9: scrypt. Strongest current Cisco password hash. Memory-hard, resistant to GPU attacks.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "line console 0 → password [pw] → login."
options: Console password config
logging synchronous
exec-timeout
'login' gotcha
questions.explanation: 5.3.c.1 · weight high. Console password config: line console 0 → password [pw] → login. Critical: 'login' is required or password is NOT enforced.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Password set but no 'login' = password is ignored."
options: Console password config
logging synchronous
'login' gotcha
exec-timeout
questions.explanation: 5.3.c.2 · weight high. 'login' gotcha: Password set but no 'login' = password is ignored. Classic exam trap question.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "line vty 0 15 → password [pw] → login (or login local)."
options: access-class vs access-group
VTY password config
access-class (VTY)
No password = no access
questions.explanation: 5.3.d.1 · weight high. VTY password config: line vty 0 15 → password [pw] → login (or login local). Password protects remote access.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "If VTY has no password configured, remote login is REJECTED by default on IOS."
options: No password = no access
VTY password config
access-class vs access-group
access-class (VTY)
questions.explanation: 5.3.d.2 · weight high. No password = no access: If VTY has no password configured, remote login is REJECTED by default on IOS. Access denied.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Apply ACL to VTY to restrict which IPs can connect: 'access-class [ACL] in'."
options: access-class (VTY)
No password = no access
VTY password config
access-class vs access-group
questions.explanation: 5.3.d.3 · weight high. access-class (VTY): Apply ACL to VTY to restrict which IPs can connect: 'access-class [ACL] in'. Use for management IP restrictions.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Creates local user with hashed password."
options: login local
login block-for
username X secret Y
privilege 15
questions.explanation: 5.3.f.1 · weight high. username X secret Y: Creates local user with hashed password. Preferred over 'username X password Y' (plaintext).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "On line (VTY/console) — activates local user database."
options: login local
privilege 15
username X secret Y
Enforcement scope
questions.explanation: 5.3.f.2 · weight high. login local: On line (VTY/console) — activates local user database. Without it, local usernames are ignored.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Max privilege level. 'username admin privilege 15 secret X' gives instant enable-mode access on login."
options: login local
enable secret
username X secret Y
privilege 15
questions.explanation: 5.3.f.3 · weight high. privilege 15: Max privilege level. 'username admin privilege 15 secret X' gives instant enable-mode access on login.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Applies Type 7 (Vigenère — REVERSIBLE) to plaintext passwords."
options: service password-encryption
Type 7 weakness
secret ALWAYS wins
Always prefer 'secret'
questions.explanation: 5.3.g.1 · weight high. service password-encryption: Applies Type 7 (Vigenère — REVERSIBLE) to plaintext passwords. Not real security — only obscures casual viewing.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Vigenère cipher. Reversible with online Cisco Type 7 decoders."
options: service password-encryption
privilege 15
Type 7 weakness
Always prefer 'secret'
questions.explanation: 5.3.g.2 · weight high. Type 7 weakness: Vigenère cipher. Reversible with online Cisco Type 7 decoders. Same password always produces same cipher.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Use 'enable secret' and 'username X secret Y'."
options: service password-encryption
Type 7 weakness
enable secret
Always prefer 'secret'
questions.explanation: 5.3.g.3 · weight high. Always prefer 'secret': Use 'enable secret' and 'username X secret Y'. Both use one-way hashing, can't be reversed.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "MOTD → Login banner → password prompt → EXEC banner."
options: Banner display order
MOTD banner content
Parameter order
banner motd # text #
questions.explanation: 5.3.h.1 · weight high. Banner display order: MOTD → Login banner → password prompt → EXEC banner. MOTD first, EXEC last (post-login).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Should contain LEGAL NOTICE (unauthorized access prohibited)."
options: No password = no access
Banner display order
banner motd # text #
MOTD banner content
questions.explanation: 5.3.h.2 · weight high. MOTD banner content: Should contain LEGAL NOTICE (unauthorized access prohibited). NEVER 'Welcome' or device info.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Global command. Enforces minimum length for NEW passwords."
options: security passwords min-length
Enforcement scope
'login' gotcha
login block-for
questions.explanation: 5.3.i.1 · weight high. security passwords min-length: Global command. Enforces minimum length for NEW passwords. Does NOT affect existing passwords.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Global command. Throttles login attempts."
options: login block-for
AUX port
Block-for example
Parameter order
questions.explanation: 5.3.j.1 · weight high. login block-for: Global command. Throttles login attempts. Syntax: 'login block-for [sec] attempts [N] within [sec]'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60' = block login for 120s after 3 failed attempts in 60s."
options: Parameter order
Block-for example
login block-for
enable secret
questions.explanation: 5.3.j.2 · weight high. Block-for example: 'login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60' = block login for 120s after 3 failed attempts in 60s.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "block-for THEN attempts THEN within."
options: Block-for example
Parameter order
Type 8
login block-for
questions.explanation: 5.3.j.3 · weight high. Parameter order: block-for THEN attempts THEN within. Memorize the exact order — exam tests this.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Mix of upper/lower case, numbers, symbols."
options: Traditional rotation (90 days)
Password length
NIST guidance
Password complexity
questions.explanation: 5.4.a.1 · weight high. Password complexity: Mix of upper/lower case, numbers, symbols. IOS doesn't enforce; AAA servers (ISE) do.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Longer = stronger. IOS enforces with 'security passwords min-length'."
options: Password length
Traditional rotation (90 days)
Password complexity
NIST guidance
questions.explanation: 5.4.a.2 · weight high. Password length: Longer = stronger. IOS enforces with 'security passwords min-length'. Longer+complex = strongest.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Authentication using 2+ factors from DIFFERENT categories."
options: MFA trap (same category)
MFA (Multi-Factor Auth)
Password complexity
Three factor categories
questions.explanation: 5.4.c.1 · weight high. MFA (Multi-Factor Auth): Authentication using 2+ factors from DIFFERENT categories. Something you know + have + are.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Know (password, PIN, security question)."
options: MFA trap (same category)
MFA (Multi-Factor Auth)
Three factor categories
Password length
questions.explanation: 5.4.c.2 · weight high. Three factor categories: Know (password, PIN, security question). Have (token, phone, smartcard). Are (biometric).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Password + PIN = NOT MFA (both 'know')."
options: NIST guidance
MFA trap (same category)
MFA (Multi-Factor Auth)
Three factor categories
questions.explanation: 5.4.c.3 · weight high. MFA trap (same category): Password + PIN = NOT MFA (both 'know'). Password + security question = NOT MFA. Must be different categories.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "MFA requires factors from DIFFERENT categories."
options: Traditional rotation (90 days)
Valid MFA examples
Different categories required
Biometric disadvantage
questions.explanation: 5.4.d.1 · weight high. Different categories required: MFA requires factors from DIFFERENT categories. Not just 'two things' — specifically different types.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Password + TOTP token (know + have)."
options: MFA (Multi-Factor Auth)
Valid MFA examples
Different categories required
Traditional rotation (90 days)
questions.explanation: 5.4.d.2 · weight high. Valid MFA examples: Password + TOTP token (know + have). Fingerprint + card (are + have). Password + fingerprint (know + are).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Electronic document binding a public key to an identity."
options: PKI
Certificate Authority (CA)
X.509 contents
Digital certificate (X.509)
questions.explanation: 5.4.e.1 · weight high. Digital certificate (X.509): Electronic document binding a public key to an identity. Signed by a CA. Used for HTTPS, SSL/TLS, IPsec.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Trusted third party that signs certificates."
options: Digital certificate (X.509)
Certificate Authority (CA)
PKI
X.509 contents
questions.explanation: 5.4.e.2 · weight high. Certificate Authority (CA): Trusted third party that signs certificates. Chain of trust: root CA → intermediate CA → end-entity cert.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Subject (owner), Issuer (CA), public key, validity (not-before/not-after), serial number, signature."
options: Digital certificate (X.509)
X.509 contents
PKI
Certificate Authority (CA)
questions.explanation: 5.4.e.3 · weight high. X.509 contents: Subject (owner), Issuer (CA), public key, validity (not-before/not-after), serial number, signature.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'Something you ARE.' Fingerprint, iris, facial recognition, voice."
options: Different categories required
Biometric disadvantage
Biometrics
FAR / FRR / CER
questions.explanation: 5.4.f.1 · weight high. Biometrics: 'Something you ARE.' Fingerprint, iris, facial recognition, voice. Physical characteristics.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "CANNOT BE CHANGED if compromised."
options: FAR / FRR / CER
Digital certificate (X.509)
Biometric disadvantage
Biometrics
questions.explanation: 5.4.f.2 · weight high. Biometric disadvantage: CANNOT BE CHANGED if compromised. Unlike passwords, you can't reset your fingerprint.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Always-on router-to-router (or firewall-to-firewall)."
options: Confidentiality (IPsec)
VPN
Site-to-site VPN
Remote access VPN
questions.explanation: 5.5.a.2 · weight high. Site-to-site VPN: Always-on router-to-router (or firewall-to-firewall). Connects entire networks.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Remote access VPN. Single user needs access from anywhere."
options: Traveling employee scenario
Branch-to-HQ always-on scenario
Clientless SSL VPN
Site-to-site VPN
questions.explanation: 5.5.b.1 · weight high. Traveling employee scenario: Remote access VPN. Single user needs access from anywhere. AnyConnect client connects to HQ.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Site-to-site VPN. Branch office connects to HQ continuously."
options: IPsec Tunnel mode
Traveling employee scenario
Clientless SSL VPN
Branch-to-HQ always-on scenario
questions.explanation: 5.5.b.2 · weight high. Branch-to-HQ always-on scenario: Site-to-site VPN. Branch office connects to HQ continuously. Users don't know VPN exists.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Data CAN'T BE READ by eavesdroppers."
options: Confidentiality (IPsec)
Anti-replay (IPsec)
Authentication (IPsec)
Integrity (IPsec)
questions.explanation: 5.5.c.1 · weight high. Confidentiality (IPsec): Data CAN'T BE READ by eavesdroppers. Provided by encryption (AES, 3DES, DES).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Data HAS NOT BEEN MODIFIED in transit."
options: Confidentiality (IPsec)
Anti-replay (IPsec)
Integrity (IPsec)
Authentication (IPsec)
questions.explanation: 5.5.c.2 · weight high. Integrity (IPsec): Data HAS NOT BEEN MODIFIED in transit. Provided by HMAC hashing (MD5, SHA).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "IP protocol 51. Provides integrity + auth BUT NOT encryption."
options: AH + NAT incompatibility
AH = protocol number 51
AH (Authentication Header)
ESP works with NAT
questions.explanation: 5.5.d.1 · weight high. AH (Authentication Header): IP protocol 51. Provides integrity + auth BUT NOT encryption. Authenticates entire packet including IP header.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "AH authenticates the IP header."
options: Site-to-site = Tunnel mode
AH = protocol number 51
AH (Authentication Header)
AH + NAT incompatibility
questions.explanation: 5.5.d.2 · weight high. AH + NAT incompatibility: AH authenticates the IP header. NAT changes the IP header → AH breaks. Can't use AH through NAT.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "IP protocol 50. Provides encryption + integrity + auth."
options: ESP works with NAT
ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)
ESP vs AH rule
ESP = protocol 50
questions.explanation: 5.5.e.1 · weight high. ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload): IP protocol 50. Provides encryption + integrity + auth. Standard IPsec choice in modern VPNs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "ESP doesn't include IP header in authentication."
options: ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)
ESP works with NAT
ESP = protocol 50
ESP vs AH rule
questions.explanation: 5.5.e.3 · weight high. ESP works with NAT: ESP doesn't include IP header in authentication. NAT traversal (UDP 4500) makes it work across NAT.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Authenticate peers, build bidirectional management (ISAKMP) SA."
options: Phase order
IKE Phase 1
GRE over IPsec
IKE Phase 2
questions.explanation: 5.5.f.1 · weight high. IKE Phase 1: Authenticate peers, build bidirectional management (ISAKMP) SA. Happens first.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Negotiate IPsec SAs (one per direction) for data traffic."
options: IKE Phase 1
ESP works with NAT
IKE Phase 2
Phase order
questions.explanation: 5.5.f.2 · weight high. IKE Phase 2: Negotiate IPsec SAs (one per direction) for data traffic. Uses the Phase 1 tunnel to protect negotiation.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "ISAKMP SA. Used only for Phase 2 negotiation."
options: Branch-to-HQ always-on scenario
Quick Mode (Phase 2)
Phase 1 = 1 bidirectional SA
Phase 2 = 2 unidirectional SAs
questions.explanation: 5.5.g.1 · weight high. Phase 1 = 1 bidirectional SA: ISAKMP SA. Used only for Phase 2 negotiation. Not for user data.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "IPsec SAs. One for inbound, one for outbound."
options: Phase 2 = 2 unidirectional SAs
Phase 1 = 1 bidirectional SA
Quick Mode (Phase 2)
Anti-replay (IPsec)
questions.explanation: 5.5.g.2 · weight high. Phase 2 = 2 unidirectional SAs: IPsec SAs. One for inbound, one for outbound. Protect actual data traffic.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "TUNNEL mode. Transport mode leaves IP header visible."
options: Which hides original IP?
IPsec Transport mode
Branch-to-HQ always-on scenario
IPsec Tunnel mode
questions.explanation: 5.5.h.3 · weight high. Which hides original IP?: TUNNEL mode. Transport mode leaves IP header visible. Exam test point.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Gateway-to-gateway IPsec always uses Tunnel mode."
options: GRE
AH = protocol number 51
Site-to-site = Tunnel mode
Host-to-host = Transport mode
questions.explanation: 5.5.i.1 · weight high. Site-to-site = Tunnel mode: Gateway-to-gateway IPsec always uses Tunnel mode. Entire original packet hidden inside the tunnel.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "End-to-end IPsec (e.g., between two servers)."
options: Site-to-site = Tunnel mode
GRE
Site-to-site VPN
Host-to-host = Transport mode
questions.explanation: 5.5.i.2 · weight high. Host-to-host = Transport mode: End-to-end IPsec (e.g., between two servers). Transport mode protects just the payload.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Generic Routing Encapsulation."
options: IPsec can't carry multicast
DMVPN
GRE over IPsec
GRE
questions.explanation: 5.5.j.1 · weight high. GRE: Generic Routing Encapsulation. Cisco tunneling protocol. Carries multicast/broadcast and multiple L3 protocols.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Wrap traffic in GRE (adds multicast support) then encrypt with IPsec."
options: GRE over IPsec
IPsec can't carry multicast
GRE
DMVPN
questions.explanation: 5.5.j.3 · weight high. GRE over IPsec: Wrap traffic in GRE (adds multicast support) then encrypt with IPsec. Enables OSPF over VPN.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "As close to DESTINATION as possible."
options: Standard ACL syntax
Standard ACL placement
host keyword
any keyword
questions.explanation: 5.6.a.2 · weight high. Standard ACL placement: As close to DESTINATION as possible. Broad source matching means you'd block legitimate traffic if placed near source.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'access-list [1-99] {permit|deny} [source] [wildcard]'."
options: Standard ACL syntax
Standard ACL placement
host keyword
any keyword
questions.explanation: 5.6.a.3 · weight high. Standard ACL syntax: 'access-list [1-99] {permit|deny} [source] [wildcard]'. Or named: 'ip access-list standard [name]'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Matches source + destination + protocol + port."
options: Extended ACL syntax
Port operators
Extended ACL placement
Extended ACL
questions.explanation: 5.6.b.1 · weight high. Extended ACL: Matches source + destination + protocol + port. Numbered 100-199, 2000-2699. Granular filtering.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "As close to SOURCE as possible."
options: Port operators
Extended ACL syntax
Extended ACL placement
Extended ACL
questions.explanation: 5.6.b.2 · weight high. Extended ACL placement: As close to SOURCE as possible. Granular match = can block specific traffic without affecting others.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'access-list [100-199] {permit|deny} [proto] [src wildcard] [dst wildcard] [operator port]'."
options: Extended ACL placement
Port operators
Extended ACL syntax
Extended ACL
questions.explanation: 5.6.b.3 · weight high. Extended ACL syntax: 'access-list [100-199] {permit|deny} [proto] [src wildcard] [dst wildcard] [operator port]'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "eq (equals), gt, lt, neq, range."
options: Extended ACL
Extended ACL syntax
Port operators
Extended ACL placement
questions.explanation: 5.6.b.4 · weight high. Port operators: eq (equals), gt, lt, neq, range. Example: 'eq 80' matches HTTP. 'range 20 21' matches FTP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each ACE has a seq number (10, 20, 30...)."
options: Quick math: 255 − mask octet
Sequence numbers
Named ACL
resequence command
questions.explanation: 5.6.c.2 · weight high. Sequence numbers: Each ACE has a seq number (10, 20, 30...). Insert by specifying seq. Delete with 'no [seq#]'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "/24 mask 255.255.255.0 → 0.0.0.255."
options: Quick math: 255 − mask octet
Single host wildcard
Standard ACL
Wildcard mask

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'host 10.1.1.1' = '10.1.1.1 0.0.0.0'."
options: Standard ACL near destination
Both forms valid
any keyword
host keyword
questions.explanation: 5.6.e.1 · weight high. host keyword: 'host 10.1.1.1' = '10.1.1.1 0.0.0.0'. Exact single-host match.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'any' = '0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255'."
options: host keyword
any keyword
ip access-group [ACL] {in|out}
Both forms valid
questions.explanation: 5.6.e.2 · weight high. any keyword: 'any' = '0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255'. Matches all IPs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Exam uses both. 'permit host 10.1.1.1' and 'permit 10.1.1.1 0.0.0.0' are identical."
options: Both forms valid
host keyword
any keyword
Scenario question method
questions.explanation: 5.6.e.3 · weight high. Both forms valid: Exam uses both. 'permit host 10.1.1.1' and 'permit 10.1.1.1 0.0.0.0' are identical.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Every ACL has invisible 'deny any' at the end."
options: Missing permit any trap
ACL placement decision
Always end with explicit deny?
Implicit deny any
questions.explanation: 5.6.f.1 · weight high. Implicit deny any: Every ACL has invisible 'deny any' at the end. Traffic NOT explicitly permitted = BLOCKED.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Optional but best practice — makes implicit deny visible."
options: Implicit deny any
Always end with explicit deny?
Missing permit any trap
show ip access-lists
questions.explanation: 5.6.f.2 · weight high. Always end with explicit deny?: Optional but best practice — makes implicit deny visible. Also enables match-counter logging.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "ACL only permits specific traffic → everything else silently dropped."
options: Always end with explicit deny?
Implicit deny any
Missing permit any trap
ACL edit — delete entry
questions.explanation: 5.6.f.3 · weight high. Missing permit any trap: ACL only permits specific traffic → everything else silently dropped. Classic exam 'what's broken?' scenario.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Router evaluates ACEs in order (sequence number)."
options: Order matters
Top-down processing
show access-lists
Trace exam method
questions.explanation: 5.6.g.1 · weight high. Top-down processing: Router evaluates ACEs in order (sequence number). First match wins. Stops processing immediately.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Specific rules must come BEFORE general rules."
options: Standard ACL
Top-down processing
Order matters
Trace exam method
questions.explanation: 5.6.g.2 · weight high. Order matters: Specific rules must come BEFORE general rules. 'deny host X' above 'permit subnet' — reverse and host X would match 'permit' first.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Given packet + ACL, trace ACE-by-ACE until first match."
options: Top-down processing
Order matters
ip access-group (interface)
Trace exam method
questions.explanation: 5.6.g.3 · weight high. Trace exam method: Given packet + ACL, trace ACE-by-ACE until first match. Action at first match = the result.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Specific match (src+dst+proto+port)."
options: Standard ACL near destination
Extended ACL near source
Why placement matters
Missing permit any trap
questions.explanation: 5.6.h.2 · weight high. Extended ACL near source: Specific match (src+dst+proto+port). Place near source to save bandwidth by dropping early.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Wrong placement = traffic dropped unnecessarily OR consumes bandwidth before being dropped."
options: Standard ACL near destination
host keyword
Why placement matters
Extended ACL near source
questions.explanation: 5.6.h.3 · weight high. Why placement matters: Wrong placement = traffic dropped unnecessarily OR consumes bandwidth before being dropped.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Given topology diagram, determine where to apply ACL."
options: ACL placement decision
Trace exam method
host keyword
Scenario question method
questions.explanation: 5.6.i.1 · weight high. ACL placement decision: Given topology diagram, determine where to apply ACL. Standard → closest to dest. Extended → closest to source.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Traffic ENTERING the router through this interface."
options: ACL direction: in
ip access-group [ACL] {in|out}
Think from router's view
ACL direction: out
questions.explanation: 5.6.j.1 · weight high. ACL direction: in: Traffic ENTERING the router through this interface. Before routing decision.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Traffic LEAVING the router through this interface."
options: ACL direction: in
ip access-group [ACL] {in|out}
Think from router's view
ACL direction: out
questions.explanation: 5.6.j.2 · weight high. ACL direction: out: Traffic LEAVING the router through this interface. After routing decision.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Direction is relative to the ROUTER, not the client."
options: Think from router's view
ip access-group [ACL] {in|out}
ACL direction: in
ACL direction: out
questions.explanation: 5.6.j.4 · weight high. Think from router's view: Direction is relative to the ROUTER, not the client. In = into router. Out = out of router.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Apply ACL to VTY lines: 'access-class [ACL] in'."
options: access-class (VTY)
Don't confuse the two
host keyword
ip access-group (interface)
questions.explanation: 5.6.k.1 · weight high. access-class (VTY): Apply ACL to VTY lines: 'access-class [ACL] in'. Controls who can SSH/Telnet to the router.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Apply ACL to physical interface: 'ip access-group [ACL] {in|out}'."
options: Named ACL
ip access-group (interface)
access-class (VTY)
Don't confuse the two
questions.explanation: 5.6.k.2 · weight high. ip access-group (interface): Apply ACL to physical interface: 'ip access-group [ACL] {in|out}'. Filters transit traffic.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "access-class for VTY, access-group for interfaces."
options: Don't confuse the two
access-class (VTY)
Wildcard mask
ip access-group (interface)
questions.explanation: 5.6.k.3 · weight high. Don't confuse the two: access-class for VTY, access-group for interfaces. Classic exam trap.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Under 'ip access-list' sub-config: 'no [seq#]'."
options: Scenario question method
ACL edit — insert entry
ACL edit — delete entry
ip access-list resequence
questions.explanation: 5.6.l.1 · weight high. ACL edit — delete entry: Under 'ip access-list' sub-config: 'no [seq#]'. Removes a single ACE without affecting others.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Type '[seq#] permit/deny ...' under the named ACL sub-config."
options: ACL edit — insert entry
ip access-list resequence
Both forms valid
ACL edit — delete entry
questions.explanation: 5.6.l.2 · weight high. ACL edit — insert entry: Type '[seq#] permit/deny ...' under the named ACL sub-config. Inserts at that sequence number.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Displays all ACLs and their entries with match counts."
options: show ip access-lists
show access-lists
show ip interface [int]
ip access-group [ACL] {in|out}
questions.explanation: 5.6.m.1 · weight high. show access-lists: Displays all ACLs and their entries with match counts. Primary ACL verification command.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Shows which ACL is applied on interface and direction."
options: show access-lists
ACL direction: in
show ip interface [int]
show ip access-lists
questions.explanation: 5.6.m.2 · weight high. show ip interface [int]: Shows which ACL is applied on interface and direction. Useful for tracking application.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L2 security feature. Blocks rogue DHCP servers."
options: Trusted vs untrusted ports
DHCP snooping binding table
DHCP snooping
DHCP starvation attack
questions.explanation: 5.7.a.1 · weight high. DHCP snooping: L2 security feature. Blocks rogue DHCP servers. Only trusted ports forward DHCP offers.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Trusted = facing legit DHCP server."
options: Max MACs per port
Trusted vs untrusted ports
DHCP snooping binding table
DHCP snooping
questions.explanation: 5.7.a.2 · weight high. Trusted vs untrusted ports: Trusted = facing legit DHCP server. Untrusted = user ports, block DHCP server messages (offers).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Enables DHCP snooping globally on switch."
options: ip dhcp snooping trust
Option 82 gotcha
ip dhcp snooping vlan [#]
ip dhcp snooping (global)
questions.explanation: 5.7.b.1 · weight high. ip dhcp snooping (global): Enables DHCP snooping globally on switch. Step 1.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Interface command on trusted port (uplink toward DHCP server)."
options: ip dhcp snooping (global)
ip dhcp snooping vlan [#]
Option 82 gotcha
ip dhcp snooping trust
questions.explanation: 5.7.b.3 · weight high. ip dhcp snooping trust: Interface command on trusted port (uplink toward DHCP server). Default = untrusted.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DAI uses the DHCP snooping binding table to validate ARP replies."
options: maximum / mac-address
ip dhcp snooping trust
show ip dhcp snooping binding
Binding table = DAI source
questions.explanation: 5.7.c.1 · weight high. Binding table = DAI source: DAI uses the DHCP snooping binding table to validate ARP replies. Without snooping, DAI has no data.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "View the binding table: IP, MAC, port, VLAN, lease expiration."
options: Binding table = DAI source
show ip dhcp snooping binding
Trusted vs untrusted ports
Restrict mode
questions.explanation: 5.7.c.2 · weight high. show ip dhcp snooping binding: View the binding table: IP, MAC, port, VLAN, lease expiration.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Attacker requests many DHCP leases using spoofed MACs."
options: DHCP starvation mitigation
DHCP starvation attack
ip dhcp snooping limit rate
DHCP snooping
questions.explanation: 5.7.d.1 · weight high. DHCP starvation attack: Attacker requests many DHCP leases using spoofed MACs. Exhausts DHCP pool. Legit clients get denied.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DHCP snooping + port security (limit MACs) + rate limiting DHCP messages per port."
options: maximum / mac-address
DHCP starvation mitigation
ip dhcp snooping limit rate
DHCP starvation attack
questions.explanation: 5.7.d.2 · weight high. DHCP starvation mitigation: DHCP snooping + port security (limit MACs) + rate limiting DHCP messages per port.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L2 security. Validates ARP replies against DHCP snooping binding table."
options: ip arp inspection vlan [#]
DAI requires DHCP snooping
ip arp inspection trust
DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection)
questions.explanation: 5.7.e.1 · weight high. DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection): L2 security. Validates ARP replies against DHCP snooping binding table. Stops ARP spoofing.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DAI uses the binding table that DHCP snooping builds."
options: ip arp inspection trust
ip arp inspection vlan [#]
DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection)
DAI requires DHCP snooping
questions.explanation: 5.7.e.2 · weight high. DAI requires DHCP snooping: DAI uses the binding table that DHCP snooping builds. Enable snooping FIRST.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Interface command. Trusted ports (uplinks) bypass ARP inspection."
options: DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection)
ip arp inspection vlan [#]
ip arp inspection trust
DAI requires DHCP snooping
questions.explanation: 5.7.e.4 · weight high. ip arp inspection trust: Interface command. Trusted ports (uplinks) bypass ARP inspection. User ports = untrusted (default).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "DHCP snooping → populates binding table → DAI reads binding table → validates ARP."
options: Trust same ports on both
Full L2 security stack
DHCP starvation attack
Dependency chain
questions.explanation: 5.7.f.1 · weight high. Dependency chain: DHCP snooping → populates binding table → DAI reads binding table → validates ARP.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Port connecting to legit DHCP server + ARP source = trusted for both DHCP snooping AND DAI."
options: errdisable recovery cause
Dependency chain
Trust same ports on both
Full L2 security stack
questions.explanation: 5.7.f.2 · weight high. Trust same ports on both: Port connecting to legit DHCP server + ARP source = trusted for both DHCP snooping AND DAI.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "L2 feature. Limits which MAC addresses can connect on a port."
options: Purpose
Port security
Max MACs per port
ip dhcp snooping (global)
questions.explanation: 5.7.g.1 · weight high. Port security: L2 feature. Limits which MAC addresses can connect on a port. Prevents rogue device attachment.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Stops MAC flooding attacks + enforces 'one-device-per-port' policies."
options: Max MACs per port
Purpose
DHCP snooping
Port security
questions.explanation: 5.7.g.3 · weight high. Purpose: Stops MAC flooding attacks + enforces 'one-device-per-port' policies. First line of access-layer defense.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Interface command. Enables port security on the port."
options: Config order
switchport port-security
maximum / mac-address
Port-security prereq
questions.explanation: 5.7.h.2 · weight high. switchport port-security: Interface command. Enables port security on the port.

options: maximum / mac-address

Config order

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "(1) mode access, (2) port-security enable, (3) max, (4) mac-address or sticky, (5) violation mode."
options: Config order
Port-security prereq
maximum / mac-address
switchport port-security
questions.explanation: 5.7.h.4 · weight high. Config order: (1) mode access, (2) port-security enable, (3) max, (4) mac-address or sticky, (5) violation mode.

ip dhcp snooping (global)

Sticky reboot gotcha
questions.explanation: 5.7.i.1 · weight high. switchport port-security mac-address sticky: Auto-learn connected MACs and ADD them to running-config. Convenient — no manual MAC entry.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Sticky MACs are in RUNNING-config."
options: Sticky reboot gotcha
switchport port-security mac-address sticky
Static vs sticky
ip dhcp snooping trust
questions.explanation: 5.7.i.2 · weight high. Sticky reboot gotcha: Sticky MACs are in RUNNING-config. Must 'copy run start' or they're lost on reboot (re-learned after).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Static = manually typed in config."
options: Sticky reboot gotcha
switchport port-security mac-address sticky
Port security
Static vs sticky
questions.explanation: 5.7.i.3 · weight high. Static vs sticky: Static = manually typed in config. Sticky = auto-learned but saved as static. Both persistent.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Drops violating frames silently."
options: Protect mode
Restrict mode
Shutdown mode (default)
switchport port-security violation
questions.explanation: 5.7.j.1 · weight high. Protect mode: Drops violating frames silently. NO log, NO SNMP trap. Port stays up. Weakest enforcement.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Drops violating frames + LOGS syslog/SNMP trap + increments counter."
options: switchport port-security violation
Protect mode
Shutdown mode (default)
Restrict mode
questions.explanation: 5.7.j.2 · weight high. Restrict mode: Drops violating frames + LOGS syslog/SNMP trap + increments counter. Port stays up.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Port → err-disabled. Requires manual intervention."
options: Restrict mode
Shutdown mode (default)
switchport port-security violation
Protect mode
questions.explanation: 5.7.j.3 · weight high. Shutdown mode (default): Port → err-disabled. Requires manual intervention. Logs + increments violation count. DEFAULT.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Interface command. Takes 'protect', 'restrict', or 'shutdown'."
options: Shutdown mode (default)
Protect mode
Restrict mode
switchport port-security violation
questions.explanation: 5.7.j.4 · weight high. switchport port-security violation: Interface command. Takes 'protect', 'restrict', or 'shutdown'. Default is 'shutdown'.

errdisable recovery interval

Accounting
AAA order
Authorization

Privilege levels

Accounting purpose
Compliance value

802.1X Authenticator
802.1X Supplicant
EAP

EAP
802.1X Supplicant

options: 802.1X Supplicant
802.1X Authenticator
EAP
802.1X Authentication Server

WEP
WEP static keys
TKIP purpose

WPA (TKIP)

AES-CCMP
WPA2
WPA2 minimum standard

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Newest WPA standard. Adds SAE, OWE, forward secrecy."
options: SAE (Dragonfly)
WPA3
WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit
OWE

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Simultaneous Authentication of Equals."
options: SAE (Dragonfly)
OWE
WPA3
WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit
questions.explanation: 5.9.d.2 · weight high. SAE (Dragonfly): Simultaneous Authentication of Equals. Replaces PSK handshake. Forward secrecy; resists offline dictionary attacks.

SAE (Dragonfly)
OWE
WPA3

Personal mode
Enterprise mode

TKIP = wrong answer

WPA2-PSK config

Key automation benefits
Compliance provability
questions.explanation: 6.1.c.1 · weight high. Scalability benefit: Same effort to manage 1,000 devices as 10. One template applies to entire fleet.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Same template → identical configs across devices."
options: Compliance provability
Consistency via templates
Key automation benefits
Configuration drift
questions.explanation: 6.1.d.1 · weight high. Consistency via templates: Same template → identical configs across devices. Eliminates 'snowflake' devices with unique tweaks.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Devices gradually differ from intended config."
options: Configuration drift
Consistency via templates
Automation reduces human error
Compliance provability
questions.explanation: 6.1.d.2 · weight high. Configuration drift: Devices gradually differ from intended config. Automation detects drift and re-aligns to template.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Traditional: per-device CLI, distributed."
options: Contrast table
Traditional networking
SDN / controller-based
Southbound APIs
questions.explanation: 6.2.a.3 · weight high. Contrast table: Traditional: per-device CLI, distributed. SDN: centralized controller, APIs, single pane of glass.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Centralized brain. Hosts control plane logic."
options: Data plane (devices)
Southbound APIs
Traditional networking
SDN controller
questions.explanation: 6.2.b.1 · weight high. SDN controller: Centralized brain. Hosts control plane logic. Pushes forwarding info to devices via southbound APIs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Switches/routers in SDN only forward traffic."
options: Southbound APIs
Data plane (devices)
SDN controller
Single pane of glass
questions.explanation: 6.2.b.2 · weight high. Data plane (devices): Switches/routers in SDN only forward traffic. No local control decisions.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "One dashboard manages all devices."
options: Intent-Based Networking (IBN)
SDN controller
Single pane of glass
DPPA four pillars
questions.explanation: 6.2.c.1 · weight high. Single pane of glass: One dashboard manages all devices. Replaces SSH-ing to each device individually. SDN buzzword.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Describe DESIRED state (intent) → controller translates to device config."
options: Intent-Based Networking (IBN)
Single pane of glass
Data plane (devices)
DPPA four pillars
questions.explanation: 6.2.c.2 · weight high. Intent-Based Networking (IBN): Describe DESIRED state (intent) → controller translates to device config. Declarative, not procedural.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance."
options: REST APIs
DPPA four pillars
DNA Center / Catalyst Center
Traditional networking
questions.explanation: 6.2.d.2 · weight high. DPPA four pillars: Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance. Memorize the acronym.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Control = routing decisions (who to send where)."
options: Fabric
SDN separation
Control plane vs data plane
Management plane
questions.explanation: 6.3.a.1 · weight high. Control plane vs data plane: Control = routing decisions (who to send where). Data = actual packet forwarding. SDN separates them.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Traditional: each device has both."
options: Southbound APIs
Management plane
Control plane vs data plane
SDN separation
questions.explanation: 6.3.a.2 · weight high. SDN separation: Traditional: each device has both. SDN: control centralized on controller, data stays distributed on devices.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Apps are 'up top' (north), devices are 'below' (south)."
options: Control plane vs data plane
North vs South memory trick
Northbound APIs
Southbound APIs
questions.explanation: 6.3.b.3 · weight high. North vs South memory trick: Apps are 'up top' (north), devices are 'below' (south). Controller in the middle.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Physical network. Actual devices, links, routing protocols."
options: Underlay network
Management plane
Fabric
Overlay network
questions.explanation: 6.3.c.1 · weight high. Underlay network: Physical network. Actual devices, links, routing protocols. Foundation.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Virtual network built on top of underlay."
options: Fabric
Control plane vs data plane
Underlay network
Overlay network
questions.explanation: 6.3.c.2 · weight high. Overlay network: Virtual network built on top of underlay. VXLAN tunnels. Logically independent from physical paths.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "24-bit identifier. 16 million segments — vs VLAN's 12-bit / 4094."
options: VXLAN UDP 4789
VNI (VXLAN Network ID)
Northbound APIs
VXLAN
questions.explanation: 6.3.d.2 · weight high. VNI (VXLAN Network ID): 24-bit identifier. 16 million segments — vs VLAN's 12-bit / 4094. Massive scale.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "VXLAN encapsulates in UDP 4789."
options: North vs South memory trick
VNI (VXLAN Network ID)
VXLAN
VXLAN UDP 4789
questions.explanation: 6.3.d.3 · weight high. VXLAN UDP 4789: VXLAN encapsulates in UDP 4789. Travels over any L3 infrastructure.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Cisco campus fabric. Underlay = IS-IS."
options: LISP (in SDA)
SD-Access fabric
CTS / SGTs
Control plane vs data plane
questions.explanation: 6.3.e.1 · weight high. SD-Access fabric: Cisco campus fabric. Underlay = IS-IS. Overlay = VXLAN. Control plane = LISP. Policy = CTS/SGTs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "AI that CREATES output — configs, summaries, troubleshooting suggestions."
options: Generative AI in networking
Event correlation
Anomaly detection
Gen-AI use cases
questions.explanation: 6.4.a.1 · weight high. Generative AI in networking: AI that CREATES output — configs, summaries, troubleshooting suggestions. ChatGPT-style assistance for netops.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "AI that FORECASTS — detects anomalies, predicts failures, trends traffic."
options: Generative vs Predictive
Predictive AI
Anomaly detection
Generative AI in networking
questions.explanation: 6.4.b.1 · weight high. Predictive AI: AI that FORECASTS — detects anomalies, predicts failures, trends traffic. Proactive ops.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Learns normal patterns over time (daily/weekly/monthly)."
options: Gen-AI use cases
Cisco DNA Center Assurance
ML-based baselines
Predictive AI
questions.explanation: 6.4.c.1 · weight high. ML-based baselines: Learns normal patterns over time (daily/weekly/monthly). Adapts. Unlike static 'CPU > 80%' thresholds.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Connects seemingly unrelated events to find true cause."
options: AIOps
Automated root cause
Generative vs Predictive
Event correlation
questions.explanation: 6.4.d.2 · weight high. Event correlation: Connects seemingly unrelated events to find true cause. Dozens of alarms → one root problem.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "REpresentational State Transfer."
options: URI-based resources
REST
Client-server
Stateless
questions.explanation: 6.5.a.1 · weight high. REST: REpresentational State Transfer. Architecture style for APIs. Uses HTTP methods + URIs + JSON/XML.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each request contains all info needed."
options: REST
URI-based resources
Stateless
Client-server
questions.explanation: 6.5.a.2 · weight high. Stateless: Each request contains all info needed. Server doesn't store client session between requests.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Each resource has a unique URI."
options: URI-based resources
Client-server
REST
Stateless
questions.explanation: 6.5.a.4 · weight high. URI-based resources: Each resource has a unique URI. /api/v1/devices/42 represents device ID 42. Nouns, not verbs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Add a new resource. 'POST /devices' creates a device."
options: DELETE = Delete
POST = Create
PUT = Update (full)
GET = Read
questions.explanation: 6.5.b.1 · weight high. POST = Create: Add a new resource. 'POST /devices' creates a device. Server assigns ID.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Replace an existing resource entirely."
options: PATCH = Update (partial)
PUT = Update (full)
POST = Create
GET = Read
questions.explanation: 6.5.b.3 · weight high. PUT = Update (full): Replace an existing resource entirely. 'PUT /devices/42' overwrites device 42.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Remove resource. 'DELETE /devices/42' destroys the device entry."
options: GET = Read
POST = Create
DELETE = Delete
PATCH = Update (partial)
questions.explanation: 6.5.b.5 · weight high. DELETE = Delete: Remove resource. 'DELETE /devices/42' destroys the device entry.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Success. GET/PUT/DELETE returned what was expected."
options: 200 OK
400 Bad Request
401 Unauthorized
404 Not Found
questions.explanation: 6.5.c.1 · weight high. 200 OK: Success. GET/PUT/DELETE returned what was expected.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "POST succeeded. New resource was created."
options: 400 Bad Request
403 Forbidden
200 OK
201 Created
questions.explanation: 6.5.c.2 · weight high. 201 Created: POST succeeded. New resource was created. Response often includes location header with new URI.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Client sent malformed request."
options: 403 Forbidden
201 Created
400 Bad Request
4xx vs 5xx
questions.explanation: 6.5.c.3 · weight high. 400 Bad Request: Client sent malformed request. Syntax or validation error.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Missing or invalid credentials."
options: 403 Forbidden
500 Internal Server Error
404 Not Found
401 Unauthorized
questions.explanation: 6.5.c.4 · weight high. 401 Unauthorized: Missing or invalid credentials. Authentication required.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Authenticated but NOT authorized."
options: 500 Internal Server Error
400 Bad Request
401 Unauthorized
403 Forbidden
questions.explanation: 6.5.c.5 · weight high. 403 Forbidden: Authenticated but NOT authorized. Valid creds but not allowed to access this resource.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Resource doesn't exist at that URI."
options: 401 Unauthorized
4xx vs 5xx
200 OK
404 Not Found
questions.explanation: 6.5.c.6 · weight high. 404 Not Found: Resource doesn't exist at that URI.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Server-side failure. Not client's fault — server bug or crash."
options: 401 Unauthorized
500 Internal Server Error
404 Not Found
403 Forbidden
questions.explanation: 6.5.c.7 · weight high. 500 Internal Server Error: Server-side failure. Not client's fault — server bug or crash.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "4xx = CLIENT error (you sent bad request)."
options: 4xx vs 5xx
401 Unauthorized
403 Forbidden
201 Created
questions.explanation: 6.5.c.8 · weight high. 4xx vs 5xx: 4xx = CLIENT error (you sent bad request). 5xx = SERVER error (server failed to handle valid request).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Static secret string sent in header."
options: Bearer token
OAuth 2.0
API key
Basic Auth
questions.explanation: 6.5.d.1 · weight high. API key: Static secret string sent in header. Simplest auth. Fine for internal or low-sensitivity APIs.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Token obtained after login, sent in 'Authorization: Bearer [token]' header."
options: Bearer token
Basic Auth
API key
OAuth 2.0
questions.explanation: 6.5.d.2 · weight high. Bearer token: Token obtained after login, sent in 'Authorization: Bearer [token]' header. Session-based.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Delegated access. User grants limited permissions to third-party apps without sharing password."
options: OAuth 2.0
API key
Bearer token
Basic Auth
questions.explanation: 6.5.d.3 · weight high. OAuth 2.0: Delegated access. User grants limited permissions to third-party apps without sharing password.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "{ } JSON. XML. --- + indented lines = YAML."
options: Identify by sight
XML
JSON
YAML
questions.explanation: 6.5.e.4 · weight high. Identify by sight: { } JSON. < /> XML. --- + indented lines = YAML. Exam may show a snippet and ask format.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "HTTP verb + URI + headers + body."
options: Bearer token
PATCH = Update (partial)
API call format
CRUD ↔ HTTP mapping drill
questions.explanation: 6.5.f.1 · weight high. API call format: HTTP verb + URI + headers + body. Example: 'POST /api/devices' + JSON body = create a device.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Given 'PUT /devices/42' → Update."
options: API call format
500 Internal Server Error
CRUD ↔ HTTP mapping drill
PATCH = Update (partial)
questions.explanation: 6.5.f.2 · weight high. CRUD ↔ HTTP mapping drill: Given 'PUT /devices/42' → Update. Given 'Create a device' → POST /devices. Practice both directions.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "No software installed on managed devices."
options: Idempotent
Ansible vs Puppet/Chef
Ansible
Agentless
questions.explanation: 6.6.a.2 · weight high. Agentless: No software installed on managed devices. Uses SSH/WinRM. Network-friendly.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Running a playbook multiple times produces the same result."
options: Idempotent
Ansible
Agentless
Ansible vs Puppet/Chef
questions.explanation: 6.6.a.3 · weight high. Idempotent: Running a playbook multiple times produces the same result. Safe to re-run.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Ansible = agentless, push, YAML."
options: Ansible vs Puppet/Chef
Idempotent
Ansible
Agentless
questions.explanation: 6.6.a.4 · weight high. Ansible vs Puppet/Chef: Ansible = agentless, push, YAML. Puppet/Chef = agent-based, pull, Ruby DSL.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "YAML file defining a sequence of tasks to run against devices."
options: Playbook
Role
Inventory
Module
questions.explanation: 6.6.b.2 · weight high. Playbook: YAML file defining a sequence of tasks to run against devices. The 'what to do'.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Individual action (install package, copy file, run command)."
options: Playbook
Inventory
Module
Role
questions.explanation: 6.6.b.3 · weight high. Module: Individual action (install package, copy file, run command). Playbooks call modules.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Define infrastructure in code files."
options: IaC (Infrastructure as Code)
State file (Terraform)
Terraform
Declarative vs procedural
questions.explanation: 6.6.c.2 · weight high. IaC (Infrastructure as Code): Define infrastructure in code files. Version-controlled, repeatable, reviewable. Terraform is the canonical IaC tool.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Declarative (Terraform): describe end state."
options: Terraform
State file (Terraform)
Declarative vs procedural
IaC (Infrastructure as Code)
questions.explanation: 6.6.c.4 · weight high. Declarative vs procedural: Declarative (Terraform): describe end state. Procedural (Ansible): describe steps to reach state.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Preview changes. Shows what Terraform WOULD do."
options: terraform plan
terraform init
terraform destroy
terraform apply
questions.explanation: 6.6.d.2 · weight high. terraform plan: Preview changes. Shows what Terraform WOULD do. Safety net — always review before apply.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Execute the plan. Creates/modifies/destroys resources to match config."
options: terraform init
terraform plan
terraform destroy
terraform apply
questions.explanation: 6.6.d.3 · weight high. terraform apply: Execute the plan. Creates/modifies/destroys resources to match config.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Tear down all managed resources."
options: terraform init
terraform destroy
terraform apply
terraform plan
questions.explanation: 6.6.d.4 · weight high. terraform destroy: Tear down all managed resources. Useful for test environments. Irreversible.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Create VMs, VPCs, cloud infra → Terraform."
options: Complementary use
Tool selection scenario
Idempotent
Playbook
questions.explanation: 6.6.e.1 · weight high. Tool selection scenario: Create VMs, VPCs, cloud infra → Terraform. Configure existing routers/servers → Ansible.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Real workflow: Terraform creates VMs → Ansible configures them."
options: IaC (Infrastructure as Code)
Complementary use
Playbook
Tool selection scenario
questions.explanation: 6.6.e.2 · weight high. Complementary use: Real workflow: Terraform creates VMs → Ansible configures them. Each tool's strength.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Server pushes config to managed devices on demand."
options: Push model
Pull model
Module
Agent vs agentless
questions.explanation: 6.6.f.1 · weight high. Push model: Server pushes config to managed devices on demand. Ansible uses push. Immediate application.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Agent on device periodically pulls config from master."
options: Push model
Agent vs agentless
Ansible vs Puppet/Chef
Pull model
questions.explanation: 6.6.f.2 · weight high. Pull model: Agent on device periodically pulls config from master. Puppet/Chef use pull. Continuous reconciliation.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Agent = software running on device."
options: Agent vs agentless
Pull model
Idempotent
Push model
questions.explanation: 6.6.f.3 · weight high. Agent vs agentless: Agent = software running on device. Agentless = remote connection (SSH/WinRM). Ansible is agentless.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Key-value pairs. Keys in quotes."
options: JSON array
JSON structure
JSON object
{ } = object
questions.explanation: 6.7.a.1 · weight high. JSON structure: Key-value pairs. Keys in quotes. Values: string, number, boolean, null, object, array.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "{ } braces. Key-value pairs inside."
options: JSON array
JSON object
Access path
JSON structure
questions.explanation: 6.7.a.2 · weight high. JSON object: { } braces. Key-value pairs inside. Example: {"name": "R1", "type": "router"}.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "[ ] brackets. Ordered list of values."
options: JSON uses double quotes
JSON array
JSON object
JSON structure
questions.explanation: 6.7.a.3 · weight high. JSON array: [ ] brackets. Ordered list of values. Example: ["R1", "R2", "R3"].

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Curly braces. Collection of unordered key-value pairs."
options: Visual identification
[ ] = array
No trailing commas
{ } = object
questions.explanation: 6.7.b.1 · weight high. { } = object: Curly braces. Collection of unordered key-value pairs. Access by key.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Square brackets. Ordered list."
options: JSON structure
{ } = object
Visual identification
[ ] = array
questions.explanation: 6.7.b.2 · weight high. [ ] = array: Square brackets. Ordered list. Access by numeric index (0-based).

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "string, number, boolean, null, object, array."
options: { } = object
6 valid JSON types
JSON booleans lowercase
No date/undefined in JSON
questions.explanation: 6.7.c.1 · weight high. 6 valid JSON types: string, number, boolean, null, object, array. Memorize all six.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "JSON has no 'date' type (use string)."
options: 6 valid JSON types
Tracing strategy
JSON booleans lowercase
No date/undefined in JSON
questions.explanation: 6.7.c.3 · weight high. No date/undefined in JSON: JSON has no 'date' type (use string). No 'undefined' (use null). Common exam traps.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Objects inside objects, arrays inside objects, etc."
options: Access path
Nested JSON
Tracing strategy
{ } = object
questions.explanation: 6.7.d.1 · weight high. Nested JSON: Objects inside objects, arrays inside objects, etc. Exam shows nested structure and asks for a value.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Read outer keys first → drill down."
options: Nested JSON
Exam JSON validation
Access path
Tracing strategy
questions.explanation: 6.7.d.3 · weight high. Tracing strategy: Read outer keys first → drill down. Object key → array index [N] → nested key → value.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Strings + keys MUST use double quotes " "."
options: No comments
JSON uses double quotes
Exam JSON validation
No trailing commas
questions.explanation: 6.7.e.1 · weight high. JSON uses double quotes: Strings + keys MUST use double quotes " ". Single quotes ' ' are INVALID JSON.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "JSON does NOT support // or /* */ comments."
options: JSON uses double quotes
No trailing commas
Exam JSON validation
No comments
questions.explanation: 6.7.e.3 · weight high. No comments: JSON does NOT support // or /* */ comments. Any comment = invalid JSON.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "'text' INVALID. True/FALSE INVALID."
options: JSON uses double quotes
No comments
No trailing commas
No single quotes / capitalized booleans
questions.explanation: 6.7.e.4 · weight high. No single quotes / capitalized booleans: 'text' INVALID. True/FALSE INVALID. Must be: "text", true, false.

questions.question: Which term is best described by: "Scan for: double quotes only, no trailing commas, no comments, lowercase booleans."
options: Exam JSON validation
No comments
JSON uses double quotes
No single quotes / capitalized booleans
questions.explanation: 6.7.e.5 · weight high. Exam JSON validation: Scan for: double quotes only, no trailing commas, no comments, lowercase booleans. Any violation = invalid.
